topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Production of Low Temperatures, Refrigerants - Classification, Nomenclature and Properties (Physical, Chemical, Safety, Thermodynamic and Economical)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Production of Low Temperatures — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body (refrigerated space / food product) and transferring it to a high-temperature body (surroundings / atmosphere) by consuming external work (mechanical, thermal, or electrical), in order to maintain the body at a temperature *below* that of its surroundings.

**Production of low temperatures** is the engineering science of *creating and sustaining* temperatures below the ambient. In food industry applications, this typically spans **−40 °C to +10 °C** (chillers, freezers, cold stores) and, for cryogenic flash-freezing, down to **−196 °C** (liquid nitrogen boiling point).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clausius Statement (Second Law of Thermodynamics):** It is *impossible* to transfer heat from a body at lower temperature to a body at higher temperature without the input of external work. Refrigeration systems are therefore **work-consuming devices**, and the efficiency of this work transfer is captured by the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**.

### 1.2 Refrigerant — Formal Definition

A **refrigerant** is a working fluid that undergoes a thermodynamic cycle (evaporation → compression → condensation → expansion) to absorb heat at a low temperature and reject it at a higher temperature. Without a refrigerant, a vapour-compression cycle simply cannot function.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sweat That Cools"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a refrigerator like a person sweating in summer.
> - The *blood* (refrigerant) absorbs heat from the *body core* (refrigerated space) and carries it to the *skin* (condenser), where evaporation of *sweat* (heat rejection) releases heat into the surrounding air.
> - The *heart's pumping action* (compressor) is the external work input.
> - **No sweat = No cooling. No compressor = No refrigerant cycle.**
> - If the refrigerant is *toxic*, sweating in a closed room is dangerous — exactly like a CFC leak in a kitchen!

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Conventions (KTU Board Standard)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration Effect (1 Ton) | **TR** | **3.516 kW** or **210 kJ/min** or **50.4 kcal/min** |
| Standard Evaporator Temperature (food chilling) | T_evap | −10 °C to −35 °C |
| Standard Condenser Temperature | T_cond | 35 °C to 45 °C |
| Ambient Design Temperature | T_amb | 35 °C (KTU convention) |
| Atmospheric Pressure | P_atm | **101.325 kPa** |
| Absolute Zero | T₀ | **−273.15 °C** = **0 K** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always use temperatures in **°C** for saturated tables and **K** (Kelvin) when computing COP ratios or efficiencies. Mixing the two is a guaranteed loss of marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Methods of Production of Low Temperatures

The methods are broadly classified into **Non-Cyclic (one-shot)** and **Cyclic (continuous)** processes. KTU examiners frequently ask for a comparison or specific method diagrams.

#### A. Non-Cyclic (One-shot) Methods

1. **Melting of Ice / Use of Ice-Salt Mixtures**
   - Latent heat of fusion of ice = **334 kJ/kg**.
   - Adding salt (NaCl) *depresses* the freezing point — eutectic point at −21.2 °C (23.3% salt).
   - **Limitation:** Single-use; temperature limited; weight loss.
2. **Sublimation of Solid CO₂ (Dry Ice)**
   - Sublimation temperature at 1 atm = **−78.5 °C**.
   - Heat absorbed = **571 kJ/kg** (latent heat of sublimation).
   - **Use:** Direct contact quick-freezing of fish, meat.
3. **Evaporation of Volatile Liquids**
   - Liquids like **NH₃** (boiling point −33.3 °C), **liquid nitrogen** (BP −195.8 °C).
   - **Limitation:** Extremely high cost; one-shot use.

#### B. Cyclic (Continuous) Methods

1. **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** — *Most common in food industry*.
2. **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** — *Uses heat as input, ideal for waste-heat recovery in food plants*.
3. **Air Refrigeration Cycle** — *Used in aircraft food trolleys*.
4. **Cascade Refrigeration System** — *For very low temperatures (−80 °C to −150 °C)* such as freeze-drying.
5. **Vortex Tube (Ranque-Hilsch)** — *For spot cooling in packaging lines*.
6. **Thermoelectric (Peltier) Effect** — *Used in portable food carriers, wine chillers*.
7. **Magnetic Refrigeration (Adiabatic Demagnetisation)** — *Emerging eco-friendly technology*.
8. **Joule-Thomson Expansion (Throttling)** — *Used in cryogenic food freezing with CO₂ or N₂*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot-Trend:** Vapour Absorption Systems are highly emphasized because food processing plants generate abundant low-grade waste heat from boilers and CHP systems — making VARS economically attractive and energy-efficient.

### 2.2 Refrigerants — Classification

#### A. Based on Chemical Origin

| Category | Examples | KTU Remark |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Refrigerants** | NH₃ (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), Water (R-718), Propane (R-290), Isobutane (R-600a), Air (R-729) | Zero ODP, Eco-friendly |
| **Synthetic Refrigerants (Halocarbons)** | CFCs (R-12), HCFCs (R-22), HFCs (R-134a, R-404A, R-410A) | Industry workhorses, phased-out |
| **Inorganic Refrigerants** | NH₃, CO₂, Air, Water | Predate halocarbons |
| **Organic Refrigerants** | Hydrocarbons, Halocarbons | Engineered molecules |

#### B. Based on Application (Food Industry Specific)

| Type | Role | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Refrigerant** | Undergoes cycle (evaporates and condenses) | R-134a, R-404A, R-717 |
| **Secondary Refrigerant** | Carries heat from food to primary refrigerant (brine, glycol) | Brine (CaCl₂), Ethylene Glycol, Propylene Glycol |

#### C. ASHRAE Classification by Boiling Point at 1 atm

| Class | Boiling Point Range (°C) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Temperature Refrigerants** | > 0 °C | AC, chilled water |
| **Medium-Temperature Refrigerants** | −40 °C to 0 °C | Cold storage, milk chilling |
| **Low-Temperature Refrigerants** | < −40 °C | Frozen food, freeze-drying |

### 2.3 Nomenclature of Refrigerants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASHRAE Standard 34 Nomenclature Rules — KTU Favourite (3-mark question)**

The standard "R" code decodes a refrigerant's chemistry as follows:

For **Halogenated (synthetic)** refrigerants of the form **C**a**H**b**Cl**c**F**d (where a = C atoms, b = H atoms, c = Cl atoms, d = F atoms, and a = b + c + d, i.e., fully saturated):

$$\text{R Number} = 1000 \cdot \left[a + 1\right] + 100 \cdot \left[b + 1\right] + 10 \cdot c + d \quad \text{(for saturated alkanes only)}$$

| Code | Decode → Formula | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| R-12 | C(1+1) H(0+1) Cl(2) F(0) → CCl₂F₂ | Dichlorodifluoromethane (CFC) |
| R-22 | C(1+1) H(1+1) Cl(1) F(0) → CHClF₂ | Chlorodifluoromethane (HCFC) |
| R-134a | Contains C₂ → formula CH₂FCF₃ | Tetrafluoroethane (HFC) |
| R-502 | Zeotrope of R-22/R-115 | Low-temp commercial |

**Suffix codes:**
- **No suffix:** Pure compound (azeotrope), e.g., R-500.
- **Suffix 'a'** (e.g., R-134**a**): Isomer — different molecular arrangement, different properties.
- **Suffix 'B', 'C'**: Other isomers.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Decoder Examples (KTU Short-Answer Hot Favourite):**
> - R-12 → **CCl₂F₂** (CFC, ODP = 1.0, banned)
> - R-22 → **CHClF₂** (HCFC, ODP = 0.055, phasing out)
> - R-134a → **CH₂F-CF₃** (HFC, ODP = 0, GWP = 1430)
> - R-717 → **NH₃** (Ammonia, ODP = 0, GWP = 0, slightly toxic)
> - R-744 → **CO₂** (transcritical in modern systems)
> - R-404A → Zeotropic blend of R-125/R-143a/R-134a

### 2.4 Desired Properties of Refrigerants — Master Classification

This is the **most high-yield topic** for KTU Module 1.

| Property | Ideal Requirement | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Physical** | | |
| Low boiling point (at atm) | Below evaporator T | Ensures evaporation at desired low T |
| Low freezing point | Below evaporator T | No solidification in evaporator |
| Low specific heat (vapour) | Low cp | Lower compressor work |
| High latent heat of vaporization | High h_fg | Higher refrigerant effect per kg |
| Low viscosity | Low μ | Lower friction losses, better heat transfer |
| High thermal conductivity (liquid) | High k_l | Better heat transfer in evaporator |
| High density (liquid) | High ρ_l | Smaller pipe sizes, compact equipment |
| Small specific volume (vapour) | Low v_g | Smaller compressor displacement |
| **B. Chemical** | | |
| Chemically stable | Inert | Long life, no breakdown |
| Non-corrosive | Inert to metals | No damage to components |
| Non-flammable | — | Safety |
| Non-toxic | — | Food safety (no contamination) |
| **C. Safety / Environmental** | | |
| Zero ODP | 0 | No ozone layer damage |
| Low GWP | < 150 (Kyoto Protocol) | Low global warming impact |
| Non-flammable | — | Safe operation |
| Low leakage tendency | — | No losses to atmosphere |
| **D. Thermodynamic** | | |
| Low pressure ratio (P_cond/P_evap) | Low | Better volumetric efficiency |
| High COP | High | Energy-efficient |
| Low discharge temperature | < 150 °C | Safer compression |
| **E. Economical** | | |
| Low cost | Cheap | Affordable for SME food plants |
| Easy availability | Wide supply | Procurement ease |
| Easy to detect leaks | Visible / detectable | Maintenance |

### 2.5 Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) and Global Warming Potential (GWP) — KTU Buzzwords

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ODP** = (Mass of ozone destroyed by 1 kg of refrigerant) / (Mass destroyed by 1 kg of R-11). **R-11 is the reference, ODP = 1.0**.
> **GWP** = (Integrated radiative forcing over 100 years from 1 kg of gas) / (Same from 1 kg of CO₂). **CO₂ is reference, GWP = 1.0**.

| Refrigerant | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-12 (CFC) | 0.82–1.0 | 10,900 | **Banned** (Montreal Protocol 1987) |
| R-22 (HCFC) | 0.055 | 1,810 | Phasing out (developed nations = 0% by 2020) |
| R-134a (HFC) | 0 | 1,430 | Being phased down (Kigali Amendment) |
| R-404A | 0 | 3,922 | High-GWP, being replaced |
| R-717 (NH₃) | 0 | 0 | Excellent, but toxic |
| R-744 (CO₂) | 0 | 1 | Eco-friendly, high pressure |
| R-290 (Propane) | 0 | 3 | Mildly flammable, very efficient |
| R-600a (Isobutane) | 0 | 3 | Domestic refrigerators |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Real refrigerator |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (all in K) | Theoretical max |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = 1 + \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}}$ | Heat pump relation |
| 4 | $1 \text{ TR} = 210 \text{ kJ/min} = 3.516 \text{ kW}$ | Capacity conversion |
| 5 | $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ (dry comp.) | Refrigerating effect per kg |
| 6 | $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ | Compressor work per kg |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE}$ | Mass flow rate |
| 8 | $V_s = \dot{m} \cdot v_1$ | Compressor swept volume |
| 9 | $P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{TR \cdot 3.516}{\text{COP}}$ | Power input |
| 10 | $\text{Performance Factor} = \dfrac{\text{RE}}{\text{Displacement Volume}} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{evap}}}{P_{\text{cond}}}$ | Comparing refrigerants |
| 11 | $\eta_{vol} \approx 1 - C \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]$ | Volumetric efficiency |
| 12 | $\text{Discharge T: } T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Polytropic compression |
| 13 | $\Delta h_{\text{cycle}} = 0$ (cyclic, steady-state) | First Law for cycle |
| 14 | $\text{GWP} = \dfrac{\text{Radiative forcing of gas}}{\text{Radiative forcing of CO}_2}$ | Environmental index |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Rule:** Use **Kelvin** in the COP_Carnot equation. Writing "TL/(TH−TL)" with °C will give a wrong answer. KTU Examiners explicitly deduct **1 mark** for unit inconsistency in COP problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Coefficient of Performance (Carnot COP)

**Why we need this:** Establishes the *upper bound* of performance; all real refrigerants are compared against this benchmark.

#### Derivation from First Law

Let the cycle operate between:
- **T_L** = absolute temperature of refrigerated space (K)
- **T_H** = absolute temperature of surroundings (K)

For a reversible (Carnot) cycle:

$$Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S \quad \text{(heat absorbed from cold body during isothermal expansion)}$$

$$Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S \quad \text{(heat rejected to hot body during isothermal compression)}$$

Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the cyclic device:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

The Coefficient of Performance is defined as:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

Substituting the expressions for heat:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{T_H \cdot \Delta S - T_L \cdot \Delta S}$$

The $\Delta S$ term cancels (this is the *key step* — it shows that COP is *independent* of the working fluid, depending only on temperature limits):

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,Ref}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

#### Heat Pump COP (Dual Relation)

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

Adding the two:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = 1 + \text{COP}_{Ref}$$

This proves that for the *same* cycle, the heat-pump mode always delivers more useful energy than refrigeration mode.

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical Sanity Check (KTU Style):** At T_L = 263 K (−10 °C) and T_H = 308 K (35 °C): COP = 263 / (308 − 263) = 263/45 = **5.84**. This means a perfect Carnot fridge delivers 5.84 units of cooling per unit of work — a real VCRS typically delivers 3.0 to 3.5, indicating significant irreversibilities.

### 3.2 Comparative Performance Analysis — Worked Example

**Problem:** A refrigeration system operates between −10 °C and 30 °C with refrigerant R-12.
- Saturated values at evaporator: h_1 = 183.19 kJ/kg, v_1 = 0.0766 m³/kg
- Saturated values at condenser: h_2 = 209.84 kJ/kg, h_3 = 67.55 kJ/kg (after throttling)
- Compressor exit: h_2 = h_1 + W_s = 183.19 + (209.84 − 183.19) →

**Let us re-derive step by step for an ideal cycle:**

For **dry (isentropic) compression**:

$$h_2 = \text{function of } (P_{cond}, s_1)$$

Given:
- T_evap = −10 °C → P_evap ≈ 0.2191 MPa
- T_cond = 30 °C → P_cond ≈ 0.7449 MPa

Reading the superheated R-12 table at P_cond = 0.7449 MPa and s_2 = s_1:
- h_2 = 209.84 kJ/kg (assume)

#### Step-by-step calculation of COP

**Step 1: Refrigerating Effect (RE)**

$$RE = h_1 - h_4$$

In a standard cycle, throttling is isenthalpic:

$$h_4 = h_3 = h_{f \text{ at } T_{evap}} = 67.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$RE = 183.19 - 67.55 = 115.64 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2: Compressor Work**

$$W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1 = 209.84 - 183.19 = 26.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: COP**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \frac{115.64}{26.65} = 4.34$$

**Step 4: Compare with Carnot**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} = 6.575$$

**Step 5: Relative COP (Second-Law Efficiency)**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{4.34}{6.575} = 0.66 = 66\%$$

**Step 6: Mass flow rate for 1 TR**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{3.516}{115.64 / 1000} = 0.0304 \text{ kg/s} = 109.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 7: Compressor swept volume per second**

$$V_s = \dot{m} \cdot v_1 = 0.0304 \times 0.0766 = 2.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Performance Matrix — Common Refrigerants (KTU Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is built from R-12 (CFC) as the *baseline* and compares refrigerants under identical operating conditions (T_evap = −10 °C, T_cond = 40 °C).

| Refrigerant | Type | T_evap (°C) at 1 atm | P_cond / P_evap ratio | Discharge T (°C) | COP vs R-12 | ODP | GWP | Toxicity | Flammability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R-12** | CFC | −29.8 | 4.13 | ~80 | 1.00 (Base) | 0.82 | 10900 | Low | None |
| **R-22** | HCFC | −40.8 | 4.10 | ~88 | 1.04 | 0.055 | 1810 | Low | None |
| **R-134a** | HFC | −26.1 | 4.45 | ~55 | 0.97 | 0 | 1430 | Very Low | None |
| **R-717 (NH₃)** | Natural | −33.3 | 4.36 | ~110 | 1.20 | 0 | 0 | **High** | Mild |
| **R-744 (CO₂)** | Natural | −78.4 (sub) | 3.0–5.0 | ~80–100 | 1.05 (trans) | 0 | 1 | Low | None |
| **R-290 (Propane)** | HC | −42.1 | 4.30 | ~70 | 1.10 | 0 | 3 | Low | **High** |
| **R-404A** | Blend | −46.5 | 4.50 | ~65 | 1.00 | 0 | 3922 | Low | None |
| **R-502** | CFC Blend | −45.6 | 3.60 | ~50 | 1.10 | 0.33 | 4657 | Low | None |
| **R-123** | HCFC | 27.9 | Low | — | — | 0.012 | 79 | Low | None |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Selection Algorithm — Which Refrigerant for Which Food Application?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Decision Tree (Frequently Asked):**

$$
\text{Food Application?}
\begin{cases}
\text{Chillers (0 °C to 5 °C)} \rightarrow \text{R-134a, R-22, R-717}\\
\text{Cold Storage (−10 °C to −20 °C)} \rightarrow \text{R-22, R-404A, NH₃}\\
\text{Frozen Food (−25 °C to −40 °C)} \rightarrow \text{R-404A, R-507, NH₃ cascade}\\
\text{Ice Cream / Freeze-Drying (< −40 °C)} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 \text{ cascade, CO}_2 \text{ cascade}\\
\text{Cryogenic (LN}_2, \text{LCO}_2) \rightarrow \text{R-744, N}_2\\
\text{Plant-wide system} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 \text{ (with waste heat)} \rightarrow \text{VARS}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Compressor Power and Ton of Refrigeration

For a **simple Vapour Compression Cycle (VCC)**, the **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** is defined as the rate of heat removal equivalent to melting **1 ton (907.185 kg)** of ice at 0 °C in 24 hours.

$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{907.185 \text{ kg} \times 334 \text{ kJ/kg}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}}$$

$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{302{,}999 \text{ kJ}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} = 3.516 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 3.516 \text{ kW} = 210 \text{ kJ/min} = 50.4 \text{ kcal/min}}$$

For a system producing N TR with mass flow $\dot{m}$ and refrigerating effect RE:

$$\text{TR} = \frac{\dot{m} \times RE}{3.516}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\text{TR} \times 3.516}{RE} \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

**Shaft power input:**

$$P_{\text{shaft}} = \dot{m} \times W_{comp} = \frac{\text{TR} \times 3.516}{\text{COP}} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**3.5 kW per TR, COP ≈ 3, so 1 TR ≈ 1.2 kW input.**" This rule-of-thumb checks your numerical answers in seconds.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Methods of Producing Low Temperatures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PRODUCTION OF LOW TEMPERATURES"]
    A --> B["NON-CYCLIC METHODS"]
    A --> C["CYCLIC METHODS"]

    B --> B1["Ice-Melting and Ice-Salt Mixture"]
    B --> B2["Sublimation of Dry Ice CO2"]
    B --> B3["Evaporation of Volatile Liquids"]

    C --> C1["Vapour Compression System VCRS"]
    C --> C2["Vapour Absorption System VARS"]
    C --> C3["Air Refrigeration Cycle"]
    C --> C4["Cascade Refrigeration"]
    C --> C5["Vortex Tube Ranque-Hilsch"]
    C --> C6["Thermoelectric Peltier Effect"]
    C --> C7["Magnetic Refrigeration"]
    C --> C8["Joule Thomson Throttling"]

    C1 --> C1a["Most used in food industry"]
    C2 --> C2a["Heat input ideal for food plants"]
    C4 --> C4a["Used below minus 40 degree C"]
    C8 --> C8a["Cryogenic food freezing LN2 LCO2"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Refrigerants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["REFRIGERANTS"]
    R --> R1["BY CHEMICAL ORIGIN"]
    R --> R2["BY APPLICATION"]
    R --> R3["BY BOILING POINT"]

    R1 --> R1A["Natural Inorganic"]
    R1 --> R1B["Natural Organic HC"]
    R1 --> R1C["Synthetic Halocarbons"]

    R1A --> R1A1["NH3 R717, CO2 R744, Water R718, Air R729"]
    R1B --> R1B1["Propane R290, Isobutane R600a, Ethylene R1150"]
    R1C --> R1C1["CFCs R12, HCFCs R22, HFCs R134a, R404A, HFO R1234yf"]

    R2 --> R2A["Primary"]
    R2 --> R2B["Secondary Brines and Glycol"]

    R3 --> R3A["High Temp above 0 C"]
    R3 --> R3B["Medium Temp minus 40 to 0 C"]
    R3 --> R3C["Low Temp below minus 40 C"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Vapour Compression Cycle (The Reference Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EVAP["EVAPORATOR\nLow Pressure\nLow Temperature\nRefrigerant Absorbs Heat from Food Space"] --> COMP["COMPRESSOR\nVapour Compressed\nto High Pressure\nWork Input W"]
    COMP --> COND["CONDENSER\nHigh Pressure\nHigh Temperature\nHeat QH Rejected to Surroundings"]
    COND --> EXV["EXPANSION VALVE\nThrottling Process\nIsenthalpic Expansion\nPressure Drop"]
    EXV --> EVAP

    QL["Heat QL from Cold Food Space"] -.-> EVAP
    QH["Heat QH to Ambient Air"] -.-> COND
    WIN["External Work W in"] -.-> COMP
```

### 4.4 Decision Architecture — Refrigerant Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Need a Refrigerant"]
    Q --> Q1{"Application\nTemperature"}
    Q1 -->|"Above 0 C"| Q1A["R134a or R123"]
    Q1 -->|"0 to minus 25 C"| Q1B["R22 R404A NH3"]
    Q1 -->|"Below minus 40 C"| Q1C["Cascade R508B or CO2 NH3"]

    Q --> Q2{"Environmental\nPriority"}
    Q2 -->|"Eco Critical"| Q2A["NH3 CO2 Propane Isobutane"]
    Q2 -->|"Standard HFC"| Q2B["R134a R404A R407C"]

    Q --> Q3{"Plant Scale"}
    Q3 -->|"Small Shop Chiller"| Q3A["R134a R404A"]
    Q3 -->|"Large Cold Storage"| Q3B["NH3 Industrial"]
    Q3 -->|"Dairy Food Plant"| Q3C["NH3 Absorption VARS"]

    Q --> Q4{"Safety"}
    Q4 -->|"Public Space"| Q4A["Low Toxicity R134a"]
    Q4 -->|"Industrial Only"| Q4B["NH3 with leak sensors"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Hint:** In answer sheets, even a simple hand-drawn block diagram showing the 4-component cycle (Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator) with arrows labelled Q_L, Q_H, and W carries **2–3 easy marks** in long-answer questions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *(Internal marks allowed)*

#### **Q1. Define 'Refrigerant' and list FIVE ideal properties of a refrigerant used in a food cold-storage plant.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A refrigerant is a working fluid that absorbs heat at low temperature by evaporating and rejects heat at high temperature by condensing, in a continuous thermodynamic cycle (VCRS or VARS).

Five ideal properties:
1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (below −10 °C for cold storage)
2. **High latent heat of vaporization** (for higher RE per kg)
3. **Non-toxic and non-flammable** (food safety + operator safety)
4. **Chemically stable and non-corrosive** (compatibility with copper, steel)
5. **Zero ODP and low GWP** (Montreal Protocol compliance — eco-friendly)

---

#### **Q2. What is the significance of the suffixes 'a' and 'B' in refrigerant numbering? Give one example each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- The suffix **'a'** (e.g., R-134**a**) denotes an *isomer* of the same chemical formula — a different arrangement of atoms giving different thermodynamic and safety properties. R-134a is the isomer of CH₂FCF₃ with the boiling point suited to refrigeration.
- The suffix **'B'** (e.g., R-134**B**, in older nomenclature) denotes *another isomer* of the same parent compound, with a different boiling point.
- (Optional note for KTU: R-134a is preferred over R-134 because of its favourable saturation curve.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Classify refrigerants used in the food industry. Explain in detail the ASHRAE nomenclature used for naming halogenated refrigerants, with at least **four worked decode examples**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **physical, chemical, safety, thermodynamic, and economical properties** an ideal refrigerant must possess for use in a frozen-food cold store. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a)**

**Classification of Refrigerants:**

1. **Primary Refrigerants** — undergo the actual phase-change cycle (e.g., NH₃, R-134a, R-404A, CO₂).
2. **Secondary Refrigerants** — act as heat carriers (e.g., brine, ethylene glycol, propylene glycol).

**By Chemical Origin:**
- **Inorganic natural** — NH₃ (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), Water (R-718), Air (R-729)
- **Organic natural (Hydrocarbons)** — Propane (R-290), Isobutane (R-600a), Ethylene (R-1150)
- **Synthetic Halocarbons** — CFCs, HCFCs, HFCs, HFOs

**ASHRAE Nomenclature — Halogenated Refrigerants:**

For a saturated halocarbon of formula **C_a H_b Cl_c F_d** (with a = b + c + d), the ASHRAE number is decoded as:

- First digit on the left (plus 1) = number of C atoms (a) → for halocarbons with 2 or more C, a special "−1" rule applies.
- Second digit + 1 = number of H atoms
- Third digit = number of Cl atoms
- Fourth digit = number of F atoms

**Worked Decode Examples:**

1. **R-12** → Digits 1, 2, 0, 0 → C = (1+1)/… → for single-carbon: a = 1, b = (2−1) = 1, but formula shows no H…  
   **Correct decode:** R-12 = **CCl₂F₂** (Dichlorodifluoromethane, CFC).
2. **R-22** → 0, 2, 2, 0 → C = 0+1 = 1; H = 2−1 = 1; Cl = 2; F = 0 → **CHClF₂**.
3. **R-134a** → Starts with 1 → indicates 2-carbon compound. Digits 3, 4 → H atoms = 3−1 = 2; Cl = 4; F = (formula) → **CH₂FCF₃**.
4. **R-502** → Blend of R-22 and R-115; written as a blend identifier.

*(Note: Suffix 'a' = preferred isomer. R-12 = CFC, R-22 = HCFC, R-134a = HFC, R-404A = zeotropic blend.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b)**

**Properties an Ideal Refrigerant Should Possess:**

| Category | Property | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Low boiling point (< −26 °C) | Ensures evaporation at sub-zero |
| | High latent heat (h_fg) | Reduces mass flow rate |
| | Low specific heat of vapour | Lower compressor work |
| | Low viscosity | Reduces friction, improves heat transfer |
| | High density of liquid | Smaller piping |
| **Chemical** | Stable (no dissociation at T_high) | Long service life |
| | Non-corrosive to metals | Protects compressor, evaporator |
| | Non-reactive with oil | Lubricant compatibility |
| **Safety** | Non-toxic | Food safety; no operator harm |
| | Non-flammable, non-explosive | Plant safety |
| | Zero ODP | Montreal Protocol compliance |
| | Low GWP (< 150) | Kigali Amendment |
| **Thermodynamic** | Low pressure ratio (P_c/P_e) | Better volumetric efficiency |
| | High COP | Lower operating cost |
| | Low discharge temperature (< 150 °C) | Safer, no lubricant breakdown |
| **Economical** | Low cost | Affordable CAPEX |
| | Easy availability | Supply chain security |
| | Easy leak detection | Lower maintenance cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do NOT write generic points like "good refrigerant" — every property MUST be linked to a physical or thermodynamic reason.
> - Listing only 3–4 properties out of 18+ is *partial answer* — KTU examiners deduct marks for *incomplete coverage*. Use a **table format** like above; examiners reward **structured presentation**.
> - Missing ODP and GWP specifically will cost **at least 2 marks** out of 7 in 2024-scheme valuation.

---

#### **Question B: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**(a)** Define **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** and **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**. Compare R-12, R-22, R-134a, and R-717 (Ammonia) on the basis of ODP, GWP, toxicity, and flammability, with one relevant food-industry application for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration System using **R-134a** operates between an evaporator temperature of **−10 °C** and a condenser temperature of **40 °C**. The refrigerant is dry and saturated at compressor inlet. Given:
- h_1 = 247.32 kJ/kg, v_1 = 0.0694 m³/kg
- h_3 = 108.28 kJ/kg (saturated liquid at 40 °C)
- h_2 = 273.87 kJ/kg (compressor exit, dry saturated at 40 °C — using isentropic compression)

**Calculate:** *(i)* Refrigerating Effect per kg, *(ii)* Compressor work per kg, *(iii)* COP, *(iv)* Compare with Carnot COP and *(v)* Mass flow rate for 5 TR capacity. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a)**

**ODP Definition:**
$$\text{ODP}_X = \frac{\text{Mass of ozone destroyed by release of 1 kg of refrigerant X}}{\text{Mass destroyed by 1 kg of R-11 (reference)}}$$
R-11 = 1.0 (reference).

**GWP Definition:**
$$\text{GWP}_X = \frac{\text{Integrated radiative forcing over 100 years from 1 kg of gas X}}{\text{Same from 1 kg of CO}_2 \text{ (reference)}}$$
CO₂ = 1.0.

**Comparison Table:**

| Refrigerant | Type | ODP | GWP | Toxicity | Flammability | Food Industry Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R-12** | CFC | 1.0 | 10,900 | Low | None | Legacy domestic fridges (banned) |
| **R-22** | HCFC | 0.055 | 1,810 | Low | None | Cold storage, window AC (phasing out) |
| **R-134a** | HFC | 0 | 1,430 | Very Low | None | Domestic refrigeration, milk chillers, frozen food cabinets |
| **R-717 (NH₃)** | Natural | 0 | 0 | **High** | Mild | Large cold stores, ice plants, dairy processing, VARS in food plants |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b)**

**Given:** R-134a VCRS, T_evap = −10 °C, T_cond = 40 °C, Dry-saturated compression.

**(i) Refrigerating Effect (RE):**

$$h_1 = 247.32 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$h_3 = 108.28 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$h_4 = h_3 = 108.28 \text{ kJ/kg (throttling — isenthalpic)}$$

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 247.32 - 108.28 = 39.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{RE = 39.04 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(ii) Compressor Work (W):**

$$W = h_2 - h_1 = 273.87 - 247.32 = 26.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W = 26.55 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**(iii) Coefficient of Performance:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W} = \frac{39.04}{26.55}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{actual} = 1.47}$$

**(iv) Carnot COP comparison:**

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
$$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \text{ K}$$
$$T_H = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}$$

$$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{Carnot} = 5.263}$$

**Second-Law Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{Carnot}} = \frac{1.47}{5.263} = 0.279 = 27.9\%$$

*(This unusually low value indicates R-134a is not optimal for −10 °C evaporator; in practice, the problem statement uses hypothetical/simplified h values — real R-134a COP at this condition is 2.5–3.0.)*

**(v) Mass Flow Rate for 5 TR:**

$$\text{Refrigeration load} = 5 \times 3.516 = 17.58 \text{ kW} = 17.58 \text{ kJ/s}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE} = \frac{17.58}{39.04}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 0.450 \text{ kg/s} = 1620 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts:**
> 1. **Throttling assumption:** Always assume $h_4 = h_3$ for the expansion valve process — students often mistakenly write $h_4 = h_3 + W$ or $h_4 = h_3 - Q$. The expansion valve has **NO work, NO heat transfer**, only pressure drop. *This single error costs 2 marks.*
> 2. **Unit conversion for TR:** Writing $1 \text{ TR} = 3.5 \text{ kW}$ instead of **3.516 kW** in KTU 2024 scheme is acceptable in approximation, but for precise 14-mark solutions use the exact value.
> 3. **Temperature in COP:** Mixing °C and K in the Carnot equation is the **most common single mistake** in 2023/2024 papers.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Module 1**

**🔑 Production of Low Temperatures:**
- [ ] 8 main methods: ice-salt, dry ice, volatile liquids, VCRS, VARS, Air cycle, Cascade, Vortex, Thermoelectric, Magnetic, Joule-Thomson
- [ ] VCRS = dominant in food industry
- [ ] VARS = heat-driven, ideal for food plants with waste heat

**🔑 Refrigerant Classification:**
- [ ] Primary (undergoes cycle) vs Secondary (brine/glycol)
- [ ] Natural vs Synthetic (Halocarbons: CFC → HCFC → HFC → HFO)
- [ ] High / Medium / Low temperature by boiling point
- [ ] Inorganic (NH₃, CO₂, H₂O, Air) vs Organic (hydrocarbons, halocarbons)

**🔑 Nomenclature — Master Decoders:**
- [ ] R-12 = CCl₂F₂ (CFC, ODP = 1.0, **BANNED**)
- [ ] R-22 = CHClF₂ (HCFC, ODP = 0.055, phasing out)
- [ ] R-134a = CH₂FCF₃ (HFC, ODP = 0, GWP = 1430)
- [ ] R-717 = NH₃ (Ammonia, eco, toxic)
- [ ] R-744 = CO₂ (transcritical, eco)
- [ ] R-290 = Propane (HC, mild flammability)
- [ ] Suffix 'a' = isomer; Suffix 'B' = second isomer

**🔑 Desired Properties Mnemonic — "PCSTE":**
- [ ] **P**hysical — low BP, high h_fg, low cp, low viscosity, high k, high ρ_l, low v_g
- [ ] **C**hemical — stable, non-corrosive, non-reactive with oil
- [ ] **S**afety — zero ODP, low GWP, non-toxic, non-flammable
- [ ] **T**hermodynamic — low pressure ratio, high COP, low discharge T
- [ ] **E**conomical — cheap, available, easy leak detection

**🔑 Environmental Indices:**
- [ ] ODP reference = R-11 (= 1.0)
- [ ] GWP reference = CO₂ (= 1.0)
- [ ] CFCs banned under Montreal Protocol (1987)
- [ ] Kigali Amendment (2016) — phase down HFCs

**🔑 Critical Formulae:**
- [ ] $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = Q_L/W_{net} = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ — *Kelvin mandatory*
- [ ] $\text{COP}_{HP} = 1 + \text{COP}_{Ref}$
- [ ] $1 \text{ TR} = 3.516 \text{ kW}$
- [ ] $RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ (throttling $h_3 = h_4$)
- [ ] $W = h_2 - h_1$
- [ ] $\dot{m} = (\text{TR} \times 3.516)/RE$

**🔑 Food Industry Best-Practice Choices:**
- [ ] **Small plant (< 50 kW):** R-134a or R-404A
- [ ] **Large cold storage:** R-717 (NH₃)
- [ ] **Sub-zero (< −40 °C):** Cascade (NH₃/CO₂)
- [ ] **Waste-heat rich plant:** VARS using NH₃-water
- [ ] **Future-proof:** R-744 (CO₂) transcritical, HFO R-1234yf

**🔑 Examiners' Common Deductions (Avoid These!):**
- [ ] ❌ Using °C in Carnot COP → −2 marks
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting $h_3 = h_4$ in expansion valve → −2 marks
- [ ] ❌ Not stating ODP/GWP reference values → −1 mark
- [ ] ❌ Confusing Primary vs Secondary refrigerants → −1 mark
- [ ] ❌ Missing block diagram in long answers → −2 marks
- [ ] ❌ Writing "good for environment" without naming ODP/GWP values → −1 mark

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Production of Low Temperatures & Refrigerants**  
> *Next Module → Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — Detailed Thermodynamic Cycle Analysis*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Brines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Brines # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **BRINE (Secondary Refrigerant)** > A **brine** is an aqueous solution of salts (e.g., NaCl, CaCl₂, MgCl₂) or organic compounds (e.g., ethylene glycol, propylene glycol) that remains in the **liquid phase** at temperatures below the freezing point of pure water (0 °C). It functions as a **secondary refrigerant** (heat-transfer fluid) that absorbs heat from the food/product at the load side and releases it to the **primary refrigerant** at the evaporator of the vapor-compression refrigeration plant. In food-industry engineering, brines are classified under **indirect refrigeration systems** (also called **secondary-loop** or **distributed refrigeration systems**). They bridge the gap between the centralized refrigeration plant and dispersed thermal loads such as immersion tanks, jacketed mixers, spray freezers, scraped-surface heat exchangers (SSHE), and chilled-water circulation loops. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Secondary"?** The vapor-compression refrigerant (NH₃, R-404A, R-22) inside the plant is the *primary* refrigerant. The brine is *secondary* because it never undergoes a phase change inside the food-side heat exchanger — it merely **carries sensible heat** (or in some cases latent heat during ice-bank formation) from the product to the evaporator. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coolant Delivery Boy" Imagine you own a small ice-cream factory. The huge ammonia refrigeration plant sits 100 m away from your mixing vats. You cannot run refrigerant pipes through the entire kitchen (leakage hazard, contamination, pressure losses). So instead: | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent | |---|---| | Delivery boy carrying chilled water bottles | Brine circulating in a closed loop | | Cold bottles placed in vats to cool ice-cream mix | Brine flowing through the jacket of a vat | | Delivery boy returns to the central cold store to reload | Brine returning to the evaporator to be re-cooled | | Salt added to water prevents it from freezing at sub-zero temps | Brine salt depresses the freezing point | Just as the delivery boy's "coldness" depends on how much salt is dissolved in his water, a brine's usable temperature depends on its **concentration** and the salt used. --- ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold-Listed) - **Latent heat of fusion of water (at 0 °C):** **h_fg,water = 333.4 kJ/kg** (often rounded to **334 kJ/kg**). - **Specific heat of pure water:** **c_p,water = 4.186 kJ/kg·K**. - **Eutectic point** — the lowest temperature at which the brine remains a single liquid phase without ice or salt crystallization. - **Salinity (S)** — expressed as **mass percent of salt in solution (% by weight)**. - **Density (ρ)** — kg/m³, varies with concentration and temperature. - **Viscosity (μ)** — Pa·s, **critical for pumping power** calculations. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Brines (Food-Industry Relevant) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Broad Families** > 1. **Aqueous Salt Brines** — Inorganic. Cheap, high specific heat, but corrosive. > - **NaCl brine** (sodium chloride) — common, food-compatible. > - **CaCl₂ brine** (calcium chloride) — lower freezing point, used in cold storage. > - **MgCl₂ brine** (magnesium chloride) — less corrosive, niche use. > 2. **Glycol-Based Brines (Antifreeze Solutions)** — Organic. Lower freezing point, higher viscosity, **food-grade propylene glycol** is preferred for direct-contact food cooling. > - **Ethylene glycol (EG)** — toxic, **NOT** for direct food contact. > - **Propylene glycol (PG)** — non-toxic, FDA-approved for food applications. --- ## 1.5 Why Brines Matter in the Food Industry > [!NOTE] > **Driving Reasons** > - **Hygiene & Safety:** Brine leaks cannot contaminate food products (closed loop), unlike NH₃ leaks. > - **Centralized Plant:** One refrigeration plant can serve multiple cold rooms, IQF tunnels, blast freezers via brine distribution. > - **Temperature Range:** Brines can be tailored from **−1 °C** (chilled-water fish preservation) to **−40 °C** (ethylene-glycol-based low-temp brine). > - **Thermal Energy Storage (TES):** Ice-bank / eutectic-plate systems use brine to charge and discharge latent cold storage. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Freezing Point Depression — The Governing Principle When salt is dissolved in water, the salt ions disrupt the hydrogen-bonded crystal lattice of ice. This **colligative property** (depends on number of dissolved particles, not their identity) **lowers the freezing point** of the solution. > [!NOTE] > **Raoult's Law for Ideal Dilute Solutions** > > $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$$ > > where: > - $\Delta T_f$ = freezing-point depression (°C or K), > - $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant of water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol**, > - $m$ = molality (mol solute per kg solvent), > - $i$ = **van't Hoff factor** (number of ions per formula unit). For **NaCl** → $i \approx 2$ (Na⁺ + Cl⁻). For **CaCl₂** → $i \approx 3$ (Ca²⁺ + 2Cl⁻). Hence, CaCl₂ is theoretically ~1.5× more effective per mole in depressing the freezing point. **However**, real brines deviate from Raoult's law at high concentrations due to ion-ion interactions. Hence, empirical formulas are used in engineering practice. --- ## 2.2 Properties of Common Brines ### 2.2.1 Sodium Chloride (NaCl) Brine | Property | Behavior | |---|---| | **Eutectic composition** | **23.3 % by weight** of NaCl | | **Eutectic temperature** | **−21.1 °C** | | **Maximum practical limit** | ~ −18 °C (to avoid salt crystallization) | | **Specific heat (kJ/kg·K)** | $c_p \approx 3.52 - 0.0056 \cdot T - 0.00096 \cdot S$ (empirical) | | **Density (kg/m³)** | $\rho \approx 1000 + 6.2 \cdot S - 0.36 \cdot T$ | | **Viscosity** | Increases with concentration and decreasing temperature | | **Corrosiveness** | High to ferrous metals; requires inhibitors | | **Food compatibility** | Excellent (table salt) | ### 2.2.2 Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂) Brine | Property | Behavior | |---|---| | **Eutectic composition** | **29.9 % by weight** of CaCl₂ | | **Eutectic temperature** | **−51 °C** | | **Maximum practical limit** | ~ −40 °C | | **Specific heat (kJ/kg·K)** | $c_p \approx 2.78 + 0.0036 \cdot S$ (approximate) | | **Density (kg/m³)** | Higher than NaCl brine at same concentration (~1200 kg/m³ at 20%) | | **Corrosiveness** | Very high; aggressive to most metals | ### 2.2.3 Propylene Glycol (PG) Brine | Property | Behavior | |---|---| | **Concentration range** | 30–60 % by volume | | **Freezing point (50% vol)** | **−32 °C** | | **Freezing point (60% vol)** | **−50 °C** | | **Specific heat** | Lower than salt brines (~3.0–3.5 kJ/kg·K) | | **Viscosity** | Much higher than water; pump power concerns | | **Toxicity** | Low; FDA approved for indirect food contact | | **Cost** | Higher than salt brines | --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in ≥ 80% of KTU numerical problems on Brines.** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by brine | kW (kJ/s) | Heat-load calculation | | 2 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required mass flow | kg/s | Pipe sizing, pump duty | | 3 | $V = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | Volumetric flow | m³/s | Pump selection | | 4 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Volumetric flow from heat load | m³/s | Direct from Q | | 5 | $\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out}$ | Brine temperature rise | K or °C | Heat-exchanger design | | 6 | $c_{p,\text{brine}} \approx c_{p,w}(1 - 0.006 \cdot S)$ | Approximate c_p for NaCl brine | kJ/kg·K | Quick estimates (S in %) | | 7 | $T_f \approx -0.583 \cdot S - 0.0059 \cdot S^2$ | Empirical freezing point, NaCl | °C | Quick estimate | | 8 | $Q_{latent} = m \cdot h_{fg,\text{brine}}$ | Latent heat transfer in ice-brine | kW | Ice-bank / eutectic storage | | 9 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta p}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pumping power | W or kW | Brine circulation cost | | 10 | $\rho_{\text{brine}} = 1000 + 6.2 S - 0.36 T$ | NaCl density correlation | kg/m³ | Mass flow → volume flow | | 11 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) | | 12 | $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \cdot \text{Re}^{0.8} \cdot \text{Pr}^{0.4}$ | Dittus–Boelter (turbulent) | dimensionless | Convective h inside pipes | > [!NOTE] > **Board-Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Always state assumptions clearly — *incompressible flow, constant c_p over the temperature range, negligible kinetic-energy change*. Examiners specifically award the 1-mark assumption step. --- ## 2.4 Selection Criteria for a Brine (Engineering Decision Matrix) | Criterion | NaCl | CaCl₂ | Ethylene Glycol | Propylene Glycol | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cost | Low | Low | Medium | High | | Lowest usable temp | −18 °C | −40 °C | −50 °C | −45 °C | | Specific heat | High | High | Medium | Medium | | Viscosity | Low | Medium | High | High | | Corrosiveness | Medium | High | Low | Low | | Toxicity | Low | Low | **High** | Low | | Food contact (indirect) | OK | OK | **Avoid** | OK | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse ethylene glycol (toxic — radiator coolant) with propylene glycol (food-grade). Remember: **"E"**thylene = **"E"**vil; **"P"**ropylene = **"P"**alate-safe. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering - **Cold storage warehouses:** CaCl₂ brine circulated through ceiling/finned coolers. - **Immersion freezing of fish:** Direct immersion in cold NaCl brine at −18 °C. - **Scraped-surface heat exchangers (SSHE):** PG brine in the jacket for continuous ice-cream freezing. - **Ice-bank / chilled-water TES:** Brine charged overnight, discharged during peak daytime loads. - **Chill tanks for poultry carcass pre-cooling:** CaCl₂ brine at −2 to −5 °C. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Brine Mass Flow Rate from Cooling Load **Given:** A batch immersion freezer must remove **Q = 50 kW** of heat from packed fish. The CaCl₂ brine enters the tank at **T_in = −20 °C** and leaves at **T_out = −16 °C**. Concentration = 25 % by weight. Find (a) mass flow rate, (b) volumetric flow rate. **Step 1 — List knowns.** - $Q = 50 \text{ kW} = 50 \text{ kJ/s}$ - $\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out} = -16 - (-20) = 4 \text{ K}$ - $S = 25\%$ by weight - Assume $c_p$ for 25% CaCl₂ brine ≈ **2.85 kJ/kg·K** (from standard chart). **Step 2 — Apply sensible heat equation.** $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerically.** $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{50 \text{ kJ/s}}{2.85 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times 4 \text{ K}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute.** $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{50}{11.4} = 4.386 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to volumetric flow.** - Density of 25% CaCl₂ brine ≈ **1230 kg/m³** (from chart at 0 °C). $$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{4.386}{1230} = 3.566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\dot{V} = 3.566 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 12.84 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 6 — Result and physical check.** - **Mass flow:** $\dot{m} = 4.386 \text{ kg/s}$ - **Volumetric flow:** $\dot{V} \approx 12.84 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ - **Physical sense check:** Brine absorbs 50 kW with only 4 K rise — consistent because $c_p$ is high. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Freezing Point of NaCl Brine (Empirical Correlation) **Derivation from thermodynamic basis:** The chemical potential of liquid water in solution equals that of pure ice at the freezing point: $$\mu_{w,\text{solution}}(T_f) = \mu_{w,\text{ice}}(T_f)$$ For an ideal dilute solution: $$\ln(x_w) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_f} - \dfrac{1}{T_f^0} \right)$$ where $x_w$ is mole fraction of water, $T_f^0 = 273.15$ K, $\Delta H_{fus} = 6008$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. **Step 1 — Express mole fraction for NaCl ($i = 2$).** For $S$% by weight in 1 kg solution: $n_{\text{salt}} = \dfrac{S/100}{58.44}$, $n_w = \dfrac{(1 - S/100)}{18.015}$, $i = 2$. $$x_w = \dfrac{n_w}{n_w + i \cdot n_{\text{salt}}}$$ **Step 2 — For S = 20% by weight NaCl:** - $n_{\text{salt}} = 0.20 / 58.44 = 0.003422$ kmol/kg solution - $n_w = 0.80 / 18.015 = 0.04441$ kmol/kg solution - $i \cdot n_{\text{salt}} = 2 \times 0.003422 = 0.006844$ - $x_w = 0.04441 / (0.04441 + 0.006844) = 0.8665$ - $\ln(x_w) = \ln(0.8665) = -0.1433$ **Step 3 — Solve for $T_f$:** Rearrange: $$\dfrac{1}{T_f} = \dfrac{1}{273.15} - \dfrac{R \cdot \ln(x_w)}{\Delta H_{fus}}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{T_f} = 0.003661 - \dfrac{8.314 \times (-0.1433)}{6008}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{T_f} = 0.003661 + 1.984 \times 10^{-4} = 0.003859 \text{ K}^{-1}$$ $$T_f = 259.1 \text{ K} = -14.05 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 4 — Compare with empirical formula $T_f \approx -0.583 S - 0.0059 S^2$:** $T_f \approx -0.583(20) - 0.0059(400) = -11.66 - 2.36 = -14.02 \text{ °C}$ ✓ (Excellent agreement.) **Step 5 — Final answer:** Freezing point of 20% NaCl brine ≈ **−14 °C**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brine Selection for a Cold Storage (Decision Tree) **Problem:** A food cold-storage room must be maintained at **−25 °C**. Recommend the most suitable brine and justify. | Step | Reasoning | Calculation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Working temp must be **at least 3–5 K below** load temp to provide $\Delta T$ for heat transfer. | Required brine temp = −25 − 5 = **−30 °C** | | 2 | Check NaCl eutectic: −21.1 °C | **NaCl REJECTED** (cannot reach −30 °C without freezing). | | 3 | Check CaCl₂ eutectic: −51 °C | **CaCl₂ acceptable** but concentration must keep freezing point < −30 °C. | | 4 | Check PG brine: 60% vol → −50 °C | Acceptable; higher viscosity concern. | | 5 | Cost vs. performance trade-off | CaCl₂ chosen if indirect contact; PG if food-grade indirect. | **Final recommendation:** **30 % by weight CaCl₂ brine** — freezing point ≈ **−35 °C**, provides safety margin, lower cost than PG. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Brines at −20 °C | Property | 23% NaCl | 29% CaCl₂ | 50% EG | 50% PG | |---|---|---|---|---| | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | 3.30 | 2.85 | 3.05 | 3.50 | | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | 1180 | 1280 | 1070 | 1040 | | $\mu$ (mPa·s) | 3.5 | 5.2 | 6.0 | 8.0 | | Freezing point (°C) | −18 | −40 | −35 | −32 | | Corrosion inhibitor needed | Yes | Yes | No | No | | Cost index (relative) | 1.0 | 1.2 | 2.5 | 4.0 | > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Insight:** NaCl has the highest $c_p$ but lowest usable temperature. CaCl₂ offers the best low-temperature reach. EG/PG are corrosion-resistant but suffer high viscosity → larger pump power. $$\text{Pump power ratio} \propto \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot \dot{V}}{\eta} \propto \mu$$ Hence for **same mass flow**, PG brine needs ~2.3× the pumping power of NaCl brine. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Brine-Circulation Refrigeration System (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["FOOD LOAD
Cold Room / Immersion Tank / SSHE"] nodeB["BRINE
Circulating Pump"] nodeC["HEAT EXCHANGER 1
Brine Cooler / Evaporator Side"] nodeD["PRIMARY REFRIGERANT
NH3 or HFC Inside Plant"] nodeE["COMPRESSOR"] nodeF["CONDENSER
Outside Plant"] nodeG["EXPANSION VALVE
Throttling Device"] nodeH["EVAPORATOR
Inside Plant / Brine Cooler"] nodeA -->|Heat absorbed by brine| nodeB nodeB -->|Warm brine returns| nodeC nodeC -->|Cold rejected to primary refrigerant| nodeD nodeD -->|Vapor compressed| nodeE nodeE -->|Hot vapor| nodeF nodeF -->|Liquid refrigerant| nodeG nodeG -->|Low pressure liquid-vapor mix| nodeH nodeH -->|Cold vapor primary refrigerant| nodeC style nodeA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B85400,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#B5D5FF,stroke:#1A4A8F,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#2C6B2E,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#7A1F1F,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#5B2C6F,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#A87C00,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#555,color:#000 style nodeH fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#2C6B2E,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote the *direction of working fluid flow*. The **brine loop** is the *lower loop* (food side), while the **vapor-compression loop** is the *upper loop* (plant side). The **brine cooler / evaporator** is the **thermal bridge** between the two loops. --- ## 4.2 Brine Classification & Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["START
Choose Brine for Food Industry"] nodeA{"Required Working
Temperature?"} nodeB["WARM BRINE 0 to -10 C
NaCl 5 to 15 percent"] nodeC{"Below -20 C?"} nodeD["COLD BRINE -20 to -40 C
CaCl2 20 to 30 percent"] nodeE["VERY COLD BRINE below -40 C
PG or EG 50 to 60 percent"] nodeF{"Direct Food
Contact?"} nodeG["USE NaCl brine
Food grade salt"] nodeH["USE PG Brine
Propylene Glycol Food Grade"] nodeI["USE CaCl2 Brine
With corrosion inhibitor"] nodeJ["USE EG Brine
Ethylene Glycol only for indirect"] nodeStart --> nodeA nodeA -->|Above -10 C| nodeB nodeB --> nodeF nodeA -->|Below -10 C| nodeC nodeC -->|Yes -20 to -40| nodeD nodeD --> nodeI nodeC -->|Below -40| nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF -->|Yes direct contact| nodeG nodeF -->|No indirect only| nodeJ nodeF -.->|PG alternative| nodeH style nodeStart fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#888800,color:#000 style nodeA fill:#FFCCAA,stroke:#883300,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#B5D5FF,stroke:#1A4A8F,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#FFCCAA,stroke:#883300,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#A8D8E6,stroke:#1A5F7A,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#5B2C6F,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#FFCCAA,stroke:#883300,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#2C6B2E,color:#000 style nodeH fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#2C6B2E,color:#000 style nodeI fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#7A1F1F,color:#000 style nodeJ fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#7A1F1F,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Schematic — Immersion Freezing System Using NaCl Brine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PLANT["REFRIGERATION PLANT HOUSE"] COMP["COMPRESSOR"] COND["CONDENSER"] TXV["EXPANSION VALVE"] EVAP["EVAPORATOR
BRINE COOLER"] end subgraph LOOP["BRINE CIRCULATION LOOP"] PUMP["CENTRIFUGAL PUMP"] TANK["IMMERSION TANK
Fish in NaCl Brine"] PIPE1["Supply Pipe -18 C"] PIPE2["Return Pipe -14 C"] end EVAP -->|"Cold brine -20 C"| PIPE1 PIPE1 --> TANK TANK --> PIPE2 PIPE2 --> PUMP PUMP --> EVAP COMP --> COND COND --> TXV TXV --> EVAP style PLANT fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#333399,color:#000 style LOOP fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#1A4A8F,color:#000 style COMP fill:#FF9999,stroke:#660000,color:#000 style COND fill:#FFCC66,stroke:#996600,color:#000 style TXV fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,color:#000 style EVAP fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#2C6B2E,color:#000 style PUMP fill:#FFD580,stroke:#996600,color:#000 style TANK fill:#B5D5FF,stroke:#1A4A8F,color:#000 style PIPE1 fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#2C6B2E,color:#000 style PIPE2 fill:#FFB380,stroke:#996600,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define the term "brine" as used in food-industry refrigeration. Why is it called a "secondary refrigerant"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A brine is an aqueous solution of salts (such as NaCl or CaCl₂) or organic compounds (such as ethylene glycol or propylene glycol) that remains in the liquid state at sub-zero temperatures due to **freezing-point depression**. It is called a *secondary refrigerant* because it does not undergo phase change inside the food-side heat exchanger; instead, it absorbs **sensible heat** from the product and transports it to the evaporator of the primary vapor-compression refrigeration system, where the heat is rejected to the primary refrigerant (e.g., ammonia). The brine thus *carries* coldness but does not *generate* it. **[3 marks]** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **State the eutectic composition and eutectic temperature of NaCl brine and CaCl₂ brine.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Brine | Eutectic Composition (wt %) | Eutectic Temperature (°C) | |---|---|---| | NaCl | 23.3 % | −21.1 °C | | CaCl₂ | 29.9 % | −51 °C | Below the eutectic temperature, both ice and salt crystals precipitate simultaneously, making the brine unusable as a circulating fluid. **[3 marks]** --- ## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the required **mass flow rate of brine** to absorb a given heat load Q in a secondary-refrigeration loop. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A food cold-storage room has a total cooling load of **80 kW**. CaCl₂ brine at a concentration of 25 % by weight enters the room cooler at **−28 °C** and leaves at **−22 °C**. Calculate: **(i)** the required **mass flow rate** of brine, **(ii)** the **volumetric flow rate**, and **(iii)** the **pumping power** if the total pressure drop across the loop is **120 kPa** and pump efficiency is **70 %**. Take $c_p$ = 2.85 kJ/kg·K and ρ = 1230 kg/m³ for 25 % CaCl₂ brine. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Derivation:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state, one-dimensional flow. 2. Constant $c_p$ over the temperature range. 3. No phase change in the brine (purely sensible heat transfer). 4. Negligible heat gain/loss from pipe walls. 5. Incompressible flow. **Derivation:** Applying an **energy balance** to a control volume enclosing the brine-side heat exchanger: $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in})$$ For an incompressible substance: $$h_{out} - h_{in} = c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ Therefore: $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ Solving for the required mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}$$ **Physical meaning:** For a fixed cooling load $\dot{Q}$, the mass flow rate is *inversely proportional* to both the specific heat of the brine and the permissible temperature rise. Brines with high $c_p$ (like water-based salt solutions) require lower flow rates. **[7 marks — full derivation with stated assumptions]** --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $\dot{Q} = 80 \text{ kW} = 80 \text{ kJ/s}$ - $T_{in} = -28 \text{ °C}$, $T_{out} = -22 \text{ °C}$ - $\Delta T = -22 - (-28) = 6 \text{ K}$ - $c_p = 2.85 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ - $\rho = 1230 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $\Delta p = 120 \text{ kPa} = 120{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ - $\eta_{pump} = 0.70$ **(i) Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{80}{2.85 \times 6} = \dfrac{80}{17.10} = 4.678 \text{ kg/s}$$ **(ii) Volumetric flow rate:** $$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{4.678}{1230} = 3.803 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\dot{V} = 3.803 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 13.69 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ **(iii) Pumping power:** $$P_{pump} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta p}{\eta_{pump}} = \dfrac{3.803 \times 10^{-3} \times 120{,}000}{0.70}$$ $$P_{pump} = \dfrac{456.4}{0.70} = 652.0 \text{ W} \approx 0.652 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Results:** - $\dot{m} = 4.678 \text{ kg/s}$ - $\dot{V} = 13.69 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ - $P_{pump} \approx 0.652 \text{ kW}$ **[7 marks — 2 for m, 2 for V, 3 for pump power with unit conversion]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` — INTERNAL CHOICE **(a)** With the help of a **phase diagram sketch**, explain the concept of **eutectic point** in a binary aqueous brine system. Discuss why operating near the eutectic point is avoided in practical refrigeration systems. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the properties and applications of **NaCl, CaCl₂, and propylene glycol (PG) brines** in food-industry refrigeration. Recommend a suitable brine for each of the following applications, justifying your choice: **(i)** Immersion freezing of fresh fish (−18 °C), **(ii)** Central chilled-water system for a dairy plant (+2 °C), **(iii)** Low-temperature cold storage for frozen meat (−35 °C). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Eutectic Concept:** A binary solution of salt and water exhibits a characteristic **phase diagram** with temperature on the y-axis and composition (wt % salt) on the x-axis. As salt concentration increases, the freezing point of the solution decreases along the *liquidus line*, reaching a minimum at the **eutectic point** — the unique composition and temperature at which the solution freezes into a fine mechanical mixture of **ice + salt crystals** simultaneously. **Phase diagram (sketch description):** | Region | Phases Present | |---|---| | Above liquidus line | Single liquid phase (solution) | | Between liquidus and solidus | Liquid + ice crystals (or liquid + salt crystals) | | Below eutectic horizontal | Solid ice + solid salt (no liquid) | **At the eutectic point:** - NaCl: 23.3 % salt, **−21.1 °C**. - CaCl₂: 29.9 % salt, **−51 °C**. **Why avoid operating near eutectic:** 1. **Salt crystallization** blocks pipes, valves, and pumps. 2. **Reduced liquid fraction** reduces heat-transfer capacity. 3. **Composition drift** — pure ice preferentially freezes out, concentrating the remaining liquid beyond eutectic and causing runaway crystallization. 4. **Pump cavitation risk** due to two-phase flow. 5. Practically, brines operate at temperatures **5–10 K above** their freezing point. **[7 marks]** --- **Part (b) — Comparative Table + Application Justification:** **Comparative Properties:** | Property | NaCl Brine | CaCl₂ Brine | Propylene Glycol | |---|---|---|---| | Lowest usable temp | −18 °C | −40 °C | −45 °C | | Specific heat | High | High | Medium | | Viscosity | Low | Medium | High | | Toxicity | Low | Low | Low (food-grade) | | Cost | Low | Low | High | | Corrosion | Moderate | High | Negligible | **Application Recommendations:** **(i) Immersion freezing of fish (−18 °C):** **Recommendation:** **23 % NaCl brine** at −18 °C. **Justification:** Highest $c_p$ (efficient heat removal), low viscosity (good pumpability), food-compatible, lowest cost. Brine is in **direct contact** with fish — toxicity must be minimal (NaCl is ideal). **[2 marks]** **(ii) Dairy plant chilled-water system (+2 °C):** **Recommendation:** **10 % NaCl brine** (freezing point ≈ −6 °C). **Justification:** Operating temp +2 °C is well above NaCl freezing point — no crystallization risk. High $c_p$ means lower flow rates and smaller pipes. **Indirect contact** through plate heat exchangers allows use of simple, cheap salt brine. **[2 marks]** **(iii) Frozen meat cold storage (−35 °C):** **Recommendation:** **CaCl₂ brine at 28–29 % by weight** (freezing point ≈ −45 °C, safety margin of 10 K). **Justification:** NaCl eutectic (−21 °C) is exceeded. PG is possible but cost-prohibitive. CaCl₂ provides the lowest usable temperature among salt brines. Add **sodium chromate corrosion inhibitor**. **[3 marks]** --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in derivations → −1 mark. > 2. **Sign convention errors** in $\Delta T$: Always use $(T_{out} - T_{in})$ algebraically. If $T_{out} > T_{in}$, the brine absorbs heat (positive Q). If you write $\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out}$, you must say "brine gains heat = m c_p (T_in − T_out)." > 3. **Confusing ethylene glycol and propylene glycol.** EG is **toxic**; PG is food-safe. Examiners specifically test this in 2–3 mark questions. > 4. **Forgetting density conversion** between mass and volumetric flow rates. > 5. **Not converting kW to kJ/s** when using Q in the equation $m = Q/(c_p \cdot \Delta T)$. > 6. **Eutectic point values must be quoted with both composition and temperature** — quoting only one is incomplete. > 7. **Forgetting to add safety margin** (5–10 K) when selecting brine concentration for a target working temperature. > 8. **Not mentioning corrosion inhibitors** for salt brines — food-industry examiners love this practical detail. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Brines (Module 2)** - ☐ **Definition:** Brine = aqueous salt/glycol solution; acts as **secondary refrigerant**; remains liquid below 0 °C due to **freezing-point depression**. - ☐ **Primary vs. Secondary:** Primary refrigerant undergoes phase change (NH₃, HFCs); secondary does not (NaCl, CaCl₂, PG). - ☐ **Eutectic points to memorize:** - NaCl → **23.3 % by weight, −21.1 °C** - CaCl₂ → **29.9 % by weight, −51 °C** - ☐ **Working rule:** Always operate brine **5–10 K above** its freezing point. - ☐ **Master equations:** - $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ - $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ - $\dot{V} = \dot{m} / \rho$ - $P_{pump} = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta p / \eta_{pump}$ - ☐ **Selection logic:** - Above −10 °C → **NaCl** (cheap, high $c_p$). - −10 to −40 °C → **CaCl₂** (low freezing point, food-safe). - Below −40 °C → **PG or EG** (corrosion-resistant, expensive). - ☐ **EG vs. PG:** EG = toxic (NOT for food). PG = food-safe (FDA-approved). - ☐ **Applications in food industry:** immersion freezing, SSHE for ice-cream, cold storage, ice-bank TES, jacketed vessels. - ☐ **Engineering cautions:** corrosion inhibitors for salt brines; viscosity penalty for glycol brines; pump power scales with viscosity. - ☐ **Key properties to look up from charts:** $c_p$, $\rho$, $\mu$, freezing point — all vary with both **concentration** and **temperature**. - ☐ **Typical exam question pattern (KTU):** Given Q, ΔT, brine type → find $\dot{m}$, $\dot{V}$, $P_{pump}$, or recommend a brine for a stated application with justification. --- > **END OF NOTES — BRINES (MODULE 2) | OEFTT612 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

topics.title: Freezing Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Freezing Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FREEZING TANKS: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freezing Tank:** A freezing tank is a thermally insulated, jacketed processing vessel employed in the food industry to bring a liquid, semi-liquid, or immersed solid food product to a sub-zero temperature (typically between **-1 °C and -40 °C**) by indirect contact with a circulating refrigerant (brine, glycol, ammonia, or Freon) housed within the double walls or coils of the tank. The primary purpose is either to **partially freeze** the product (e.g., ice cream mix at -2 °C to -6 °C) or to **rapidly chill and freeze** it for preservation (e.g., immersion freezing of fish in brine at **-20 °C**).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEFTT612)**, freezing tanks fall under the umbrella of **indirect-contact freezers**, where the food product is never in direct contact with the refrigerant; instead, heat is removed via a cooled wall surface or through an intermediate secondary coolant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of OEFTT612 covers various food freezing techniques — air-blast, plate, immersion, cryogenic, and **tank/immersion freezing systems**. Freezing tanks are categorized under **contact freezers** and are critical in continuous industrial food processing lines.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are making **homemade ice cream** in a metal bowl. You place the bowl inside a larger container filled with **ice and salt** (a brine). The salt lowers the freezing point of water, making the mixture colder than 0 °C. As you stir, the cold brine extracts heat through the metal walls of the bowl, and the cream mixture gradually freezes.

A **commercial freezing tank** operates on exactly this principle — but at industrial scale:

- The **outer jacket** = your salt-ice bath.
- The **refrigerant or brine** = the cooling medium.
- The **inner tank wall** = the metal bowl acting as a heat-transfer surface.
- The **agitator/scraper blade** = your hand-stirring spoon, which continuously removes the frozen layer from the wall to maintain efficient heat transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a home freezer, an industrial freezing tank uses **continuous mechanical agitation** and a **precisely controlled refrigerant cycle** to ensure uniform freezing and prevent large ice crystals from forming (which would damage food texture).

---

## 1.3 Primary Functions of Freezing Tanks in the Food Industry

1. **Rapid Surface Freezing** of packaged/unpackaged food to lock in freshness.
2. **Ice Cream Hardening** (preparation of mix before entering a continuous freezer).
3. **Brine Immersion Freezing** of seafood (shrimp, fish fillets) at **-15 °C to -25 °C**.
4. **Pre-cooling of bulk liquids** (juice concentrates, dairy products) before packaging.
5. **Partial Freezing / Slush Production** for beverages and dessert bases.

## 1.4 Standard Operational Metrics (Industry Benchmarks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Refrigerant temperatures:** typically **-25 °C to -40 °C** for ammonia (R-717) systems.
> - **Brine (secondary coolant) temperatures:** typically **-18 °C to -25 °C** (calcium chloride or sodium chloride brine).
> - **Product residence time:** ranges from **15 minutes (thin fish fillets)** to **4 hours (bulk ice cream mix)**.
> - **Heat transfer coefficient (h):** for agitated brine, **h ≈ 500–2000 W/m²·K** depending on agitator RPM and viscosity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FREEZING TANKS: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Freezing Tanks

Freezing tanks are broadly classified based on the **mode of heat transfer** and **method of agitation**:

| Type | Description | Typical Application | Product Examples |
|------|-------------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Jacketed Agitated Tank** | Double-wall vessel with refrigerant in jacket; mechanical scraper/agitator inside | Batch ice cream freezing, dairy chilling | Ice cream mix, custard |
| **Brine Immersion Tank** | Product directly immersed in cold brine (food-grade) | Seafood, vegetables | Shrimp, peas, fish |
| **Scraped Surface Freezing Tank (SSFT)** | Tank with rotating scraper blades that continuously remove frozen layer from wall | Continuous ice cream freezing | Ice cream, sorbet |
| **Spiral/Coil-in-Tank Freezer** | Refrigerant coils immersed directly in the product tank | Bulk liquid cooling | Juices, milk |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The **Scraped Surface Freezing Tank (SSFT)** is the most industrially significant variant and is frequently asked in Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Refrigerant Circulation:** A vapor-compression refrigeration cycle supplies liquid refrigerant (commonly **ammonia — R-717** or **R-404A**) to the tank jacket or coils.
2. **Heat Absorption:** The refrigerant absorbs heat from the tank wall through **latent heat of vaporization** (boiling inside the jacket at low pressure).
3. **Wall Conduction:** Heat flows through the stainless-steel tank wall by **Fourier conduction**.
4. **Convective Heat Transfer in Product:** The agitated food product transfers heat to the cold wall via **forced convection**.
5. **Phase Change (Latent Heat Removal):** As the product temperature drops below its freezing point, **latent heat of fusion** must be removed — this is the largest heat load.
6. **Scraper Action (in SSFT):** A rotating blade scrapes the forming ice layer off the wall, exposing fresh cold metal to the product. This maintains a **high effective heat transfer coefficient**.

---

## 2.3 Governing Heat Transfer Equation

The rate of heat removal in a freezing tank is governed by:

$$\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = rate of heat transfer (W or kW)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = effective heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = Log Mean Temperature Difference between refrigerant and product (K)

### The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD):

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{product,in} - T_{refrigerant,out}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{product,out} - T_{refrigerant,in}$

---

## 2.4 Total Freezing Time (Plank's Equation — Most Frequently Tested in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plank's Equation** is the cornerstone formula for calculating freezing time in slab/cylindrical/spherical food products inside a freezing tank.

$$t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{T_f - T_{medium}} \left( \frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k} \right)$$

Where:
- $t_f$ = freezing time (seconds)
- $\rho$ = density of food (kg/m³)
- $L$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $T_f$ = initial freezing temperature of food (°C)
- $T_{medium}$ = temperature of cooling medium (brine/refrigerant) (°C)
- $a$ = characteristic half-thickness (m) — for slab = thickness/2
- $h$ = surface heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of frozen food (W/m·K)
- $P, R$ = Plank's geometric constants

### Plank's Constants for Common Geometries:

| Shape | P | R |
|-------|---|---|
| Infinite slab | 1/2 | 1/8 |
| Infinite cylinder | 1/4 | 1/16 |
| Sphere | 1/6 | 1/24 |
| Finite cylinder (length = diameter) | ~1/3.6 | ~1/24 |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Exam — Freezing Tanks**

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Refrigeration load on tank |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Heat exchanger driving force |
| 3 | $t_f = \frac{\rho L (P \cdot a / h + R \cdot a^2 / k)}{T_f - T_m}$ | Plank's freezing time |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{total} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f$ | Sensible + latent heat load |
| 5 | $COP = \frac{Q_L}{W_{comp}}$ | Refrigeration system efficiency |
| 6 | $R = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k_{wall}} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | Overall thermal resistance |
| 7 | $U = \frac{1}{R}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{h_{fg,ref}}$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Industry:** SSFT freezing tanks are the heart of continuous ice cream production lines (capacity: **2000–10,000 L/h**).
- **Seafood Industry:** Brine immersion tanks achieve freezing rates **5–10× faster** than air-blast, reducing cell damage.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Modern SSFT systems achieve **COP of 3.5–4.5**, making them one of the most thermally efficient food freezing methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FREEZING TANKS: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Refrigeration Load of a Freezing Tank

> **Problem Statement:** A freezing tank processes **500 kg/h** of fish fillets entering at **+10 °C** and leaving at **-15 °C** (frozen). The initial freezing point of fish is **-1 °C**. The refrigerant (ammonia) enters the jacket at **-30 °C** and leaves at **-25 °C**. Calculate:
> (a) The total heat load to be removed.
> (b) The log mean temperature difference.
> (c) The required jacket area if $U = 800 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot$K$.

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m} = 500 \text{ kg/h} = 0.1389 \text{ kg/s}$
- $T_{in} = +10 °C$, $T_{out} = -15 °C$
- $T_{freeze} = -1 °C$
- $c_{p,above} = 3.6 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (fish above freezing)
- $c_{p,below} = 1.7 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ (fish below freezing)
- $L_f = 235 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (latent heat of fish)
- $T_{ref,in} = -30 °C$, $T_{ref,out} = -25 °C$

### (a) Step-by-Step: Total Heat Load

The total heat load consists of three components:
1. Sensible heat from **+10 °C to -1 °C** (above freezing)
2. Latent heat removal at **-1 °C**
3. Sensible heat from **-1 °C to -15 °C** (below freezing)

**Step 1: Sensible heat above freezing point**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{freeze}) \\
Q_1 &= 0.1389 \times 3.6 \times (10 - (-1)) \\
Q_1 &= 0.1389 \times 3.6 \times 11 \\
Q_1 &= 5.500 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Latent heat of fusion**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= \dot{m} \cdot L_f \\
Q_2 &= 0.1389 \times 235 \\
Q_2 &= 32.642 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat below freezing point**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_3 &= \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,below} \cdot (T_{freeze} - T_{out}) \\
Q_3 &= 0.1389 \times 1.7 \times (-1 - (-15)) \\
Q_3 &= 0.1389 \times 1.7 \times 14 \\
Q_3 &= 3.306 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Total heat load**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 \\
\dot{Q}_{total} &= 5.500 + 32.642 + 3.306 \\
\dot{Q}_{total} &= 41.448 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** The total refrigeration load on the freezing tank is **41.45 kW** (approximately **3.58 TR** — Tons of Refrigeration, since 1 TR = 3.517 kW).

---

### (b) Step-by-Step: Log Mean Temperature Difference

**Step 1: Compute temperature differences at both ends**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{product,in} - T_{ref,out} = 10 - (-25) = 35 °C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{product,out} - T_{ref,in} = -15 - (-30) = 15 °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply LMTD formula**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{35 - 15}{\ln(35 / 15)} \\
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{20}{\ln(2.3333)} \\
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{20}{0.8473} \\
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= 23.60 °C
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = 23.60 \text{ K}$

---

### (c) Step-by-Step: Required Jacket Heat Transfer Area

Rearranging the fundamental heat transfer equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} \\
A &= \frac{41{,}448 \text{ W}}{800 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K} \times 23.60 \text{ K}} \\
A &= \frac{41{,}448}{18{,}880} \\
A &= 2.195 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** The required jacket heat transfer area is **2.20 m²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice Note:** A safety factor of **1.15–1.25** is typically applied, giving a design area of **~2.6 m²**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Plank's Freezing Time for a Fish Fillet (Slab Geometry)

> **Problem Statement:** Calculate the freezing time of a **2 cm thick fish fillet** (slab geometry) being frozen in a brine tank at **-25 °C**. Use Plank's equation.
>
> **Given:**
> - $a = 0.01 \text{ m}$ (half-thickness)
> - $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - $L_f = 235 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - $T_f = -1 °C$, $T_m = -25 °C$
> - $h = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$ (brine-side convection)
> - $k = 1.5 \text{ W/m·K}$ (frozen fish)
> - $P = 0.5$, $R = 0.125$ (slab geometry)

$$\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{\rho \cdot L_f \cdot \left(\frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k}\right)}{T_f - T_m} \\
t_f &= \frac{1050 \times 235{,}000 \times \left(\frac{0.5 \times 0.01}{500} + \frac{0.125 \times (0.01)^2}{1.5}\right)}{-1 - (-25)} \\
t_f &= \frac{1050 \times 235{,}000 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 8.333 \times 10^{-6})}{24} \\
t_f &= \frac{1050 \times 235{,}000 \times 1.8333 \times 10^{-5}}{24} \\
t_f &= \frac{4523.04}{24} \\
t_f &= 188.46 \text{ seconds} \\
t_f &\approx 3.14 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** The fish fillet will freeze in approximately **3.14 minutes**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FREEZING TANKS: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Scraped Surface Freezing Tank (SSFT) System

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The diagram below represents the **functional architecture flow** of a typical SSFT system, showing all major subsystems and their interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[PRODUCT INLET TANK]:::tank --> node2[STAINLESS STEEL JACKETED VESSEL]:::tank
    node3[REFRIGERANT INLET -30C]:::refrig --> node2
    node2 --> node4[REFRIGERANT OUTLET -25C]:::refrig
    node2 --> node5[ROTATING SCRAPER BLADES WITHIN TANK]:::scraper
    node5 --> node6[FROZEN PRODUCT SCRAPED FROM WALL]:::product
    node5 --> node7[AGITATOR DRIVE MOTOR 200 RPM]:::motor
    node2 --> node8[PRODUCT OUTLET -15C]:::product
    node9[VAPOR COMPRESSOR UNIT]:::refrig --> node10[CONDENSER UNIT]:::refrig
    node10 --> node11[EXPANSION VALVE]:::refrig
    node11 --> node3
    node2 --> node12[INSULATION LAYER 100 MM PU FOAM]:::tank
    node8 --> node13[PACKAGING LINE]:::product

    classDef tank fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef refrig fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef scraper fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Description of Subsystem Functions:

| Subsystem | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Product Inlet Tank** | Pre-mixed food product (e.g., ice cream mix) enters at controlled flow rate |
| **Stainless Steel Jacketed Vessel** | Primary freezing chamber; double-wall construction |
| **Refrigerant Inlet (-30 °C)** | Liquid ammonia or R-404A from expansion valve |
| **Rotating Scraper Blades** | Mechanically remove frozen layer from inner wall |
| **Agitator Drive Motor** | Provides rotational energy (typically **100–300 RPM**) |
| **Vapor Compressor Unit** | Compresses refrigerant vapor (the heart of the refrigeration cycle) |
| **Insulation Layer** | Polyurethane foam (100 mm) minimizes heat ingress from surroundings |

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Heat Flow Through Freezing Tank Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
