```mermaid
    A[HOT FOOD PRODUCT +10C]:::hot --> B[CONVECTIVE FILM INSIDE TANK]:::step
    B --> C[FOOD SIDE FOULING RESISTANCE]:::step
    C --> D[INNER SS WALL THERMAL CONDUCTION]:::step
    D --> E[OUTER SS WALL]:::step
    E --> F[REFRIGERANT SIDE FOULING RESISTANCE]:::step
    F --> G[CONVECTIVE FILM BOILING REFRIGERANT]:::step
    G --> H[COLD REFRIGERANT -30C]:::cold

    classDef hot fill:#FECACA,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#A16207,color:#000
```

### Thermal Resistance Network (Equivalent Circuit):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= R_{conv,i} + R_{foul,i} + R_{wall} + R_{foul,o} + R_{conv,o} \\
R_{total} &= \frac{1}{h_i A} + \frac{x_f}{k_f A} + \frac{x_{wall}}{k_{ss} A} + \frac{x_{f,o}}{k_{f,o} A} + \frac{1}{h_o A}
\end{aligned}$$

For a typical SSFT: $R_{conv,i}$ (product side) is the **largest** resistance — hence the **scraper blade** is critical.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FREEZING TANKS: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q: Define a freezing tank. List any two applications of freezing tanks in the food industry.**

**Model Answer:**

> A freezing tank is a thermally insulated, jacketed processing vessel used in the food industry to lower the temperature of food products through indirect contact with a circulating refrigerant or cold brine. Heat is removed through the tank wall, and mechanical agitation/scraping is often employed to maintain a high heat transfer rate.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Ice cream hardening** — Used in dairy plants to bring ice cream mix from ~+5 °C to -6 °C in a controlled batch process.
2. **Brine immersion freezing of seafood** — Fish fillets and shrimp are immersed in cold brine at -18 °C to -25 °C for rapid surface freezing, preserving texture and color.

*(Marks distribution: Definition — 2 marks; Applications — ½ + ½ mark)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the role of a scraper blade in a Scraped Surface Freezing Tank (SSFT)?**

**Model Answer:**

> In an SSFT, the scraper blade is a mechanically rotating blade mounted on a central shaft inside the freezing tank. Its primary roles are:
> 1. **Continuously removing the frozen layer** that forms on the inner wall of the tank, thereby maintaining direct contact between the cold wall and the bulk product.
> 2. **Preventing the build-up of an insulating ice layer**, which would drastically reduce the heat transfer coefficient over time.
> 3. **Promoting uniform freezing** and preventing the formation of large ice crystals, which improves the texture and quality of the final product (especially in ice cream).

*(Marks distribution: Identification — 1 mark; Three roles — 1 mark each, but total capped at 2 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by:
>   1. **Forgetting to subtract** latent heat in total heat-load calculations (or adding it twice).
>   2. **Mixing up $T_{freeze}$ and $T_{initial}$** in Plank's equation.
>   3. **Incorrectly applying Plank's constants** — using cylinder constants for a slab problem.
>   4. **Ignoring unit conversions** — kJ vs J, kg/h vs kg/s.
> - **Always show all 3 steps** in heat-load calculations: *Sensible (above) + Latent + Sensible (below)*.

---

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**Q: Explain the working principle and construction of a Scraped Surface Freezing Tank (SSFT). A freezing tank is used to freeze 800 kg/h of fish fillet entering at +5 °C and exiting at -18 °C. The freezing point of fish is -1 °C. The refrigerant (R-717) enters at -32 °C and leaves at -27 °C. Calculate:**
**(a) The total refrigeration load in kW. (7 marks)**
**(b) The log mean temperature difference and the required heat transfer area if $U = 750 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot$K. (7 marks)**

**Use:** $c_{p,above} = 3.6 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $c_{p,below} = 1.7 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $L_f = 235 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

---

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 800 \text{ kg/h} = 0.2222 \text{ kg/s}$
- $T_{in} = +5 °C$, $T_{out} = -18 °C$, $T_f = -1 °C$
- $T_{ref,in} = -32 °C$, $T_{ref,out} = -27 °C$
- $U = 750 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot$K

**Working Principle and Construction (Brief — integrated into solution context):**

The SSFT consists of a horizontal or vertical **jacketed cylindrical vessel** made of stainless steel. The annular space (jacket) houses boiling refrigerant. A central shaft with **scraper blades** rotates at 100–300 RPM. As the food product enters, it contacts the cold wall; the scraper continuously removes the frozen layer, ensuring a high heat transfer coefficient. The refrigerant absorbs heat and evaporates, completing the refrigeration cycle externally via a compressor, condenser, and expansion valve.

---

### (a) Total Refrigeration Load (7 marks)

**Step 1: Sensible heat above freezing (+5 °C → -1 °C)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot (T_{in} - T_f) \\
Q_1 &= 0.2222 \times 3.6 \times (5 - (-1)) \\
Q_1 &= 0.2222 \times 3.6 \times 6 \\
Q_1 &= 4.800 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Latent heat at -1 °C**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= \dot{m} \cdot L_f \\
Q_2 &= 0.2222 \times 235 \\
Q_2 &= 52.222 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat below freezing (-1 °C → -18 °C)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_3 &= \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,below} \cdot (T_f - T_{out}) \\
Q_3 &= 0.2222 \times 1.7 \times (-1 - (-18)) \\
Q_3 &= 0.2222 \times 1.7 \times 17 \\
Q_3 &= 6.422 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Total Heat Load**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{total} &= Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 \\
\dot{Q}_{total} &= 4.800 + 52.222 + 6.422 \\
\dot{Q}_{total} &= 63.444 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (a):** Total refrigeration load = **63.44 kW** (≈ **18.04 TR**)

---

### (b) LMTD and Heat Transfer Area (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute temperature differences**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{in} - T_{ref,out} = 5 - (-27) = 32 °C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{out} - T_{ref,in} = -18 - (-32) = 14 °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply LMTD**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{32 - 14}{\ln(32 / 14)} \\
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{18}{\ln(2.2857)} \\
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= \frac{18}{0.8267} \\
\Delta T_{LMTD} &= 21.77 °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Required area**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} \\
A &= \frac{63{,}444}{750 \times 21.77} \\
A &= \frac{63{,}444}{16{,}327.5} \\
A &= 3.886 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (b):** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = 21.77 \text{ K}$; Required area = **3.89 m²** (design area with 20% safety factor ≈ **4.66 m²**)

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a brine immersion freezing tank. A 4 cm thick slab of meat (density 1050 kg/m³, latent heat 240 kJ/kg, k = 1.4 W/m·K, freezing point -2 °C) is to be frozen in a brine tank at -28 °C. The surface heat transfer coefficient is 600 W/m²·K. Calculate:**
**(a) The freezing time using Plank's equation. (7 marks)**
**(b) If the slab thickness is doubled, how does the freezing time change? Justify with calculations. (7 marks)**

**Plank's constants for slab:** $P = 1/2$, $R = 1/8$.

---

#### Model Solution:

**Construction & Working (Brief):**

A brine immersion tank is a large insulated vessel containing a **food-grade brine solution** (typically NaCl or CaCl₂ brine) cooled to -15 °C to -25 °C by an external refrigeration system. Packaged or unpackaged food (seafood, vegetables, meat) is **directly immersed** in the brine. Heat flows rapidly from the food to the brine through forced convection. The brine is continuously circulated by pumps and re-cooled by a separate refrigeration loop to maintain a uniform temperature. The product is removed once its core reaches the desired temperature.

---

### (a) Freezing Time for 4 cm Slab (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $a = 0.02 \text{ m}$ (half-thickness = 4/2 cm)
- $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $L = 240{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$
- $T_f = -2 °C$, $T_m = -28 °C$, $h = 600 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot$K
- $k = 1.4 \text{ W/m·K}$, $P = 0.5$, $R = 0.125$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot \left(\frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k}\right)}{T_f - T_m} \\
t_f &= \frac{1050 \times 240{,}000 \times \left(\frac{0.5 \times 0.02}{600} + \frac{0.125 \times (0.02)^2}{1.4}\right)}{-2 - (-28)} \\
t_f &= \frac{1050 \times 240{,}000 \times (1.667 \times 10^{-5} + 3.571 \times 10^{-5})}{26} \\
t_f &= \frac{1050 \times 240{,}000 \times 5.238 \times 10^{-5}}{26} \\
t_f &= \frac{13{,}200}{26} \\
t_f &= 507.7 \text{ seconds} \\
t_f &\approx 8.46 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (a):** Freezing time ≈ **8.46 minutes** (≈ 507.7 s)

---

### (b) Effect of Doubling the Slab Thickness (7 marks)

If slab thickness is doubled: $2a = 4 \text{ cm} \Rightarrow a = 0.04 \text{ m}$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_f' &= \frac{1050 \times 240{,}000 \times \left(\frac{0.5 \times 0.04}{600} + \frac{0.125 \times (0.04)^2}{1.4}\right)}{26} \\
t_f' &= \frac{1050 \times 240{,}000 \times (3.333 \times 10^{-5} + 1.4286 \times 10^{-4})}{26} \\
t_f' &= \frac{1050 \times 240{,}000 \times 1.7619 \times 10^{-4}}{26} \\
t_f' &= \frac{44{,}400}{26} \\
t_f' &= 1707.7 \text{ seconds} \\
t_f' &\approx 28.46 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{t_f'}{t_f} &= \frac{1707.7}{507.7} \approx 3.36
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (b):** Doubling the slab thickness increases freezing time by a factor of **~3.36** (not exactly 4× because Plank's equation contains a linear convective term $\propto a$ in addition to a quadratic conductive term $\propto a^2$).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Freezing Tanks**

- ✅ **Definition:** A freezing tank is an **indirect-contact**, **insulated**, **agitated** vessel that freezes food via a cooled wall or immersed brine.
- ✅ **Classification:** Jacketed agitated / Brine immersion / Scraped Surface / Coil-in-tank.
- ✅ **Most important industrial type:** **SSFT** (Scraped Surface Freezing Tank) — used for ice cream.
- ✅ **Critical component:** **Scraper blade** — removes ice layer from wall, maintains high $h$.
- ✅ **Plank's Equation:** Used to calculate **freezing time** for slab/cylinder/sphere geometries. Constants: $P$ and $R$ depend on geometry.
- ✅ **Slab constants:** $P = 1/2$, $R = 1/8$. **Sphere constants:** $P = 1/6$, $R = 1/24$.
- ✅ **Heat-load calculation:** Three steps — Sensible (above) + **Latent** + Sensible (below). Skipping any step loses marks.
- ✅ **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — ensure $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$.
- ✅ **Typical operating temperatures:** Refrigerant -25 to -40 °C; Brine -18 to -25 °C; Product exit -15 to -25 °C.
- ✅ **Industry advantage of brine immersion:** Freezing rate is **5–10× faster** than air-blast; minimal cell damage.
- ✅ **Insulation:** **Polyurethane foam (PUF)**, 75–100 mm thick, is standard.
- ✅ **Most common refrigerant:** **Ammonia (R-717)** for large industrial tanks; **R-404A** for smaller units.
- ✅ **Typical heat transfer coefficient (brine-side, agitated):** $h = 500$–$2000 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot$K.
- ✅ **Engineering safety factor:** Always apply **15–25% safety margin** when sizing jacket area.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ice Cans
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ice Cans

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Ice Can** (also called an **ice block can** or **ice mould**) is a thin-walled, rectangular, leak-proof metallic container used in batch-type **block ice plants** to produce solid rectangular blocks of ice by immersion in a brine tank or by direct contact with a refrigerant. Each can is filled with treated water and frozen from the outside inwards, following the principle of **unidirectional thermal conduction** through the metal wall, after which the ice block is extracted by a brief thaw cycle or mechanical dumping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 2):**
> Ice cans are classified under **Secondary Refrigeration & Ice-Making Equipment** in food cold chain infrastructure. They are the **traditional workhorse** of ice production in Indian fisheries, meat markets, and dairy cold rooms.

## 1.2 Standard Block Sizes in India

| Standard Block Mass | Approximate Can Dimensions (L × B × H) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **25 kg** | 380 × 230 × 380 mm | Small retail fish vendors |
| **50 kg** | 500 × 250 × 500 mm | Most common in India |
| **75 kg** | 600 × 280 × 600 mm | Medium fisheries |
| **100 kg** | 700 × 300 × 700 mm | Harbour/wholesale markets |
| **150 kg** | 800 × 350 × 800 mm | Industrial ice supply |

> [!NOTE]
> The most widely used ice can in Indian food industry plants is the **50 kg block ice can**, manufactured to **BIS standard IS 4594** specifications for material thickness and dimensional tolerance.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice Tray" of Industrial Refrigeration

Think of an ice can exactly like the **plastic ice tray in your home refrigerator** — but scaled up massively and made of galvanized iron instead of plastic. Just as your home tray freezes water block-by-block, an ice can does the same thing, except:

- **Home tray**: freezes in 4–6 hours because the walls are thin and the refrigerant is at a moderate temperature.
- **Ice can**: freezes a 50 kg block in **8 to 12 hours** because there is much more water and the freezing front must travel from the outer surface inward through the full thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does freezing take so long?** The thermal conductivity of water is only about **0.6 W/m·K**, while ice is **2.2 W/m·K**. As the freezing front moves inward, it must push heat through an ever-thickening layer of ice — this is why **larger blocks take disproportionately longer** to freeze (Plank's equation governs this).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Density of ice at 0 °C: **ρ_ice = 917 kg/m³**
- Latent heat of fusion of water: **L_f = 334 kJ/kg**
- Specific heat of water: **c_w = 4.187 kJ/kg·K**
- Specific heat of ice: **c_ice = 2.093 kJ/kg·K**
- Thermal conductivity of ice: **k_ice = 2.22 W/m·K**
- Brine temperature for can immersion: **−10 °C to −15 °C**
- Typical can wall thickness (galvanized iron): **1.6 mm to 2.0 mm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Unidirectional Freezing Process

When a filled ice can is immersed in cold brine (or exposed to evaporating refrigerant), heat is removed from the water **exclusively through the side walls** (since the top is open to air and the bottom is insulated). This creates a **planar freezing front** that advances inward symmetrically from all four vertical walls.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Freezing Cycle:

1. **Sensible Cooling Phase (Water from ambient → 0 °C)**
   The bulk water cools from its initial temperature (typically 25–30 °C) down to its freezing point. This phase removes:
   
   $$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T$$

2. **Latent Heat Removal Phase (Phase Change at 0 °C)**
   Once the water reaches 0 °C, the latent heat of fusion must be removed. This is the **dominant energy load**, accounting for approximately **80% of total refrigeration duty**:
   
   $$Q_{latent} = m \cdot L_f$$

3. **Sub-cooling Phase (Ice from 0 °C → final can temperature)**
   The newly formed ice may be further cooled to the brine temperature for improved storage stability:
   
   $$Q_{subcooling} = m \cdot c_{ice} \cdot \Delta T_{ice}$$

4. **Total Heat to be Removed per Can**:
   
   $$Q_{total} = m \cdot c_w \cdot (T_{initial} - 0) + m \cdot L_f + m \cdot c_{ice} \cdot (0 - T_{can})$$

## 2.2 Plank's Equation — The Heart of Ice Can Design

For a slab-shaped block of ice freezing from both sides (top and bottom are adiabatic), the **freezing time** is given by the famous **Plank's Equation**:

$$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f}{k_{ice} \cdot (T_{f} - T_{brine})} \left[ \frac{a \cdot b}{2} + (a+b) \cdot \frac{\delta}{2} + \frac{\delta^2}{4} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{3600} \; [\text{hours}]$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of water/ice (kg/m³)
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $k_{ice}$ = thermal conductivity of ice (W/m·K)
- $T_f$ = freezing point of water = 0 °C
- $T_{brine}$ = brine temperature (°C, negative value)
- $a$ = thickness of ice block (smaller dimension, m)
- $b$ = width of ice block (larger dimension, m)
- $\delta$ = can wall thickness (m)
- **3600** converts seconds to hours

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Plank's Equation matters for KTU exams:** It is the single most-tested formula for any ice-making equipment question. You must memorize the bracketed term — it represents the **equivalent thickness** including the can wall correction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensible cooling load | $Q_s = m \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | Cooling water to 0 °C |
| Latent freezing load | $Q_L = m \cdot L_f$ | kJ | Phase change at 0 °C |
| Total refrigeration per can | $Q_{tot} = Q_s + Q_L + Q_{sub}$ | kJ | Per-cycle energy audit |
| Plank's freezing time | $t = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{k_{ice} \Delta T}\left[\dfrac{ab}{2}+\delta(a+b)+\dfrac{\delta^2}{4}\right]/3600$ | h | Time to freeze one block |
| Can capacity (cans/day) | $n = \dfrac{M_{daily}}{m_{block}}$ | cans/day | Plant throughput |
| Daily ice production | $M_{daily} = n \cdot m_{block}$ | tonnes/day | Plant capacity |
| Specific energy consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{kWh}}{\text{tonne ice}}$ | kWh/T | Performance benchmark |
| Required refrigeration tonnage | $TR = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{3.517 \times t \times 24}$ | TR | Compressor sizing |
| Can surface heat transfer | $q'' = h \cdot (T_{s} - T_{brine})$ | W/m² | Convection at can wall |

> [!TIP]
> **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW = 12,660 kJ/h.** This conversion is mandatory for every KTU refrigeration calculation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ice cans are not obsolete technology — they dominate the Indian cold chain because:

- **CapEx is extremely low** (a 10-tonne/day block ice plant costs a fraction of a flake ice plant of the same capacity).
- **Block ice stores and transports easily** in jute bags or insulated boxes — ideal for **fisheries, milk chilling centres, and vegetable markets**.
- **Block ice has the highest bulk density** (~917 kg/m³) of any ice form, giving maximum cooling per unit storage volume.
- **Long melt time** (~12–24 hours in open air) makes it the preferred ice for **fish landing centres**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Relevance:** The National Centre for Sustainable Aquaculture (NaCSA) and MPEDA recommend block ice from ice cans as the **gold standard** for preserving catch freshness on Indian fishing vessels and landing centres.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Refrigeration Load per Ice Can

Let us derive the total heat that must be removed to freeze one 50 kg block, taking water from 30 °C down to ice at −5 °C (final storage temperature).

### Given Data:
- Mass of water per can: $m = 50$ kg
- Initial water temperature: $T_i = 30$ °C
- Final ice temperature: $T_f = -5$ °C
- $c_w = 4.187$ kJ/kg·K
- $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg
- $c_{ice} = 2.093$ kJ/kg·K

### Step 1: Sensible heat of water (cooling from 30 °C to 0 °C)

$$Q_s = m \cdot c_w \cdot (T_i - 0)$$

$$Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times (30 - 0)$$

$$Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times 30$$

$$Q_s = 6280.5 \; \text{kJ}$$

### Step 2: Latent heat removal (water to ice at 0 °C)

$$Q_L = m \cdot L_f$$

$$Q_L = 50 \times 334$$

$$Q_L = 16{,}700 \; \text{kJ}$$

### Step 3: Sub-cooling of ice (0 °C to −5 °C)

$$Q_{sub} = m \cdot c_{ice} \cdot (0 - T_f)$$

$$Q_{sub} = 50 \times 2.093 \times 5$$

$$Q_{sub} = 523.25 \; \text{kJ}$$

### Step 4: Total heat per can

$$Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_L + Q_{sub}$$

$$Q_{total} = 6280.5 + 16{,}700 + 523.25$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 23{,}503.75 \; \text{kJ} \; \approx \; 23.5 \; \text{MJ per can}}$$

### Step 5: Energy in kWh (for plant energy audit)

$$E = \frac{Q_{total}}{3600}$$

$$E = \frac{23{,}503.75}{3600} = 6.53 \; \text{kWh per 50 kg block}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Energy Consumption** = 6.53 kWh ÷ 0.05 tonne = **130.6 kWh/tonne of ice**. This is an excellent performance benchmark — well-designed brine-immersion ice can plants operate at **80–90 kWh/tonne**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Freezing Time using Plank's Equation

### Given Data for a 50 kg can:
- Dimensions: $L = 0.50$ m, $B = 0.25$ m, $H = 0.50$ m
- Slab thickness (smaller side): $a = 0.125$ m (half-thickness, since freezing from both sides)
- Slab width: $b = 0.125$ m
- Can wall thickness: $\delta = 2$ mm = 0.002 m
- Brine temperature: $T_{brine} = -10$ °C
- $k_{ice} = 2.22$ W/m·K
- $L_f = 334{,}000$ J/kg
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (water at freezing)

### Step 1: Compute temperature difference

$$\Delta T = T_f - T_{brine} = 0 - (-10) = 10 \; \text{K}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the bracketed geometric term

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bracket} &= \frac{ab}{2} + (a+b) \cdot \frac{\delta}{2} + \frac{\delta^2}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each term:

$$ab = 0.125 \times 0.125 = 0.015625 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$\frac{ab}{2} = \frac{0.015625}{2} = 0.0078125 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$a + b = 0.125 + 0.125 = 0.25 \; \text{m}$$

$$(a+b) \cdot \frac{\delta}{2} = 0.25 \times \frac{0.002}{2} = 0.25 \times 0.001 = 0.00025 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$\frac{\delta^2}{4} = \frac{(0.002)^2}{4} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2$$

Sum:

$$\text{Bracket} = 0.0078125 + 0.00025 + 0.000001 = 0.0080635 \; \text{m}^2$$

### Step 3: Plug into Plank's equation

$$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f}{k_{ice} \cdot \Delta T} \cdot \text{Bracket} \cdot \frac{1}{3600}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\rho \cdot L_f = 1000 \times 334{,}000 = 3.34 \times 10^8 \; \text{J/m}^3$$

Compute denominator:

$$k_{ice} \cdot \Delta T = 2.22 \times 10 = 22.2 \; \text{W/m·K}$$

Compute time in seconds:

$$t = \frac{3.34 \times 10^8}{22.2} \times 0.0080635$$

$$t = 1.5045 \times 10^7 \times 0.0080635$$

$$t = 121{,}335 \; \text{seconds}$$

Convert to hours:

$$t = \frac{121{,}335}{3600} = 33.7 \; \text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why is this so long?** The result (33.7 h) is much higher than the real-world 8–12 hours! This is because **Plank's equation neglects natural convection in the water phase** and assumes the entire mass must conduct heat. In practice, brine temperatures lower than −10 °C (e.g., −20 °C with a refrigerant coil in brine) reduce freezing time to **8–10 hours**. The equation is therefore a **design upper-bound estimate**, not a real-time clock.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sizing an Ice Can Plant

| Parameter | Formula | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| Plant capacity | $C$ = 5 tonnes/day | 5,000 kg/day |
| Block mass per can | $m_b$ | 50 kg |
| Number of cans needed per day | $n = C / m_b$ | 100 cans/day |
| Freezing time per can (Plank) | $t_f$ | 10 h (actual) |
| Harvest + refill time per can | $t_h$ | 0.5 h |
| Total cycle time | $T_{cycle} = t_f + t_h$ | 10.5 h |
| Total cans in plant (with 24 h cycle) | $N_{total} = n \cdot (T_{cycle}/24)$ | $100 \times (10.5/24) = 44$ cans |
| Daily refrigeration load | $Q_R = n \cdot Q_{total}$ | $100 \times 23{,}504 = 2.35 \times 10^6$ kJ/day |
| Compressor tonnage | $TR = Q_R / (3.517 \times 24 \times 3600)$ | $\approx 7.7$ TR |
| Brine tank volume | $V_{brine} = N_{total} \times V_{can} \times 1.3$ | $44 \times 0.0625 \times 1.3 \approx 3.6$ m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **V_can** for 50 kg block ≈ 0.05 m³ (since ρ_ice ≈ 917 kg/m³ → 50/917 ≈ 0.0545 m³). The factor **1.3** accounts for void volume and brine circulation space.

## 3.4 Complete Sizing Example (KTU-style Numerical)

**Problem:** Design a 10 tonne/day block ice plant using 50 kg ice cans with brine at −15 °C. Water enters at 25 °C; final ice temperature is −5 °C. Determine: (i) cans frozen per day, (ii) total refrigeration load in kJ/day, (iii) compressor TR required, and (iv) total number of cans in plant if cycle time = 11 hours.

### Solution:

**(i) Cans per day**:
$$n = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ kg/day}}{50 \text{ kg/can}} = 200 \; \text{cans/day}$$

**(ii) Total refrigeration load**:
$$Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times 25 = 5233.75 \; \text{kJ}$$

$$Q_L = 50 \times 334 = 16{,}700 \; \text{kJ}$$

$$Q_{sub} = 50 \times 2.093 \times 5 = 523.25 \; \text{kJ}$$

$$Q_{total} = 22{,}457 \; \text{kJ/can}$$

$$Q_{daily} = 200 \times 22{,}457 = 4.49 \times 10^6 \; \text{kJ/day}$$

**(iii) Compressor TR**:
$$TR = \frac{4.49 \times 10^6}{3.517 \times 24 \times 3600} = \frac{4.49 \times 10^6}{303{,}964.8}$$

$$\boxed{TR \approx 14.77 \approx 15 \; \text{TR}}$$

**(iv) Total cans in plant**:
$$N = 200 \times \frac{11}{24} = 91.67 \approx 92 \; \text{cans}$$

Add 10% spare cans:
$$N_{total} = 92 \times 1.1 \approx 102 \; \text{cans}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructional Anatomy of an Ice Can

The following block diagram illustrates the **structural anatomy** and **material specification** of a typical galvanized-iron ice can used in Indian block ice plants:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOP OPENING - Pouring Spout] --> B[VERTICAL BODY - GI Sheet 1.6 mm]
    B --> C[REINFORCED RIM - 25 mm Flange]
    B --> D[OUTER FLAT SURFACE - Heat Transfer Zone]
    B --> E[CORNER STITCH WELD - Leak Proof Seam]
    B --> F[BOTTOM CLOSURE - Insulated Base]
    F --> G[STEEL SUPPORT SKID - For Forklift Handling]
    
    H[AUXILIARY FEATURES] --> I[SLIGHTLY TAPERED BODY - Easy Block Release]
    H --> J[LIFTING HOOKS - Two Point Suspension]
    H --> K[NUMBER PLATE - Capacity Tag]
    
    style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    style D fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    style E fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
    style F fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000
    style G fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The can body is **slightly tapered** (typically 2–3° outward from top to bottom) so that the frozen ice block releases easily when the can is inverted — no mechanical thawing needed.

## 4.2 Block Ice Plant — Functional Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Treated Water Tank] -->|Filling| IC[Ice Can Bank]
    IC -->|Immersion in Brine| BT[Brine Tank at -15 C]
    BT -->|Refrigerant Cooling| EV[Evaporator Coils]
    EV -->|Suction| COMP[Compressor]
    COMP -->|Discharge| COND[Condenser]
    COND -->|Liquid| EV
    IC -->|Thaw Cycle| EX[Ice Block Extraction]
    EX -->|Storage| IS[Ice Storage Room]
    IS -->|Distribution| MKT[Fisheries and Markets]
    
    style W fill:#dbeafe,color:#000
    style IC fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    style BT fill:#dcfce7,color:#000
    style EV fill:#fee2e2,color:#000
    style COMP fill:#ede9fe,color:#000
    style COND fill:#fce7f3,color:#000
    style EX fill:#e0f2fe,color:#000
    style IS fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    style MKT fill:#dcfce7,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Freezing Front Progression Inside the Can

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TIME ZERO - Water at 25 C throughout] -->|Sensible cooling| B[TIME 30 min - Water at 4 C, supercooled]
    B -->|Latent begins| C[TIME 1 h - Thin ice shell on walls]
    C -->|Front moves inward| D[TIME 4 h - Ice layer 25 percent of width]
    D -->|Front accelerates| E[TIME 8 h - Ice layer 60 percent]
    E -->|Plank governed| F[TIME 10 h - Block 95 percent solid]
    F -->|Subcooling| G[TIME 10.5 h - Ice at -5 C, ready to harvest]
    
    style A fill:#fee2e2,color:#000
    style B fill:#fed7aa,color:#000
    style C fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    style D fill:#d9f99d,color:#000
    style E fill:#86efac,color:#000
    style F fill:#4ade80,color:#000
    style G fill:#22c55e,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** The freezing front velocity is **not constant** — it starts fast (large surface area, small ice layer) and slows down as the front approaches the center. This is exactly what Plank's equation predicts via the $\frac{ab}{2}$ term dominating at the start.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define an ice can. List any two standard sizes of ice cans used in the Indian food industry with their typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**
An ice can is a thin-walled, rectangular, galvanized-iron container used to produce solid block ice by immersion in a brine tank. Standard sizes include:

- **50 kg block** (500 × 250 × 500 mm) — most widely used for fisheries and milk chilling.
- **100 kg block** (700 × 300 × 700 mm) — used in harbour wholesale markets and large cold storages.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State Plank's equation for freezing time of an ice block. What physical parameters does it relate?**

**Model Answer:**
$$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f}{k_{ice} \cdot (T_f - T_{brine})} \left[ \frac{ab}{2} + (a+b) \cdot \frac{\delta}{2} + \frac{\delta^2}{4} \right] \cdot \frac{1}{3600}$$

It relates freezing time to: **(i)** the thermophysical properties of water/ice ($\rho$, $L_f$, $k_{ice}$), **(ii)** the brine temperature depression ($T_f - T_{brine}$), and **(iii)** the geometric dimensions of the can ($a$, $b$, $\delta$).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the total refrigeration load required to freeze a **50 kg block of ice** in a standard ice can, given water enters at 27 °C and the final ice temperature is −5 °C. Clearly show sensible, latent, and sub-cooling contributions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Plank's equation, estimate the freezing time for a 50 kg block ice can with dimensions 500 × 250 × 500 mm, brine temperature of −12 °C, and can wall thickness of 2 mm. Comment on why the calculated time differs from the actual plant operation time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Using $c_w = 4.187$ kJ/kg·K, $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg, $c_{ice} = 2.093$ kJ/kg·K:

- Sensible: $Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times 27 = 5652.45$ kJ
- Latent: $Q_L = 50 \times 334 = 16{,}700$ kJ
- Sub-cooling: $Q_{sub} = 50 \times 2.093 \times 5 = 523.25$ kJ
- **Total: $Q_{total} = 22{,}875.7$ kJ per can**

**Model Solution (b):**
With $a = b = 0.125$ m, $\delta = 0.002$ m, $\Delta T = 12$ K, $k_{ice} = 2.22$ W/m·K, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $L_f = 334{,}000$ J/kg:

- Geometric term = $\frac{(0.125)^2}{2} + (0.25) \cdot \frac{0.002}{2} + \frac{(0.002)^2}{4}$
- Geometric term = $0.0078125 + 0.00025 + 0.000001 = 0.0080635$ m²
- $t = \dfrac{1000 \times 334{,}000}{2.22 \times 12} \times 0.0080635 / 3600$
- $t = \dfrac{3.34 \times 10^8}{26.64} \times 0.0080635 / 3600$
- $t = 1.254 \times 10^7 \times 0.0080635 / 3600 = 101{,}116 / 3600$
- **$t \approx 28.1$ hours (Plank estimate)**

**Comment:** Actual plant time is 8–10 hours because Plank's equation (i) neglects natural convection currents inside the water, (ii) assumes no brine agitation, and (iii) ignores surface heat transfer enhancement at the can–brine interface.

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Design a **5 tonne/day block ice plant** using 50 kg ice cans. Determine the number of cans to be frozen per day, total cans in the plant (assuming a 10-hour cycle), and the daily refrigeration load in kJ. Water at 30 °C is frozen to −5 °C. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat functional flow diagram the operation of a brine-immersion block ice plant. Discuss the role of the thaw cycle and the typical materials used for can construction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- Cans/day: $n = 5000 / 50 = 100$ cans/day
- Cycle time: 10 h → Total cans in plant: $N = 100 \times (10/24) = 41.67 \approx 42$ cans (+10% spare = 47 cans)
- Sensible: $Q_s = 50 \times 4.187 \times 30 = 6280.5$ kJ
- Latent: $Q_L = 50 \times 334 = 16{,}700$ kJ
- Sub-cooling: $Q_{sub} = 50 \times 2.093 \times 5 = 523.25$ kJ
- Per-can total: $Q_{total} = 23{,}503.75$ kJ
- **Daily load: $Q_{daily} = 100 \times 23{,}504 = 2.35 \times 10^6$ kJ/day**

**Model Solution (b):**
Refer to the **functional process flow** in Section 4.2. The operation sequence is:
1. Fill cans with treated water in the filling station.
2. Lower cans into the brine tank at −12 to −15 °C.
3. Freeze for ~10 hours.
4. Lift cans to the **thaw station** — a small auxiliary tank with warm brine (~+10 °C) for 30–60 seconds to release the surface ice film.
5. Invert cans on the dumping table to release the solid block.
6. Transfer blocks via conveyor to the ice storage room.

**Can material:** Galvanized iron (1.6–2.0 mm) is standard. Aluminium cans are used in premium plants for higher thermal conductivity (~3× faster freezing) but at 4× the cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sub-cooling term** in Part (a) costs you **2 marks** — every KTU numerical must account for sensible + latent + sub-cooling.
> 2. **Mixing up $a$ and $b$ in Plank's equation** — remember: $a$ is the **thickness** (smaller dimension / 2) and $b$ is the **width** (larger dimension / 2). Using the full dimension instead of half-thickness is the **most common error**, and it inflates the freezing time by **a factor of 4**.
> 3. **Not converting seconds to hours** in the final step — KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** if your final answer is in seconds.
> 4. **Forgetting the 10% spare can allowance** in plant sizing — this is a **standard KTU convention** for design numericals.
> 5. **Neglecting Plank's equation limitations** — any answer that does not discuss the assumption vs. reality gap will be marked down for lack of engineering judgment.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Ice can** = thin-walled GI container for block ice production; standard size = **50 kg** in India.
- ⭐ Material: **Galvanized Iron (GI) sheet**, 1.6–2.0 mm thick; aluminium is faster but costlier.
- ⭐ Process: **Sensible cooling → Latent freezing → Sub-cooling** (Three explicit phases — never skip any).
- ⭐ Dominant load: **Latent heat** (~80% of total); $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg.
- ⭐ **Plank's equation** governs freezing time — memorize the bracketed geometric term exactly.
- ⭐ Brine temperature: **−10 °C to −15 °C** is standard; lower brine = faster freezing but higher compressor work.
- ⭐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** = 12,660 kJ/h — memorize for all refrigeration calculations.
- ⭐ Specific energy benchmark: **80–90 kWh per tonne of ice** for well-designed plants.
- ⭐ **Tapering** of can body (2–3°) enables block release without mechanical thawing.
- ⭐ Plank's equation is an **upper-bound estimate** — actual plant time is 25–35% of Plank's prediction.
- ⭐ **Cycle time = Freezing time + Harvest time** (typically 10–11 h total per batch).
- ⭐ Spare can allowance = **10%** of working cans (KTU design convention).
- ⭐ Block ice = highest bulk density ice form; ideal for **fisheries, milk chilling, and long-distance transport**.
- ⭐ **IS 4594** governs dimensional and material standards for ice cans in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cold Storage Design for Different Categories of Food Resource (Size and Shape)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cold Storage Design for Different Categories of Food Resource (Size and Shape)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Cold Storage Design** is the systematic engineering process of determining the optimal physical dimensions, thermal capacity, refrigeration load, airflow architecture, and stacking geometry required to preserve a specific category of food resource at a controlled low temperature. The design is intrinsically governed by the **intrinsic thermo-physical properties** of the food — most critically its **size**, **shape**, **density**, **specific heat**, **moisture content**, and **thermal conductivity**.

For the KTU 2024 scheme, "categories of food resource" refers to the classification of foods into families based on their biological origin, perishability kinetics, respiration behavior (for fresh horticultural produce), and post-harvest physiology. These categories include:

| Category Code | Food Family | Typical Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **F-I** | Fresh Fruits & Vegetables (Horticulture) | Apple, Mango, Tomato, Spinach |
| **F-II** | Frozen Foods | Peas, Meat blocks, Ice cream |
| **F-III** | Dairy & Eggs | Milk, Cheese, Butter, Eggs |
| **F-IV** | Meat, Poultry & Fisheries | Chicken, Mutton, Fish, Shrimp |
| **F-V** | Dry Goods & Grains | Wheat, Rice, Pulses, Spices |
| **F-VI** | Processed / Packaged Foods | Canned goods, Ready-to-eat meals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The size and shape of the food product directly govern the **surface-area-to-volume ratio (A/V)**, which in turn dictates the rate of sensible heat removal, the boundary-layer airflow requirement, and the optimal stacking voidage fraction inside the cold room.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are trying to cool a cup of hot tea. Now imagine cooling **200 tiny marbles** of the same total volume of hot tea, spread out on a plate.

- The **single cup** has a small surface touching the air → cools slowly.
- The **200 marbles** have a massive combined surface touching the air → cools almost instantly.

This is exactly what happens in cold storage. A single large frozen meat carcass cools far more slowly than the same mass cut into small steaks. The **shape (geometry)** and **size (characteristic dimension)** of the food dictate how quickly the refrigeration system can pull heat out of the product. Designers must therefore match the **air throw pattern, duct placement, evaporator capacity, and stack height** to the geometry of the product family being stored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why size & shape matter:**
> 1. **Heat Transfer Rate** ∝ Surface Area (A) — smaller pieces release heat faster.
> 2. **Airflow Resistance** ∝ Shape (Drag coefficient) — angular shapes create turbulence.
> 3. **Storage Density** (kg/m³) ∝ Shape (Packing factor) — spheres pack loosely, cuboids pack tightly.
> 4. **Respiration Load** (for fresh produce) ∝ Mass — applies only to living tissues.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat of water**: **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Latent heat of fusion of water**: **333.4 kJ/kg**
- **Standard cold storage temperature for frozen foods**: **−18 °C to −25 °C**
- **Standard chilled storage temperature**: **0 °C to 4 °C**
- **Recommended relative humidity (RH)**: **85–95 %** for fresh produce, **50–60 %** for frozen goods
- **Recommended air velocity inside cold room**: **0.1 to 0.5 m/s**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The design of a cold storage facility for a heterogeneous food resource follows a structured decision hierarchy. Each step depends on the **physical geometry** of the food:

#### Step 1 — Food Categorization & Property Lookup
The first step is to identify the food category and pull its thermo-physical database values:
- Moisture content (**W**, decimal fraction, typically 0.60–0.90)
- Initial temperature (**T₁**)
- Final storage temperature (**T₂**)
- Freezing point (**T_f**, typically −0.5 °C to −2.5 °C)
- Specific heat above freezing (**c_u**)
- Specific heat below freezing (**c_f**)

#### Step 2 — Geometric Characterization
Each food category is approximated as a canonical geometric primitive:
- **Spherical** → eggs, fruits (apple, orange)
- **Cylindrical** → carrots, cucumbers, fish, sausages
- **Cuboidal / Rectangular** → meat carcasses, cheese blocks, packaged cartons
- **Irregular / Amorphous** → leafy greens, whole poultry, fish

The **equivalent spherical diameter (D_eq)** is computed for irregular shapes:

$$ D_{eq} = \left( \frac{6 \cdot V_p}{\pi} \right)^{1/3} $$

where **V_p** is the volume of a single product piece.

#### Step 3 — Surface Area to Volume (A/V) Ratio Determination
The A/V ratio is the **single most critical geometric parameter** for cold storage design:

$$ \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{sphere} = \frac{6}{D} $$

$$ \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{cube} = \frac{6}{a} $$

$$ \left(\frac{A}{V}\right)_{cylinder} = \frac{2}{r} \left( 1 + \frac{r}{L} \right) $$

A **higher A/V ratio** → faster pre-cooling, higher instantaneous refrigeration load, and smaller individual package dimensions.

#### Step 4 — Storage Density & Stacking Geometry
The **bulk storage density (ρ_b)** depends on the packing factor (φ), which is itself a function of shape:

$$ \rho_b = \phi \cdot \rho_s $$

| Food Shape | Packing Factor (φ) | Typical ρ_b (kg/m³) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Spheres (random close packing) | 0.60 – 0.64 | 400 – 600 |
| Cuboids (aligned) | 0.85 – 0.95 | 700 – 900 |
| Cylinders (random) | 0.65 – 0.75 | 500 – 700 |
| Irregular leafy produce | 0.20 – 0.35 | 80 – 200 |

#### Step 5 — Airflow Architecture
Once size and shape are known, the duct/evaporator layout is chosen:
- **Small items** (berries, peas, diced fruit) → **high-velocity cross-flow** (0.3–0.5 m/s)
- **Large items** (carcasses, cheese wheels) → **low-velocity displacement flow** (0.1–0.2 m/s)

#### Step 6 — Cooling Load Calculation
The refrigeration load is calculated segment-wise using the food's moisture content and geometry-driven mass.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Applicable Food Category |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $c_u = 1.673 + 0.025 W$ | Specific heat above freezing | kJ/(kg·K) | All moist foods |
| 2 | $c_f = 1.549 + 0.008 W$ | Specific heat below freezing | kJ/(kg·K) | All moist foods |
| 3 | $Q_s = m \cdot c_u \cdot (T_1 - T_f)$ | Sensible heat (above freezing) | kJ | All categories |
| 4 | $Q_s' = m \cdot c_f \cdot (T_f - T_2)$ | Sensible heat (below freezing) | kJ | Frozen foods |
| 5 | $Q_L = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat of freezing | kJ | Frozen foods |
| 6 | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot q_{CO_2} \cdot h_{resp}$ | Respiration heat load | kW | Fresh produce only |
| 7 | $D_{eq} = (6 V_p / \pi)^{1/3}$ | Equivalent diameter | m | All irregular items |
| 8 | $(A/V) = 6 / D_{eq}$ | Surface area to volume ratio | m⁻¹ | All items |
| 9 | $\rho_b = \phi \cdot \rho_s$ | Bulk density | kg/m³ | All categories |
| 10 | $Nu = C \cdot Re^n \cdot Pr^{1/3}$ | Forced convection correlation | dimensionless | All |
| 11 | $\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat transfer rate | W | All categories |
| 12 | $q_{sensible} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ | Air-side load | W | All categories |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **W** in formulas (1) and (2) is the **decimal moisture content** (e.g., 0.85 for apple, NOT 85 %). Many KTU students lose marks by plugging in 85 instead of 0.85.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design methodology is the foundation for:
- **IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) tunnels** — exploit high A/V of small items
- **Blast freezers** for fish and shrimp — use forced air at high velocity
- **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) cold rooms** — for apples, kiwifruit
- **Hydro-coolers and vacuum coolers** — for leafy vegetables
- **Plate freezers** — for irregular meat blocks

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Refrigeration Load for a Mixed Food Cold Room

**Problem Statement:** A cold storage is to be designed for **10,000 kg of fish** (W = 0.80, ρ_s = 1050 kg/m³) entering at **+10 °C** and to be stored at **−20 °C**. Fish is approximately cylindrical (L = 0.40 m, r = 0.025 m). Calculate the total cooling load per batch.

**Given:**
- Mass m = 10,000 kg
- W = 0.80
- T₁ = 10 °C
- T_f ≈ −1 °C (freezing point of fish)
- T₂ = −20 °C
- ρ_s = 1050 kg/m³
- L_f = 333.4 kJ/kg (approximated to water)
- c_u (above freezing) = 1.673 + 0.025(0.80) = **1.693 kJ/(kg·K)**
- c_f (below freezing) = 1.549 + 0.008(0.80) = **1.5554 kJ/(kg·K)**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat from T₁ to T_f (above freezing zone):**

$$ Q_{s1} = m \cdot c_u \cdot (T_1 - T_f) $$

$$ Q_{s1} = 10000 \cdot 1.693 \cdot (10 - (-1)) $$

$$ Q_{s1} = 10000 \cdot 1.693 \cdot 11 $$

$$ Q_{s1} = 186{,}230 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of freezing (phase change at T_f):**

$$ Q_L = m \cdot L_f $$

$$ Q_L = 10000 \cdot 333.4 $$

$$ Q_L = 3{,}334{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat from T_f to T₂ (below freezing zone):**

$$ Q_{s2} = m \cdot c_f \cdot (T_f - T_2) $$

$$ Q_{s2} = 10000 \cdot 1.5554 \cdot (-1 - (-20)) $$

$$ Q_{s2} = 10000 \cdot 1.5554 \cdot 19 $$

$$ Q_{s2} = 295{,}526 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 4 — Total refrigeration load:**

$$ Q_{total} = Q_{s1} + Q_L + Q_{s2} $$

$$ Q_{total} = 186{,}230 + 3{,}334{,}000 + 295{,}526 $$

$$ Q_{total} = 3{,}815{,}756 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to refrigeration tonnes (1 TR = 13,860 kJ/day or 3.517 kW):**

$$ \dot{Q} = \frac{Q_{total}}{t \cdot 3600} $$

Assuming a pull-down time **t = 8 hours = 28,800 s**:

$$ \dot{Q} = \frac{3{,}815{,}756}{28800} $$

$$ \dot{Q} = 132.49 \text{ kW} $$

Converting to tonnes of refrigeration:

$$ \dot{Q}_{TR} = \frac{132.49}{3.517} = 37.67 \text{ TR} $$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Area to Volume Ratio for an Apple

**Given:** Apple approximated as sphere with diameter D = 0.075 m.

**Step 1 — Surface area of sphere:**

$$ A = \pi D^2 = \pi \cdot (0.075)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Volume of sphere:**

$$ V = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.075)^3}{6} = 2.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — A/V ratio:**

$$ \frac{A}{V} = \frac{0.01767}{2.21 \times 10^{-4}} = 79.96 \text{ m}^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with formula:**

$$ \frac{A}{V} = \frac{6}{D} = \frac{6}{0.075} = 80 \text{ m}^{-1} \quad (\text{Matches!}) $$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bulk Volume Required

**Problem:** Determine the cold room volume required to store 10,000 kg of fish using cylindrical geometry.

**Step 1 — Single fish volume:**

$$ V_p = \pi r^2 L = \pi \cdot (0.025)^2 \cdot 0.40 = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Number of fish:**

$$ N = \frac{m}{\rho_s \cdot V_p} = \frac{10000}{1050 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-4}} = 12{,}123 \text{ fish} $$

**Step 3 — Total solid volume:**

$$ V_{solid} = N \cdot V_p = 12123 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-4} = 9.52 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 4 — Bulk volume with packing factor φ = 0.65 (random cylindrical packing):**

$$ V_{bulk} = \frac{V_{solid}}{\phi} = \frac{9.52}{0.65} = 14.65 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 5 — Add circulation & aisle allowance (factor 1.6 for working cold rooms):**

$$ V_{room} = 1.6 \cdot V_{bulk} = 1.6 \cdot 14.65 = 23.44 \text{ m}^3 $$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Shape Effect on Design

| Parameter | Spherical (Eggs) | Cylindrical (Fish) | Cuboidal (Meat Blocks) | Irregular (Leafy Greens) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A/V ratio (1/m) | **80** | **108** | **24** | **150+** |
| Packing factor φ | 0.62 | 0.65 | 0.90 | 0.25 |
| Storage density ρ_b | 620 | 682 | 945 | 150 |
| Recommended air velocity (m/s) | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.15 | 0.40 |
| Cooling time relative | 1.0 × | 0.85 × | 1.8 × | 0.5 × |
| Stacking method | Trayed | Tiered racks | Block pallet | Crate/loose |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "How does shape influence cold storage design?", always structure the answer around **3 axes**: (1) Heat transfer (A/V), (2) Storage efficiency (φ), (3) Airflow requirement (drag coefficient).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Cold Storage Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Food Category] --> B{Food is Living Tissue?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply Respiration Heat Load]
    B -- No --> D[Compute Sensible + Latent Heat]
    C --> E[Determine Geometric Primitive]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Shape Category}
    F -- G1 --> G1[Sphere - Eggs, Fruits]
    F -- G2 --> G2[Cylinder - Fish, Carrots]
    F -- G3 --> G3[Cuboid - Meat, Cheese]
    F -- G4 --> G4[Irregular - Greens, Poultry]
    G1 --> H[Compute Equivalent Diameter Deq]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Calculate A over V Ratio]
    I --> J{High A over V?}
    J -- Yes --> K[High Velocity Air Cross Flow]
    J -- No --> L[Low Velocity Displacement Flow]
    K --> M[Compute Packing Factor Phi]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Determine Bulk Storage Density]
    N --> O[Calculate Total Cooling Load]
    O --> P[Size Evaporator and Ducts]
    P --> Q[End: Final Cold Room Specification]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

        MF1[Medium Velocity 0.2 to 0.3 m/s] --> MP1[Cross Flow]
    end

```

### 4.3 Schematic — Layered Cooling Load Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Total Cooling Load Qtotal] --> A1[Qs1: Sensible Heat Above Freezing]
    T --> B1[QL: Latent Heat of Freezing]
    T --> C1[Qs2: Sensible Heat Below Freezing]
    T --> D1[Qresp: Respiration Heat]
    T --> E1[Qtrans: Transmission Heat]
    T --> F1[Qequip: Equipment and Lighting Heat]
    T --> G1[Qdoor: Door Opening Heat]
    A1 --> Sum[Sum All Loads in kW]
    B1 --> Sum
    C1 --> Sum
    D1 --> Sum
    E1 --> Sum
    F1 --> Sum
    G1 --> Sum
    Sum --> Final[Refrigeration Capacity in TR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Notice how the total load is **not just** the product heat — it includes the building envelope (transmission), internal equipment, door losses, and (for fresh produce) respiration. KTU frequently tests whether students **ignore these auxiliary loads** in their answers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Equivalent Spherical Diameter" and state its significance in cold storage design of irregular food items.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Equivalent Spherical Diameter (D_eq) of an irregular food item is defined as the diameter of a sphere that has the **same volume** as the irregular item, given by:

$$ D_{eq} = \left( \frac{6 V_p}{\pi} \right)^{1/3} $$

**Significance (2 marks):** It allows the designer to treat an irregular shape as a sphere for heat transfer calculations, enabling the use of standard forced-convection correlations (e.g., Ranz-Marshall correlation) to compute the convective heat transfer coefficient (h). This is essential for sizing the evaporator and estimating cooling time of products like poultry or fish which do not have a clean geometric primitive.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*How does the surface-area-to-volume ratio (A/V) of a food product influence the design of cold storage airflow?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The A/V ratio is a critical geometric parameter:

1. **High A/V** (small items — berries, peas, leafy greens): Faster heat release → requires **higher air velocity (0.3–0.5 m/s)** and turbulent cross-flow to remove heat quickly without causing product dehydration. **(1 mark)**
2. **Low A/V** (large items — meat carcasses, cheese wheels): Slower heat release → requires **lower air velocity (0.1–0.2 m/s)** to avoid surface freezing while interior is still warm. Displacement flow is preferred. **(1 mark)**
3. The A/V ratio also dictates the **specific refrigeration load per unit volume** stored and influences the selection of evaporator coil face area. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain in detail how the **size and shape** of food resources influence the cold storage design parameters. Discuss the effect on **surface-area-to-volume ratio**, **packing factor**, and **airflow velocity selection** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage is to be designed for **5,000 kg of apples** entering at **+25 °C** to be cooled and stored at **+2 °C**. Given:
- Average apple diameter D = 0.075 m
- Density ρ_s = 840 kg/m³
- Specific heat c_p = 3.81 kJ/(kg·K)
- Freezing point T_f = −1.5 °C (apples will not freeze at +2 °C)

Calculate:
- (i) Total refrigeration load in kJ
- (ii) Required refrigeration capacity in TR if the cooling must be completed in **6 hours**
- (iii) Bulk volume of the cold room (packing factor φ = 0.62) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The size and shape of a food product influence cold storage design through three primary mechanisms:

**1. Surface-Area-to-Volume Ratio (A/V):**
The A/V ratio governs the rate of convective heat removal. For a sphere:

$$ \left(\frac{A}{V}\right) = \frac{6}{D} $$

Smaller items (smaller D) have higher A/V → faster cooling. Example: A diced carrot (D = 0.01 m, A/V = 600 m⁻¹) cools far faster than a whole carrot (D = 0.03 m, A/V = 200 m⁻¹). Designers use this to choose between IQF tunnels (for small items) and blast freezers (for large items). **(2 marks)**

**2. Packing Factor (φ) and Storage Density:**
The shape determines how tightly items pack inside the cold room. Cuboids pack efficiently (φ = 0.90), spheres loosely (φ = 0.62), and irregular leafy produce very loosely (φ = 0.20–0.30). This directly affects the **room volume** required per kg of product. **(2 marks)**

**3. Airflow Velocity Selection:**
Airflow velocity must be matched to A/V:
- **High A/V → High velocity (0.3–0.5 m/s)** → cross-flow / turbulent flow
- **Low A/V → Low velocity (0.1–0.2 m/s)** → displacement flow to prevent surface overcooling

Examples: Berries (high A/V) → IQF tunnel with 0.5 m/s; Beef carcass (low A/V) → chilled holding room at 0.15 m/s. **(2 marks)**

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Shape and size are not just packaging concerns — they are fundamental thermodynamic inputs that drive the entire refrigeration system design.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Total Refrigeration Load:**

Since the apple is cooled from +25 °C to +2 °C (above freezing point), only sensible heat is involved:

$$ Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T $$

$$ Q = 5000 \cdot 3.81 \cdot (25 - 2) $$

$$ Q = 5000 \cdot 3.81 \cdot 23 $$

$$ Q = 438{,}150 \text{ kJ} $$

**(ii) Refrigeration Capacity:**

$$ \dot{Q} = \frac{Q}{t} = \frac{438150}{6 \cdot 3600} = 20.28 \text{ kW} $$

Converting to TR (1 TR = 3.517 kW):

$$ \dot{Q}_{TR} = \frac{20.28}{3.517} = 5.77 \text{ TR} $$

Adding **20% safety margin** for transmission, door, and respiration loads:

$$ \dot{Q}_{design} = 5.77 \cdot 1.20 = 6.92 \text{ TR} \approx 7 \text{ TR} $$

**(iii) Bulk Volume:**

Volume of one apple:

$$ V_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.075)^3}{6} = 2.21 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

Number of apples:

$$ N = \frac{m}{\rho_s \cdot V_p} = \frac{5000}{840 \cdot 2.21 \times 10^{-4}} = 26{,}937 \text{ apples} $$

Total solid volume:

$$ V_{solid} = 26937 \cdot 2.21 \times 10^{-4} = 5.95 \text{ m}^3 $$

Bulk volume:

$$ V_{bulk} = \frac{V_{solid}}{\phi} = \frac{5.95}{0.62} = 9.60 \text{ m}^3 $$

Cold room volume (with 1.6 aisle factor):

$$ V_{room} = 1.6 \cdot 9.60 = 15.36 \text{ m}^3 $$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]*

**(a)** Discuss the classification of food resources for cold storage design based on their **biological activity and thermal properties**. Provide a comparative table for at least four categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blast freezer handles **2,000 kg of fish** (W = 0.78) entering at **+5 °C** to be frozen to **−25 °C**. Fish is treated as cylindrical (L = 0.30 m, r = 0.02 m, ρ_s = 1060 kg/m³). Calculate:
- (i) Sensible heat above freezing (T_f = −1 °C)
- (ii) Latent heat of freezing
- (iii) Sensible heat below freezing
- (iv) Total refrigeration load and required capacity in TR (assume 4-hour pull-down) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Food resources are classified for cold storage design based on:
1. **Biological activity** (living vs non-living tissue)
2. **Moisture content** (driving freezing behavior)
3. **Storage temperature regime** (chilled vs frozen)
4. **Shape & bulk density**

| Category | Living Tissue? | Moisture W | Storage Temp | Typical Shape | Design Air Velocity |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Fresh Fruits (F-I) | Yes | 0.80–0.90 | 0 to 4 °C | Spherical | 0.2 m/s |
| Meat & Poultry (F-IV) | No (post-slaughter) | 0.65–0.75 | 0 to −25 °C | Irregular/Cuboid | 0.3 m/s |
| Fish (F-IV) | No | 0.70–0.82 | 0 to −25 °C | Cylindrical | 0.3 m/s |
| Frozen Vegetables (F-II) | No (blanched) | 0.80–0.90 | −18 to −25 °C | Irregular/diced | 0.4 m/s |

The key design implication is that living-tissue foods (F-I) carry a continuous **respiration load** (q_CO2 respiration) that non-living categories do not. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- m = 2,000 kg, W = 0.78, T₁ = +5 °C, T_f = −1 °C, T₂ = −25 °C
- c_u = 1.673 + 0.025(0.78) = **1.6925 kJ/(kg·K)**
- c_f = 1.549 + 0.008(0.78) = **1.5552 kJ/(kg·K)**
- L_f ≈ 333.4 kJ/kg (water-equivalent)

**(i) Sensible heat above freezing:**

$$ Q_{s1} = m \cdot c_u \cdot (T_1 - T_f) = 2000 \cdot 1.6925 \cdot 6 = 20{,}310 \text{ kJ} $$

**(ii) Latent heat of freezing:**

$$ Q_L = m \cdot L_f = 2000 \cdot 333.4 = 666{,}800 \text{ kJ} $$

**(iii) Sensible heat below freezing:**

$$ Q_{s2} = m \cdot c_f \cdot (T_f - T_2) = 2000 \cdot 1.5552 \cdot 24 = 74{,}650 \text{ kJ} $$

**(iv) Total load and capacity:**

$$ Q_{total} = 20310 + 666800 + 74650 = 761{,}760 \text{ kJ} $$

Time t = 4 h = 14,400 s

$$ \dot{Q} = \frac{761760}{14400} = 52.90 \text{ kW} $$

$$ \dot{Q}_{TR} = \frac{52.90}{3.517} = 15.04 \text{ TR} $$

With 25% safety factor (transmission + infiltration + equipment):

$$ \dot{Q}_{design} = 15.04 \cdot 1.25 = 18.80 \text{ TR} \approx 19 \text{ TR} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Moisture Content Misuse:** Plugging in W = 78 instead of W = 0.78 into the specific heat formula. This single error shifts c_u by an order of magnitude. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Ignoring the 3-zone cooling:** Many students only compute Q_L (latent heat) and forget that sensible heat exists on BOTH sides of T_f. The total load for freezing is **3-term sum**, not just latent. **(Loses 3–4 marks)**
> 3. **No safety factor:** KTU expects design capacity to include 20–25 % margin over calculated load. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 4. **Unit mistakes:** Mixing kJ/s with kW, or TR with kW without conversion (1 TR = 3.517 kW = 13,860 kJ/day). **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting packing factor φ:** Computing room volume using solid volume alone — underestimates room size by 30–60 %. **(Loses 2 marks)**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cold storage design is geometry-driven** — size and shape are not packaging concerns, they are thermodynamic inputs.
- **A/V ratio is the master geometric parameter** — dictates cooling rate, air velocity, and equipment selection.
- **Equivalent Spherical Diameter (D_eq)** unifies irregular shapes for heat transfer analysis.
- **Specific heat formulas:**
  - Above freezing: c_u = 1.673 + 0.025 W
  - Below freezing: c_f = 1.549 + 0.008 W
- **Total refrigeration load = 3-zone sum** for frozen foods: Q_s1 (above T_f) + Q_L (latent) + Q_s2 (below T_f).
- **For chilled foods (no freezing):** Only sensible heat: Q = m·c_p·ΔT.
- **Respiration heat** applies ONLY to living tissues (fresh fruits/vegetables) — NOT to meat, fish, dairy.
- **Packing factor φ** depends on shape: cuboids > cylinders > spheres > irregular leafy.
- **Air velocity selection:** High A/V → high velocity (0.3–0.5 m/s); Low A/V → low velocity (0.1–0.2 m/s).
- **Always add 20–25 % safety margin** to the calculated refrigeration load.
- **Conversion factors:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 13,860 kJ/day.
- **Room volume = (Solid volume / φ) × 1.6 (aisle/working factor)**.
- **Bulk density ρ_b = φ × ρ_s** governs storage capacity and load per unit volume.
- **KTU frequently tests** the comparative effect of shape on A/V, packing factor, and airflow in a single 7-mark sub-question — always answer along these three axes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction and Material
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Construction and Material

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Construction** in the context of Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC) systems for the food industry refers to the **physical architecture, geometric configuration, and assembly methodology** of the major system components — namely the compressor, condenser, expansion device, and evaporator — together with their associated auxiliaries (receivers, accumulators, oil separators, driers, and sight glasses).

**Materials** in this domain denote the **engineering substances** (metals, alloys, polymers, and composites) selected for the manufacture of these components and for the thermal insulation envelope of cold storage rooms, process piping, and refrigerated transport vehicles. The selection is governed by **thermophysical properties, mechanical strength, chemical compatibility with refrigerants and food products, hygienic suitability (sanitary grade), and long-term durability** under cyclic thermal loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the food industry, material selection is **not merely a mechanical or thermal decision** — it is also a **food safety decision**. The U.S. FDA (Food and Drug Administration), FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India), and EU Regulation (EC) No 1935/2004 mandate that all surfaces in contact with food must be **non-toxic, non-absorbent, non-corroding, and easily cleanable** (CIP — Clean-In-Place capability).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Refrigerator = House" Model

Think of a refrigeration system as a **house built for cold**:

| Refrigeration Component | House Analogy | Material Analogy |
|------------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Compressor** | The heart / pump that circulates blood (refrigerant) | Cast iron / forged steel body |
| **Condenser** | The radiator that rejects heat to the outside | Copper tubes + aluminum fins |
| **Expansion valve** | The narrow bottleneck / nozzle | Brass / stainless steel body |
| **Evaporator** | The cooling coil inside rooms | Copper / aluminum tubes with fins |
| **Insulation walls** | The thick brick walls of a cold house | Polyurethane foam (PUF) |
| **Piping** | The plumbing network | Copper / steel (with insulation wrap) |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a poorly built house leaks heat in winter, a poorly constructed refrigerator **leaks cold** — driving up compressor work and energy bills. The **construction** determines structural integrity, while the **material** determines thermal efficiency and food safety.

## 1.3 Why "Construction and Material" is a Standalone Module in KTU 2024

The KTU 2024 syllabus isolates this topic because:

1. **Food preservation failure modes** (spoilage, microbial growth) are directly tied to **temperature uniformity**, which depends on **evaporator coil geometry and air-flow distribution**.
2. **Energy efficiency** of the cold chain (a major cost factor in food industry logistics) is governed by **insulation material** and **heat exchanger surface area**.
3. **Equipment lifespan** in food plants (often 15–25 years) is determined by **material resistance to corrosion** from brine, cleaning chemicals, and humid environments.
4. **Refrigerant migration** regulations (F-Gas Regulation, Kigali Amendment, Montreal Protocol) require **material compatibility audits** for low-GWP refrigerants like R-290, R-744, and HFOs.

The **standard reference temperature** for cold storage design in food industry is **$-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+10^{\circ}\text{C}$** depending on the product category (frozen vs. chilled).

---

## 1.4 Standardized Metrics and Constants

The following benchmark values recur in KTU problems:

- **Thermal conductivity of copper**: $k_{\text{Cu}} = 385 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ at $27^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Thermal conductivity of aluminum**: $k_{\text{Al}} = 205 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ at $27^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Thermal conductivity of stainless steel (304)**: $k_{\text{SS}} = 16.2 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- **Thermal conductivity of Polyurethane Foam (PUF)**: $k_{\text{PUF}} = 0.020$ to $0.025 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- **Thermal conductivity of Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)**: $k_{\text{EPS}} = 0.033$ to $0.038 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- **Thermal conductivity of Cork**: $k_{\text{cork}} = 0.040 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4\text{)}$
- **Standard cold room wall surface emissivity** (galvanized steel): $\varepsilon \approx 0.28$

> [!NOTE]
> PUF has the **lowest thermal conductivity** among common insulation materials, which is why it is the **industry standard** for modern cold storage construction. Its closed-cell structure also provides a **built-in vapor barrier**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Constructional Analysis

### 2.1.1 Compressors — Three Casing Architectures

**A. Hermetic Compressors**
- Refrigerant **never leaves** the sealed shell; the motor and compressor share one welded steel shell.
- Used in: domestic refrigerators, small commercial freezers, refrigerated display units.
- Material: **Cold-rolled steel shell**, **cast iron cylinder block**, **aluminum pistons** (lightweight), **copper windings** with Class F/H insulation.
- **Advantage**: Zero leakage, no shaft seal, low maintenance.
- **Disadvantage**: Motor burnout is catastrophic — the entire compressor is replaced (throwaway philosophy).

**B. Semi-Hermetic Compressors**
- Motor and compressor in one housing, but the housing is **bolted together** (not welded). Serviceable.
- Used in: medium-capacity commercial systems (5–100 TR).
- Material: **Cast iron cylinder head + body**, **steel bolts**, **PTFE (Teflon) gaskets** for refrigerant sealing.
- **Advantage**: Field-repairable; can replace motor windings, valves, bearings.

**C. Open-Type Compressors**
- Motor and compressor are **separate units** coupled via a shaft and belt (or direct coupling).
- Used in: large industrial ammonia (R-717) plants, food processing chillers (>100 TR).
- Material: **Cast iron crankcase**, **forged steel shaft**, **carbon-graphite or ceramic seals** at shaft penetration.
- **Disadvantage**: Shaft seal is a chronic leakage point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked "Why is hermetic construction preferred in food retail refrigeration?" the model answer should mention: *food retail outlets (supermarkets, dairies) require leak-free systems to protect workers and food products from refrigerant exposure, and hermetic seals eliminate shaft-seal leakage.*

### 2.1.2 Condensers — Heat-Rejecting Heat Exchangers

**A. Air-Cooled Condensers (ACC)**
- Construction: **Copper tubes** (typically 9.52 mm OD × 0.7 mm wall) mechanically expanded into **aluminum fins** (0.1–0.2 mm thick, spaced 8–14 fins/inch).
- Tube material: **DHP copper (Deoxidized High Phosphorus, C12200)** for excellent brazing and refrigerant compatibility.
- Fin material: **Aluminum alloy 1100 or 3003** (corrosion-resistant, lightweight).
- Used in: small to medium systems, rooftop units, split ACs.

**B. Water-Cooled Shell-and-Tube Condensers**
- Construction: **Carbon steel shell** with **copper or cupro-nickel tubes (90/10 or 70/30)**. Water flows through tubes; refrigerant condenses on the shell side.
- Baffles (segmental): direct water flow, enhance turbulence.
- Material logic: **Cupro-nickel** resists chloride-induced pitting in cooling tower water.

**C. Evaporative Condensers**
- Construction: A **finned coil** (same materials as ACC) with **water spray** over the coil, plus an **axial fan** to induce air-flow.
- Combined sensible + latent heat rejection — most efficient in dry climates.

### 2.1.3 Evaporators — Heat-Absorbing Heat Exchangers

**A. Direct Expansion (DX) Finned-Tube Evaporators**
- Construction: Copper tubes with **aluminum fins** (sometimes **blue-fin epoxy-coated** for corrosion resistance in cold rooms with high humidity).
- Used in: cold rooms, display cabinets, blast freezers.

**B. Flooded Shell-and-Tube Evaporators**
- Construction: **Steel shell** with **copper tubes**; refrigerant floods the shell; liquid level maintained by a low-pressure float.
- Higher heat transfer coefficient than DX (due to phase change on shell side).

**C. Plate Evaporators (Brazed Plate Heat Exchangers — BPHE)**
- Construction: Stamped **stainless steel plates** (typically 316L) vacuum-brazed together.
- Compact, high efficiency, used in **plate freezers** and **chilled water systems** for food processing.

> [!NOTE]
> In food industry applications, **stainless steel 304 / 316L** is mandatory for any surface contacting the product (e.g., plate freezer surfaces, milk chillers). The chromium oxide passive layer provides **self-healing corrosion resistance**.

### 2.1.4 Expansion Devices

- **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV / TEV)**: **Brass body**, **stainless steel diaphragm**, **copper capillary tube** with a sensing bulb charged with refrigerant.
- **Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV)**: Maintains constant evaporator pressure; older design.
- **Capillary Tube**: A **drawn copper tube** of precise bore (0.5–2.0 mm), length 1–6 m, acts as a fixed restriction.
- **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)**: **Stainless steel needle** driven by a **stepper motor**; used in modern precision food chilling systems.

### 2.1.5 Insulation and Envelope Construction (Cold Rooms)

The standard cold room **sandwich panel** construction:
- **Outer skin**: 0.5–0.8 mm **pre-coated galvanized steel (PPGI)** or **aluminum sheet**.
- **Core**: 50–200 mm **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** continuously foamed between skins.
- **Inner skin**: 0.5–0.6 mm **stainless steel (304)** for food-grade applications, or **galvanized steel with food-safe epoxy coating**.
- **Joint**: **Cam-lock** or **sliding tongue-and-groove** with EPDM gasket seal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vapor barrier** must be on the **warm side** of the insulation. In a cold room ($T_{\text{room}} \approx -18^{\circ}\text{C}$), the warm side is the exterior. PUF's closed-cell structure inherently provides this barrier — a key reason it dominates over mineral wool or fiberglass, which require a separate vapor barrier (polyethylene film).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for this module. Every KTU 2024 question on construction and material will reduce to one or more of these equations.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ (Fourier's Law) | $k$: thermal conductivity, $A$: area, $\Delta T$: temp. diff., $L$: wall thickness | Steady-state heat conduction through insulation wall |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ (Thermal resistance network) | $h_i$, $h_o$: convective HTCs, $L/k$: conduction resistance | Overall heat transfer coefficient of wall |
| 3 | $r_2 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = \dfrac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{q}$ (Cylindrical wall) | $r_1$, $r_2$: inner/outer radii, $q$: heat flux per unit length | Heat loss through insulated pipe |
| 4 | $\text{UA} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T_{\text{lm}}$ (Effectiveness-NTU) | $\dot{m}$: mass flow, $c_p$: specific heat, $\Delta T_{\text{lm}}$: LMTD | Heat exchanger sizing |
| 5 | $t_{\text{ins}} = \dfrac{k_i (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})}{q_{\text{allow}}} - \dfrac{k_w L_w}{...}$ | $t_{\text{ins}}$: insulation thickness | Determining critical insulation thickness |
| 6 | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ | $\varepsilon$: emissivity, $\sigma$: Stefan-Boltzmann, $T$ in K | Radiation heat gain on outer wall of cold room |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{total}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{conduction}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{infiltration}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{product load}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{misc}}$ | Heat gains of cold room | Total cooling load estimation |
| 8 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{comp}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | Energy efficiency benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition** for most KTU cold-room problems: The external ambient is taken as **$T_{\text{amb}} = +35^{\circ}\text{C}$** (Indian summer design condition) unless otherwise specified, and the internal cold room temperature is **$T_{\text{room}} = -18^{\circ}\text{C}$** (frozen food storage) or **$+4^{\circ}\text{C}$** (chilled storage).

---

## 2.3 Why Construction and Material Matter in Real Engineering

1. **Energy cost**: A well-insulated cold room (PUF, 100 mm) reduces compressor running hours by **30–40%** compared to a poorly insulated one (50 mm EPS).
2. **Food safety**: Smooth **stainless steel (Ra ≤ 0.8 µm)** surfaces inside cold rooms prevent bacterial harborage (Listeria, Salmonella).
3. **Refrigerant leakage prevention**: Material compatibility testing (per **ASHRAE 15** and **ISO 17557**) prevents catastrophic failures when new refrigerants are introduced.
4. **Lifecycle cost**: Stainless steel components have a **2–3× higher initial cost** than carbon steel but a **4–5× longer service life** in corrosive food processing environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Loss Through a Composite Cold Room Wall

**Problem Statement**: A cold storage wall consists of three layers:
- Outer layer: 6 mm **pre-coated galvanized steel** ($k_1 = 52 \text{ W/(m·K)}$)
- Middle layer: 100 mm **PUF insulation** ($k_2 = 0.022 \text{ W/(m·K)}$)
- Inner layer: 4 mm **stainless steel (304)** ($k_3 = 16.2 \text{ W/(m·K)}$)

If the inside air temperature is $T_i = -18^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the outside ambient is $T_o = +35^{\circ}\text{C}$, with inside air HTC $h_i = 9 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ and outside air HTC $h_o = 12 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ (forced convection on outside), compute:
(a) The total thermal resistance per m² ($R_{\text{tot}}$).
(b) The heat flux $q$ (W/m²).
(c) The temperature at the **outer surface of the PUF** (interface between PUF and inner SS skin).

### Step 1 — Identify the Thermal Resistance Network

For a composite wall with surface convection, the thermal circuit is a series of five resistances:

$$R_{\text{tot}} = R_{h,i} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{h,o}$$

where $R_{h,i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i}$, $R_{h,o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o}$, and $R_n = \dfrac{L_n}{k_n}$.

### Step 2 — Compute Each Resistance

**Inner surface convective resistance**:
$$R_{h,i} = \frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{9} = 0.1111 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Outer GI steel layer resistance**:
$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.006}{52} = 0.000115 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**PUF insulation resistance**:
$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.100}{0.022} = 4.5455 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Inner SS layer resistance**:
$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.004}{16.2} = 0.000247 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Outer surface convective resistance**:
$$R_{h,o} = \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

### Step 3 — Sum All Resistances

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{tot}} &= 0.1111 + 0.000115 + 4.5455 + 0.000247 + 0.0833 \\
R_{\text{tot}} &= 4.7403 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Heat Flux

Using Ohm's-law analogy for heat transfer, $q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{tot}}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{T_o - T_i}{R_{\text{tot}}} \\
q &= \frac{35 - (-18)}{4.7403} \\
q &= \frac{53}{4.7403} \\
q &= 11.18 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The PUF layer accounts for **$\dfrac{4.5455}{4.7403} \times 100\% = 95.9\%$** of the total thermal resistance. This is a classic KTU result — the **insulation dominates** the heat transfer behavior, and small miscalculations in insulation thickness or conductivity disproportionately affect energy cost.

### Step 5 — Compute Interface Temperature

The temperature drop across the inner SS layer:

$$\Delta T_3 = q \times R_3 = 11.18 \times 0.000247 = 0.00276 \text{ K}$$

The temperature at the SS outer surface (i.e., at the PUF/SS interface) is:

$$T_{\text{interface}} = T_i + q(R_{h,i} + R_3)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{interface}} &= -18 + 11.18 \times (0.1111 + 0.000247) \\
T_{\text{interface}} &= -18 + 11.18 \times 0.11135 \\
T_{\text{interface}} &= -18 + 1.245 \\
T_{\text{interface}} &= -16.755^{\circ}\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Sanity Check

Compute the total temperature drop across the wall:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{\text{across PUF}} &= q \times R_2 = 11.18 \times 4.5455 = 50.82 \text{ K} \\
\Delta T_{\text{convection,outside}} &= q \times R_{h,o} = 11.18 \times 0.0833 = 0.931 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sum**: $1.245 + 0.00276 + 50.82 + 0.931 \approx 53 \text{ K}$. ✓ Matches the imposed $T_o - T_i = 53 \text{ K}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Insulation Thickness on a Refrigerant Pipe

**Problem Statement**: An ammonia (R-717) refrigerant pipe of **outer diameter 60 mm** carries refrigerant at $T_s = -10^{\circ}\text{C}$. It is insulated with mineral wool ($k_{\text{ins}} = 0.040 \text{ W/(m·K)}$). The ambient air is at $T_\infty = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$ with $h_o = 8 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$. Find the **critical radius of insulation** $r_c$ and the heat loss per metre at $r = r_c$.

### Step 1 — Critical Radius Formula

The heat loss per unit length from an insulated cylinder is:

$$q_L = \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{\text{ins}}} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_2 h_o}}$$

Setting $\dfrac{d q_L}{d r_2} = 0$ to find the extremum, one obtains the well-known **critical radius of insulation**:

$$r_c = \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $r_c$

$$\begin{aligned}
r_c &= \frac{k_{\text{ins}}}{h_o} \\
r_c &= \frac{0.040}{8} \\
r_c &= 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Pipe Geometry and Critical Thickness

The bare pipe outer radius is $r_1 = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$.

Since $r_1 = 30 \text{ mm} > r_c = 5 \text{ mm}$, the bare pipe is **beyond the critical radius**. Therefore, adding insulation will **immediately reduce** heat loss — there is **no heat-loss penalty** at small thicknesses. (This is the case for all practical metallic pipes in air.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question**: "Adding insulation always reduces heat loss — true or false?" **False** for low-conductivity cylinders in low-$h$ environments. For example, a small electrical wire ($r_1 = 1$ mm) in still air ($h_o = 5 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$) using insulation with $k = 0.04$ has $r_c = 8$ mm. Adding a thin layer of insulation actually **increases** heat loss because the increased surface area dominates over the conductive resistance added.

### Step 4 — Heat Loss at $r = r_c$
