For this case, since $r_1 \gg r_c$, the optimum is to insulate to a reasonable thickness. Let us compute heat loss for an insulation thickness of 40 mm ($r_2 = 30 + 40 = 70$ mm = 0.070 m):

$$\begin{aligned}
q_L &= \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{\dfrac{\ln(0.070/0.030)}{2\pi \times 0.040} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 0.070 \times 8}} \\
q_L &= \frac{30 - (-10)}{0.847 + 0.284} \\
q_L &= \frac{40}{1.131} \\
q_L &= 35.4 \text{ W/m}
\end{aligned}$$

(Intermediate values: $\ln(0.070/0.030) = \ln(2.333) = 0.847$; $\dfrac{0.847}{2\pi \times 0.040} = 3.37$... let me recompute carefully.)

Re-computation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Conduction term} &= \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k} = \frac{0.847}{2\pi \times 0.040} = \frac{0.847}{0.2513} = 3.370 \text{ m·K/W} \\
\text{Convection term} &= \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 h_o} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.070 \times 8} = \frac{1}{3.519} = 0.284 \text{ m·K/W} \\
q_L &= \frac{40}{3.370 + 0.284} = \frac{40}{3.654} = 10.95 \text{ W/m}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The corrected value is **$q_L \approx 10.95 \text{ W/m}$** per metre of pipe length. This is the heat that must be removed by the refrigeration system to maintain the pipe temperature — a **line-load** term in cold room design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Estimating Insulation Thickness for a Cold Room

**Problem Statement**: Design the **PUF thickness** for the wall of a blast freezer wall such that the heat flux does not exceed $q_{\max} = 8 \text{ W/m}^2$. Given: $T_o = 35^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_i = -35^{\circ}\text{C}$, $h_i = 6 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ (still air inside), $h_o = 20 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ (forced air outside), $k_{\text{PUF}} = 0.022 \text{ W/(m·K)}$. Neglect the metal skins (they contribute < 1% of resistance).

### Step 1 — Setup

Since metal skins are neglected:

$$q = \frac{T_o - T_i}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{L}{k_{\text{PUF}}} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $L$

Rearranging for $L$:

$$\frac{L}{k_{\text{PUF}}} = \frac{T_o - T_i}{q} - \frac{1}{h_i} - \frac{1}{h_o}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{L}{k_{\text{PUF}}} &= \frac{35 - (-35)}{8} - \frac{1}{6} - \frac{1}{20} \\
\frac{L}{k_{\text{PUF}}} &= \frac{70}{8} - 0.1667 - 0.05 \\
\frac{L}{k_{\text{PUF}}} &= 8.75 - 0.2167 \\
\frac{L}{k_{\text{PUF}}} &= 8.533 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $L$

$$L = 8.533 \times k_{\text{PUF}} = 8.533 \times 0.022 = 0.1877 \text{ m} \approx 188 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4 — Practical Design Decision

Standard PUF panel thicknesses are 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 200 mm. We select **$L = 200 \text{ mm}$** (next standard size up) to provide a safety margin.

Recomputing $q$ at $L = 0.200$ m:

$$\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{70}{0.1667 + \dfrac{0.200}{0.022} + 0.05} \\
q &= \frac{70}{0.1667 + 9.0909 + 0.05} \\
q &= \frac{70}{9.308} \\
q &= 7.52 \text{ W/m}^2 \;\; < 8 \text{ W/m}^2 \;\; ✓
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Summary of Derived Engineering Decisions

| Quantity | Derived Value | Engineering Decision |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Heat flux through 100 mm PUF wall | $q = 11.18 \text{ W/m}^2$ | Acceptable for chilled storage |
| Critical insulation radius (ammonia pipe) | $r_c = 5$ mm | Pipe radius 30 mm > $r_c$ → safe to insulate |
| PUF thickness for blast freezer | $L = 188$ mm (design: 200 mm) | Use 200 mm standard panel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hermetic Reciprocating Compressor Construction

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **internal functional architecture** of a hermetic reciprocating compressor used in commercial food refrigeration.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Supply Terminal Block]:::elec --> B[Overload Protector]:::elec
    B --> C[Internal Motor Windings Copper Class F]:::elec
    C --> D[Crankshaft Forged Steel]:::mech
    D --> E[Connecting Rod Aluminum Alloy]:::mech
    E --> F[Piston Aluminum or Cast Iron]:::mech
    F --> G[Cylinder Bore Cast Iron]:::mech
    G --> H[Suction Reed Valve Stainless Steel]:::mech
    H --> I[Discharge Reed Valve Stainless Steel]:::mech
    I --> J[Discharge Silencer Chamber]:::mech
    J --> K[Process Tube Outlet]:::mech
    G --> L[Oil Sump POE Oil 320 cc]:::aux
    L --> M[Oil Pickup Tube]:::aux
    M --> D
    A --> N[Welded Steel Shell 4 mm Cold Rolled]:::shell
    N -.houses.-> C
    N -.houses.-> D
    N -.houses.-> G
    O[Suction Line Accumulator]:::aux --> G
    P[Vibration Mounts Rubber]:::aux --> N

    classDef elec fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef shell fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram**: The electrical path is **gold**; the mechanical compression path is **silver**; the auxiliaries are **green**; the outer shell is **orange**. Note that the motor and compression mechanism share the same welded shell — this is what makes the compressor **hermetic** (leak-proof by design).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Shell-and-Tube Condenser Construction

The following diagram illustrates the **functional layout** of a water-cooled shell-and-tube condenser commonly used in dairy and beverage chilling plants.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Refrigerant Inlet Vapor from Compressor] --> B[Top Header Dished End]:::shell
    B --> C[Tube Bundle Copper 7/8 in OD 24 tubes]:::tube
    C --> D[Segmental Baffles 25 percent cut]:::baffle
    D --> E[Bottom Header Dished End]:::shell
    E --> F[Condensate Outlet Liquid Receiver]:::tank
    G[Cooling Water Inlet 30 C] --> E
    E --> H[Water Passes Through Tubes Counter Flow]
    H --> B
    B --> I[Cooling Water Outlet 38 C]
    J[Refrigerant Shell Side Level] -.indicates.-> F
    K[Pressure Relief Valve] --> B
    L[Purge Valve for Non Condensables] --> B
    M[Drain Plug] --> F

    classDef shell fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef tube fill:#CD7F32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef baffle fill:#778899,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef tank fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

**Engineering note**: The **counter-flow** arrangement (water enters from the bottom, refrigerant vapor enters from the top) maximizes the **log-mean temperature difference (LMTD)**, which is the key driver of heat exchanger effectiveness.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cold Room Sandwich Panel Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Ambient 35 C and 60 percent RH] --> B[Outer Skin 0.6 mm PPGI Pre Coated GI Steel]
    B --> C[PUF Core 100 mm Polyurethane Foam k equals 0.022]
    C --> D[Inner Skin 0.5 mm SS 304 Food Grade]
    D --> E[Internal Cold Room 4 C Chilled Storage]
    F[Cam Lock Joint] --> B
    G[Heated Pressure Relief Port for Infiltration Air] --> B
    H[PVC Floor with Anti Skid Texture] --> D
    I[LED Light with IP65 Rating] --> D
    J[Sliding Door 100 mm PUF Insulated] --> D
    K[Temperature Sensor PT100 Class A] --> E

    style B fill:#B0B0B0,color:#000
    style C fill:#F5DEB3,color:#000
    style D fill:#E0E0E0,color:#000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
```

**Material logic**:
- **Outer skin (PPGI)**: weather-resistant, paint doesn't peel in humid outdoor environments.
- **PUF core**: provides both **insulation** and **structural rigidity** (sandwich composite action).
- **Inner skin (SS 304)**: meets **food contact safety** standards; smooth surface (Ra < 0.8 µm) prevents bacterial harborage and is cleanable with CIP chemicals.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Material Selection Decision Tree for Food Industry RAC Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Component Design] --> Q1{Component contacts food?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Operating temperature?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Operates in corrosive brine zone?}
    Q2 -- Below minus 40 C --> SS316L[Select SS 316L Austenitic]
    Q2 -- 0 to minus 40 C --> SS304[Select SS 304 or Copper]
    Q2 -- Above 0 C --> CPVC[Select CPVC or SS 304]
    Q3 -- Yes --> CN[Select Cupro Nickel 90 10 or 70 30]
    Q3 -- No --> CS[Select Carbon Steel with Epoxy Coating]
    Q1 -- No --> Q4{Refrigerant compatibility required?}
    Q4 -- R 717 Ammonia --> CS2[Carbon Steel Acceptable]
    Q4 -- HFC R 134a R 404A --> CU[Copper or Aluminum]
    Q4 -- R 744 CO2 --> SSspec[Special SS Required High Pressure]
    Q4 -- HFO R 1234yf --> CU2[Copper Recommended]
    SS316L --> End[Material Selected]
    SS304 --> End
    CPVC --> End
    CN --> End
    CS --> End
    CS2 --> End
    CU --> End
    SSspec --> End
    CU2 --> End
```

**Reading this decision tree**: A KTU 2024 question may ask "What material would you select for a milk chiller operating at $+2^{\circ}\text{C}$?" Following the tree: **Q1 = Yes** (contacts food) → **Q2 = Above 0°C** → **Select SS 304** (or CPVC for piping). This is the kind of reasoning KTU examiners reward.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Refrigerant Compatibility Matrix with Common Construction Materials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mat[Construction Materials]
        M1[Copper]
        M2[Aluminum]
        M3[Carbon Steel]
        M4[SS 304]
        M5[SS 316L]
        M6[Brass]
        M7[PTFE Gasket]
    end
    subgraph Ref[Refrigerants]
        R1[R 134a HFC]
        R2[R 404A HFC]
        R3[R 290 Propane]
        R4[R 717 Ammonia]
        R5[R 744 CO2]
        R6[R 1234yf HFO]
    end
    M1 ---|Compatible| R1
    M1 ---|Compatible| R2
    M1 ---|Compatible| R6
    M1 ---|NOT Compatible Ammonia Attacks Copper| R4
    M2 ---|Compatible| R1
    M2 ---|Compatible| R2
    M3 ---|Compatible| R4
    M3 ---|Corrosion Risk| R5
    M4 ---|Compatible| R5
    M4 ---|Compatible| R6
    M6 ---|NOT Compatible with Ammonia Stress Cracking| R4
    M7 ---|Compatible All| R1
    M7 ---|Compatible All| R4
```

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM POINT**: Copper is **strictly forbidden** in ammonia (R-717) systems. Ammonia forms a stable, explosive, and toxic complex with copper called **cuprammonium** (Cu(NH₃)₄²⁺). This is a **hard rule** — no marks are given if you specify copper for ammonia piping. **Use carbon steel or stainless steel** instead.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sem VI]
**Differentiate between hermetic, semi-hermetic, and open-type compressors in terms of construction and application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Feature | Hermetic | Semi-Hermetic | Open Type |
|---------|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Shell construction** | Welded steel, non-serviceable | Bolted cast iron, field-serviceable | Separate motor + compressor coupled by shaft |
| **Shaft seal** | None (motor inside refrigerant) | None (motor in refrigerant space) | Required (carbon-graphite or ceramic) |
| **Leakage risk** | Negligible | Negligible | Present (rotating shaft penetration) |
| **Application** | Domestic refrigerators, small commercial chillers, food retail display | Medium-capacity cold rooms, supermarket racks | Large industrial ammonia plants, central chilling |
| **Maintenance** | Replace entire unit on burnout | Replace windings, valves, bearings | Standard mechanical servicing |
| **Capacity range** | 0.1 – 10 TR | 5 – 100 TR | > 50 TR |

**Key distinction (1 mark)**: Hermetic and semi-hermetic have **no shaft seal** (motor is inside the refrigerant), whereas open type has an **external shaft seal** that is a chronic leakage point.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sem VI]
**Why is Polyurethane Foam (PUF) preferred over Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) for cold storage insulation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

1. **Lower thermal conductivity**: PUF has $k \approx 0.022 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ vs. EPS $k \approx 0.035 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ — a **37% improvement** in insulating efficiency, requiring thinner walls for the same $U$-value.

2. **Closed-cell structure acts as built-in vapor barrier**: PUF has > 95% closed cells, so it does not require a separate polyethylene vapor barrier. EPS is more permeable to water vapor, leading to condensation and loss of insulating value over time.

3. **Higher structural strength**: PUF bonds with the metal skin to form a **sandwich composite** with significant load-bearing capacity (used for floor panels of cold rooms). EPS crumbles under sustained load.

4. **Lower water absorption**: PUF absorbs < 1% water by volume; EPS can absorb 2–4%, leading to thermal bridging.

5. **Better fire performance with proper additives**: Fire-retardant grade PUF (B-s2, d0) self-extinguishes.

(Any **three** of the above five points earn 3 marks. The first two are mandatory for full marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Pattern

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **constructional features of a hermetic reciprocating compressor** used in commercial refrigeration. List **two materials** used for the cylinder and **two for the motor windings**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage wall is built with the following layers (from outside to inside): **2 mm pre-coated GI steel sheet**, **120 mm PUF insulation**, **2 mm SS 304 inner liner**. The inside air temperature is $-20^{\circ}\text{C}$ and outside ambient is $38^{\circ}\text{C}$. The inside air HTC is $h_i = 7 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ and outside $h_o = 15 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$. Calculate: (i) overall thermal resistance, (ii) heat flux through the wall, (iii) outer surface temperature of the PUF insulation. Take $k_{\text{PUF}} = 0.022$, $k_{\text{GI}} = 52$, $k_{\text{SS}} = 16.2$ W/(m·K). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (3 marks)**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Electrical Terminals] --> B[Overload Protector]
    B --> C[Motor Stator Copper Windings Class F]
    C --> D[Rotor on Crankshaft]
    D --> E[Connecting Rod Aluminum]
    E --> F[Piston Aluminum or Cast Iron]
    F --> G[Cylinder Cast Iron or Aluminum]
    G --> H[Suction Valve Stainless Steel Reed]
    H --> I[Discharge Valve Stainless Steel Reed]
    I --> J[Discharge Muffler]
    J --> K[Discharge Tube]
    G --> L[Crankcase with POE Lubricating Oil]
    M[Suction Accumulator] --> G
    N[Vibration Mounts] --> O[Welded Steel Outer Shell]
    style C fill:#FFD700
    style G fill:#C0C0C0
    style O fill:#FFA07A
```

**Constructional features explanation (2 marks)**:
- The compressor and motor are housed inside a **single welded steel shell** filled with refrigerant and oil. There is no shaft penetrating the shell, so **leakage is structurally impossible**.
- Refrigerant vapor is drawn in through the suction tube, compressed by the piston, and discharged through the discharge tube — both tubes exit through the same shell wall via copper-to-steel braze joints.
- A small internal **oil sump** with a pickup tube ensures lubrication of the crankshaft bearings.

**Materials (2 marks)**:
- **Cylinder materials**: Cast iron (most common), aluminum alloy (light-duty compressors).
- **Motor winding materials**: Copper wire with **Class F** insulation (epoxy or polyesterimide enamel rated to $155^{\circ}\text{C}$).

---

#### Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Given**:
- $L_1 = 0.002$ m (GI), $L_2 = 0.120$ m (PUF), $L_3 = 0.002$ m (SS)
- $k_1 = 52$ W/(m·K), $k_2 = 0.022$ W/(m·K), $k_3 = 16.2$ W/(m·K)
- $h_i = 7$ W/(m²·K), $h_o = 15$ W/(m²·K)
- $T_i = -20^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_o = 38^{\circ}\text{C}$, so $\Delta T = 58$ K

**Step 1: Compute individual resistances (2 marks)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{h,i} &= \frac{1}{7} = 0.1429 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \\
R_1 &= \frac{0.002}{52} = 0.0000385 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \\
R_2 &= \frac{0.120}{0.022} = 5.4545 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \\
R_3 &= \frac{0.002}{16.2} = 0.000123 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \\
R_{h,o} &= \frac{1}{15} = 0.0667 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sum the total resistance (1 mark)**:

$$R_{\text{tot}} = 0.1429 + 0.0000385 + 5.4545 + 0.000123 + 0.0667 = 5.6643 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 3: Heat flux (2 marks)**:

$$q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{tot}}} = \frac{58}{5.6643} = 10.24 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Temperature at outer PUF surface (2 marks)**:

The outer surface of the PUF is the interface with the GI sheet. Moving from the outside ($T_o$) inward:

$$T_{\text{PUF outer}} = T_o - q \cdot R_{h,o} - q \cdot R_1$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{PUF outer}} &= 38 - (10.24 \times 0.0667) - (10.24 \times 0.0000385) \\
T_{\text{PUF outer}} &= 38 - 0.683 - 0.000394 \\
T_{\text{PUF outer}} &= 37.32^{\circ}\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The interface temperature is **only $0.68^{\circ}\text{C}$ below ambient**, confirming the well-known principle that the **metal skins** contribute negligible resistance — the PUF layer carries the entire thermal gradient.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **constructional features of an air-cooled finned-tube condenser** with a labelled diagram. Discuss the rationale for selecting **copper tubes** and **aluminum fins** in terms of thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, and refrigerant compatibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refrigeration pipe of **25 mm outer diameter** carries ammonia refrigerant (R-717) at $-15^{\circ}\text{C}$ and is insulated with **mineral wool** ($k = 0.040$ W/(m·K), thickness 50 mm). The ambient air is at $30^{\circ}\text{C}$ with surface heat transfer coefficient $h_o = 10$ W/(m²·K). Calculate: (i) the **critical radius of insulation**, (ii) the heat loss per metre of pipe length, and (iii) comment on whether the chosen insulation thickness is **adequate** from a critical-radius perspective. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**Labelled Diagram (3 marks)**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Discharge Vapor from Compressor at High Pressure and Temperature] --> B[Top Manifold Header Copper]
    B --> C[Copper Tubes 9.52 mm OD 0.7 mm Wall Mechanically Expanded]
    C --> D[Aluminum Fins 0.13 mm Thick 10 Fins per Inch Corrugated or Louvered]
    D --> E[Bottom Manifold Header Copper]
    E --> F[Liquid Refrigerant Outlet to Receiver]
    G[Induced Draft Fan 1.5 m diameter 0.75 kW] -.draws air.-> D
    H[Galvanized Steel Casing 0.8 mm] -.supports.-> D
    I[Mounting Feet with Rubber Isolators] --> H
    J[Service Valve at Inlet] --> B
    K[Service Valve at Outlet] --> E
```

**Constructional features (2 marks)**:
- Copper tubes are arranged in **staggered rows** inside a casing. Refrigerant flows inside the tubes; air is drawn across the tubes-and-fins by an axial fan.
- **Mechanical expansion** of the tubes into the fins (or use of a **copper tube-aluminum fin bond via epoxy coating**) ensures intimate thermal contact.
- The whole assembly is mounted on a **galvanized steel frame** with a protective grille on the air inlet.

**Material rationale (2 marks)**:
- **Copper tubes**: High thermal conductivity ($k = 385$ W/(m·K)) ensures the tube wall temperature is nearly uniform; it is also **resistant to HFC refrigerants** (R-134a, R-404A) and easily brazed for leak-tight joints. DHP-grade copper (C12200) has superior corrosion resistance to refrigerants containing trace moisture.
- **Aluminum fins**: Reasonable thermal conductivity ($k = 205$ W/(m·K)), **lightweight** (one-third the density of steel), and forms a self-protective **alumina (Al₂O₃) passive layer** against atmospheric corrosion. In corrosive coastal or industrial atmospheres, **epoxy-coated "blue fin"** variants are used.

---

#### Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Given**:
- $r_1 = 0.0125$ m (25 mm OD ÷ 2)
- $r_2 = 0.0125 + 0.050 = 0.0625$ m (with 50 mm insulation)
- $T_\infty = 30^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_s = -15^{\circ}\text{C}$, so $\Delta T = 45$ K
- $k = 0.040$ W/(m·K), $h_o = 10$ W/(m²·K)

**Part (i) — Critical radius (2 marks)**:

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h_o} = \frac{0.040}{10} = 0.004 \text{ m} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (ii) — Heat loss per metre (3 marks)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_L &= \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_2 h_o}} \\
\ln(r_2/r_1) &= \ln(0.0625/0.0125) = \ln(5) = 1.609 \\
\text{Conduction term} &= \frac{1.609}{2\pi \times 0.040} = \frac{1.609}{0.2513} = 6.402 \text{ m·K/W} \\
\text{Convection term} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.0625 \times 10} = \frac{1}{3.927} = 0.2546 \text{ m·K/W} \\
q_L &= \frac{45}{6.402 + 0.2546} = \frac{45}{6.657} = 6.76 \text{ W/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (iii) — Adequacy comment (2 marks)**:

Since $r_1 = 12.5 \text{ mm} \gg r_c = 4 \text{ mm}$, the bare pipe is far beyond the critical radius. Adding insulation **monotonically reduces** heat loss. The 50 mm thickness is **adequately safe** — there is no heat-loss penalty to fear. For **economic insulation thickness** (where added insulation cost equals saved refrigeration cost), one would optimize at $r_2 \approx 1.5$ to $2 r_c$ in non-critical applications, or larger when energy cost is high.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation**:
> 1. **Forgetting to include surface convective resistances** ($1/h_i$ and $1/h_o$) when computing the overall thermal resistance of a wall. The error is $\sim 3\text{–}4\%$ in well-insulated walls but the **methodology marks are forfeited**.
> 2. **Confusing "thermal conductivity" with "thermal conductance"** ($k$ vs. $k/L$). KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this slip.
> 3. **Specifying copper for ammonia systems** — this is a hard "no" because of cuprammonium formation. Always choose **carbon steel or stainless steel** for R-717.
> 4. **Missing units** in the final answer. $Q$ should be in W or kW; $q$ in W/m²; $q_L$ in W/m. An un-numbered answer loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the assumptions** in derivations (steady-state, 1-D conduction, constant $k$). Always list them.
> 6. **Drawing a Hermetic compressor with a visible shaft seal** — this is a structural contradiction. Hermetic means *no* shaft penetration.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Construction and Material**

### A. Constructional Knowledge Points
- ☐ **Compressors**: Hermetic (no shaft seal, replaceable), Semi-hermetic (bolted, serviceable), Open (shaft seal, leak risk).
- ☐ **Condensers**: Air-cooled (copper tube + aluminum fin), Water-cooled shell-and-tube (cupro-nickel tubes for chloride resistance), Evaporative (sprayed coil).
- ☐ **Evaporators**: DX finned-tube (most common in cold rooms), Flooded (high efficiency), Plate (compact, hygienic).
- ☐ **Expansion Devices**: TXV, AXV, Capillary tube, EEV.
- ☐ **Cold room envelope**: Sandwich panel — PPGI + PUF + SS304, cam-lock joint, EPDM gasket.

### B. Material Selection Rules
- ☐ **Copper + Ammonia = NEVER** (cuprammonium complex).
- ☐ **Stainless steel 304** is the **default food contact surface** (Ra ≤ 0.8 µm).
- ☐ **Cupro-nickel 90/10 or 70/30** for **cooling tower water** tubes (chloride resistance).
- ☐ **Aluminum fins** paired with copper tubes (cost-effective, corrosion-resistant with epoxy coating).
- ☐ **DHP copper (C12200)** for refrigerant-carrying tubes.

### C. Insulation Material Hierarchy
- ☐ **Best**: PUF ($k = 0.022$) — closed-cell, structural, vapor barrier built-in.
- ☐ **Good**: XPS extruded polystyrene ($k = 0.029$) — moisture resistant.
- ☐ **Adequate**: EPS expanded polystyrene ($k = 0.035$) — needs separate vapor barrier.
- ☐ **Specialty**: Vacuum Insulation Panel (VIP) ($k \leq 0.008$) — for ultra-low temp.
- ☐ **Natural**: Cork ($k = 0.040$) — older cold stores, low cost.

### D. High-Yield Formulas (Memorize)
- ☐ Fourier: $Q = kA \Delta T / L$
- ☐ Composite wall: $1/U = \Sigma R_i$
- ☐ Cylindrical: $q_L = 2\pi L (T_\infty - T_s) / [\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + 1/(r_2 h_o)]$
- ☐ Critical radius: $r_c = k / h_o$

### E. KTU-Specific Traps
- ☐ "Adding insulation always reduces heat loss" — **False** when $r_1 < r_c$.
- ☐ $T_{\text{amb}} = 35^{\circ}\text{C}$ is the default for Indian cold-room design.
- ☐ Always state "1-D, steady-state, constant $k$" in derivations.
- ☐ Inner skin in food-grade cold room = **SS 304**, not GI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Floors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Refrigeration & Air Conditioning in the Food Industry**, the term **"Floors"** refers to the engineered floor system of a **cold storage room (chiller or freezer)** that is specifically designed to:
1. Provide adequate **thermal insulation** between the cold interior and the warm earth beneath.
2. Bear **heavy static and dynamic loads** from stored food pallets, racking systems, and forklift traffic.
3. Resist **moisture and water vapor migration** that would otherwise condense and freeze within the floor structure.
4. Prevent **sub-soil frost heaving (ground freezing)** beneath freezer rooms, which can lift foundations and damage the building.
5. Maintain **hygienic, non-slip, and easily cleanable** surfaces suitable for food handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A cold storage floor is NOT a normal building floor. It is a **multi-layered composite structure** integrating load-bearing concrete, vapor barriers, thermal insulation, and frequently a **floor heating system** — making it one of the most critical and expensive components of a freezer construction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Think of a cold storage floor like a **thermos flask turned upside down and buried in soil**:

- The **thermos walls (insulation)** keep heat out (or in our case, keep the cold in and the ground heat out).
- The **cap of the thermos (vapor barrier)** prevents warm, moist air from sneaking in and condensing inside — because if moisture sneaks in and freezes, the "thermos" swells and cracks (ice damage).
- A **tiny electric blanket (floor heater)** is sometimes stitched inside the floor, not to heat the room, but to keep the soil *just warm enough* so it never freezes and pushes the building up like a popsicle out of a mould.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the floor different from walls and ceilings?**
> - **Walls & ceiling** are exposed to *outside air* (fixed ambient temperature).
> - **Floor** sits on *earth* whose temperature is naturally warm (~10–25 °C depending on depth & location). So the floor has a **larger temperature gradient** and a **continuous, un-ending heat source** (the infinite heat capacity of earth) flowing upward — making it the **largest heat gain area** in any freezer room.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Earth/ground temperature (1–2 m depth) | **10 °C to 25 °C** (tropical) / **5 °C to 15 °C** (temperate) |
| Chiller room temperature | **0 °C to +5 °C** |
| Freezer room temperature | **−18 °C to −40 °C** |
| Load on floor (pallet racking) | **5 to 15 kN/m²** |
| Forklift live load | **Up to 50 kN (point load)** |
| Concrete density | **2400 kg/m³** |
| Thermal conductivity of concrete | **k ≈ 1.4 W/m·K** |
| Thermal conductivity of soil | **k ≈ 0.8 to 1.5 W/m·K** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Construction of a Cold Storage Floor (Bottom → Top)

A modern cold storage floor is built as a **sandwich of 5 to 7 layers**, each performing a specific role:

| Layer (from bottom) | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Sub-grade | Compacted earth / sand | Provides uniform base |
| 2. Waterproofing membrane | Bitumen / polyethylene sheet | Stops liquid water from rising |
| 3. Floor heater (optional) | Electric cables / warm glycol pipes | Prevents sub-soil freezing |
| 4. Thermal insulation | **Expanded polystyrene (EPS) / XPS / Foamglass** | Reduces heat flow from earth |
| 5. Vapor barrier (on warm side of insulation) | Heavy-gauge polythene / aluminium foil | Stops moisture migration into insulation |
| 6. Load-bearing slab | Reinforced concrete (RCC), 100–150 mm | Carries storage loads |
| 7. Floor finish | Epoxy / PU screed / chequered tiles | Hygiene, slip resistance, abrasion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** The **vapor barrier must always be installed on the WARM side of the insulation** — i.e., between the insulation and the warm earth. If installed on the cold side, water vapour will diffuse *into* the insulation, condense, freeze, and destroy it.

## 2.2 Design Considerations — The "Why" and "How"

1. **Thermal Insulation Thickness**
   - *Why:* Earth is a continuous heat source; without insulation, refrigeration compressors would run non-stop.
   - *How:* Thickness is selected such that the heat gain through the floor is restricted to ~5–10 W/m² for freezers and 8–15 W/m² for chillers.

2. **Vapor Barrier Integrity**
   - *Why:* Water vapour pressure is much higher on the warm side; it will diffuse towards the cold side and freeze inside insulation if allowed.
   - *How:* All joints overlapped and sealed; penetrations (pipes, conduits) sealed with mastic.

3. **Floor Heating System (for freezers only)**
   - *Why:* If the soil beneath the freezer drops below **0 °C**, the moisture in soil freezes, expands ~9%, and **lifts the entire floor** (frost heave), destroying the building.
   - *How:* Electric heating cables or warm-water pipes embedded in the screed maintain sub-soil temperature at **+1 °C to +5 °C**.

4. **Load-Bearing Capacity**
   - *Why:* Cold stores stack food 5–8 m high on racking.
   - *How:* RCC slab is reinforced with steel mesh; sometimes ground-supported slabs are used for very heavy loads (e.g., 50 kN/m²).

5. **Hygienic Finish**
   - *Why:* Food safety regulations (FSSAI, HACCP) require seamless, non-porous, easy-to-clean floors.
   - *How:* Epoxy resin or polyurethane coatings; coving at wall-floor junctions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all formulas you must memorise** for solving floor heat-load and insulation thickness problems in the KTU exam. Pay special attention to units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat gain through floor | $Q = \dfrac{A \cdot (T_g - T_r)}{R_{total}}$ | W | $T_g$ = ground temp, $T_r$ = room temp |
| 2 | Thermal resistance of one layer | $R = \dfrac{x}{k}$ | m²·K/W | $x$ = thickness, $k$ = conductivity |
| 3 | Total thermal resistance (series) | $R_{total} = \sum R_i = \dfrac{x_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{x_2}{k_2} + \cdots$ | m²·K/W | Layers are in series |
| 4 | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$ | W/m²·K | |
| 5 | Required insulation thickness | $x_{ins} = k_{ins} \cdot \left( \dfrac{T_g - T_r}{q_{allow}} - R_{other} \right)$ | m | $q_{allow}$ = allowed heat flux |
| 6 | Floor heater capacity | $P_h = q_{allow} \cdot A$ | W | Compensates heat flow |
| 7 | Heat flux through floor | $q = U \cdot (T_g - T_r)$ | W/m² | |
| 8 | Steady-state conduction (1D) | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_g - T_r)}{x}$ | W | Fourier's law |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cold storage floor engineering is a **multi-disciplinary design problem** combining:
- **Mechanical engineers** size the refrigeration plant from floor heat-load.
- **Civil/structural engineers** size the slab, reinforcement, and foundations.
- **Electrical engineers** design the floor heating cable circuits and protection.
- **Food technologists** specify hygienic finishes and drainage gradients.

A badly designed floor can cause:
- 30–50% of **total refrigeration load** in a poorly insulated facility.
- Structural collapse due to **frost heave** (historical failures in early 20th-century cold stores).
- Ice formation, leading to **slip hazards** and contamination of stored food.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Transfer Through a Multi-Layered Cold Storage Floor

### Setup
Consider a freezer floor of total area **A**, with **n** layers stacked between the warm earth (temperature $T_g$) and the cold room (temperature $T_r$). At steady state, the same heat $Q$ flows through every layer (conservation of energy).

### Step 1 — Heat flux through any single layer i

By **Fourier's Law of Conduction** (one-dimensional, steady state, no internal heat generation):

$$q_i = \frac{k_i \cdot (T_{i,h} - T_{i,c})}{x_i}$$

where $T_{i,h}$ and $T_{i,c}$ are the hot- and cold-face temperatures of layer $i$.

### Step 2 — Express in terms of thermal resistance

$$q_i = \frac{T_{i,h} - T_{i,c}}{\dfrac{x_i}{k_i}} = \frac{T_{i,h} - T_{i,c}}{R_i}$$

### Step 3 — Apply continuity (steady state)

Because the same heat flux passes through all layers in series:

$$q = q_1 = q_2 = \cdots = q_n$$

So the **total temperature difference** is the sum of individual temperature drops:

$$T_g - T_r = q \cdot R_1 + q \cdot R_2 + \cdots + q \cdot R_n$$

$$T_g - T_r = q \cdot (R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n)$$

### Step 4 — Total thermal resistance

$$\boxed{R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_i}{k_i}}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for heat flux

$$\boxed{q = \frac{T_g - T_r}{R_{total}}}$$

### Step 6 — Multiply by area to get total heat load

$$\boxed{Q = q \cdot A = \frac{A \cdot (T_g - T_r)}{R_{total}}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example

> A cold storage floor has the following layers (from earth to room):  
> Concrete (150 mm, k = 1.4 W/m·K) → EPS insulation (100 mm, k = 0.035 W/m·K) → Concrete screed (50 mm, k = 1.4 W/m·K).  
> $T_g = 20\,°C$, $T_r = -25\,°C$, $A = 200\,m^2$.  
> Calculate: **(a)** total R-value, **(b)** heat flux, **(c)** total heat gain.

#### (a) Total R-value

$$R_{conc,150} = \frac{0.150}{1.4} = 0.1071 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{EPS} = \frac{0.100}{0.035} = 2.8571 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{conc,50} = \frac{0.050}{1.4} = 0.0357 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$\boxed{R_{total} = 0.1071 + 2.8571 + 0.0357 = 3.000 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}}$$

#### (b) Heat flux

$$\Delta T = T_g - T_r = 20 - (-25) = 45\,°C$$

$$q = \frac{45}{3.000} = 15 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

#### (c) Total heat gain

$$Q = q \cdot A = 15 \times 200 = 3000 \;\text{W} = 3.0 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The **EPS insulation contributes ~95%** of the total thermal resistance ($2.8571/3.000$). This proves that the **insulation layer dominates** the floor's thermal performance — concrete is structurally necessary but thermally negligible.

## 3.2 Derivation: Floor Heater Capacity (to Prevent Sub-Soil Freezing)

### Logic
The floor heater is installed in the concrete screed **above the vapor barrier** to keep the temperature at the **bottom of the insulation (i.e., the soil interface) above 0 °C**.

### Step 1 — Heat flow at the soil–insulation interface

If the floor heater supplies power $P_h$ per unit area, and the natural heat flux from earth is $q$, then to maintain soil-interface temperature at exactly 0 °C:

$$P_h = q - \frac{T_g - T_{soil,target}}{R_{soil}}$$

But in practice, a **simpler conservative formula** is used:

$$\boxed{P_h = U_{total} \cdot (T_g - T_{allow})}$$

where $T_{allow}$ is the design soil temperature (typically +2 °C to +5 °C).

### Step 2 — Empirical shortcut used by designers

A common rule-of-thumb is:

$$P_h \approx 10 \;\text{to}\; 20 \;\text{W/m}^2 \quad \text{for freezers at } -25\,°C$$

This is **independent of insulation thickness** because a thicker insulation means less heat enters, so the heater must replace that heat to keep soil warm. The heater is sized to *match* the heat that escapes downward.

### Numerical Example — Heater Sizing

> Freezer area: 100 m². Floor has $U = 0.33$ W/m²·K (i.e., R = 3.0).  
> $T_g = 18\,°C$, $T_{allow} = +2\,°C$ (target soil temperature).  
> Required heater power?

$$P_h = 0.33 \times (18 - 2) = 0.33 \times 16 = 5.3 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

$$P_{h,total} = 5.3 \times 100 = 530 \;\text{W}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Choosing Floor Type)

| Storage Type | Typical Temp | Insulation Type | Insulation Thickness | Heater? | Finish |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chiller (+5 °C)** | 0 to +5 °C | EPS / XPS | 50–80 mm | ❌ No | Epoxy / tiles |
| **Freezer (−18 °C)** | −18 to −25 °C | XPS / Foamglass | 100–150 mm | ✅ Yes | PU screed |
| **Deep Freezer (−40 °C)** | −30 to −40 °C | Foamglass / PIR | 150–200 mm | ✅ Yes (larger) | PU screed |
| **Blast Freezer** | −35 to −40 °C | PIR / PUF | 150–200 mm | ✅ Yes | Heavy PU |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Layered Cross-Section of a Cold Storage Floor (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Subgrade Compacted Earth at Tg 18 to 25 C] --> B[Waterproofing Bitumen Membrane]
    B --> C[Floor Heater Electric Cable or Glycol Pipe 10 to 20 W per m2]
    C --> D[Vapor Barrier Polythene 500 micron on Warm Side]
    D --> E[Thermal Insulation EPS or XPS 100 to 200 mm]
    E --> F[Reinforced Concrete Slab 100 to 150 mm]
    F --> G[Finish Epoxy or PU Screed Hygiene Layer]
    G --> H[Cold Room Air at Tr minus 18 to minus 40 C]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Heat-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EarthSide[Earth Side Warm]
        A1[Ground Heat Source Tg 20 C] --> A2[Soil Subgrade]
        A2 --> A3[Waterproofing Layer]
        A3 --> A4[Floor Heater Compensates Loss]
    end
    subgraph InsulationBlock[Insulation System]
        B1[Vapor Barrier on Warm Side] --> B2[Insulation Layer EPS XPS]
        B2 --> B3[Load Bearing Concrete Slab]
    end
    subgraph RoomSide[Cold Room Side]
        C1[PU or Epoxy Finish] --> C2[Refrigerated Air at Tr minus 25 C]
    end
    A4 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
    C2 -.Heat Leak.-> A1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Floor Heater

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start Room Type] --> Q{Chiller or Freezer?}
    Q -->|Chiller| R[No Heater Required]
    Q -->|Freezer| S{Soil Below 0 C Risk?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Install Electric Heating Cable 10 to 20 W per m2]
    S -->|No| U[Optional Glycol Pipe Heater]
    T --> V[Thermostat Set to plus 2 to plus 5 C]
    U --> V
    V --> W[End Heater System]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Heat Load Computation Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Tg Ground Temp] --> B[Input Tr Room Temp]
    B --> C[Calculate Rtotal Sum of x over k]
    C --> D[Compute U 1 over Rtotal]
    D --> E[Compute q U times Delta T]
    E --> F[Compute Q q times A]
    F --> G[Add to Refrigeration Plant Load]
    G --> H[End]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Why is a vapor barrier placed on the **warm side** of the floor insulation and not on the cold side? What happens if it is misplaced?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. (1 mark) Water vapour always diffuses from high-pressure (warm) to low-pressure (cold) side. A vapor barrier on the warm side **stops moisture from entering the insulation**.
2. (1 mark) If placed on the cold side, vapour passes through insulation, condenses inside, and **freezes**, destroying thermal performance.
3. (1 mark) Consequences: **ice formation, swelling, loss of insulation, floor heaving** and structural damage.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any **three functions** of a floor heating system in a freezer room.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. (1 mark) Prevents **sub-soil frost heaving** by keeping soil temperature above 0 °C.
2. (1 mark) Compensates for **heat loss downward** from the freezer to the earth.
3. (1 mark) Protects building **foundation and structural integrity** from uplift forces caused by freezing moisture in the soil.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction of a cold storage floor** for a freezer room operating at **−25 °C**. Clearly label all layers and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage floor (area = 250 m²) has the following layers:  
- 100 mm concrete screed (k = 1.4 W/m·K)  
- 150 mm EPS insulation (k = 0.038 W/m·K)  
- 150 mm RCC slab (k = 1.4 W/m·K)  

Ground temperature is 20 °C, room temperature is −25 °C.  
Calculate: **(i)** total R-value, **(ii)** overall U-value, **(iii)** total heat gain through the floor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

| # | Layer (bottom → top) | Material | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sub-grade | Compacted earth/sand | Uniform load-bearing base | 1 |
| 2 | Waterproofing | Bitumen / PE membrane | Stops liquid water rising | 1 |
| 3 | Floor heater | Electric cables | Prevents soil freezing | 1 |
| 4 | Vapor barrier | 500 µm polythene | Stops vapour diffusion | 1 |
| 5 | Insulation | EPS / XPS (150 mm) | Reduces heat flow | 1 |
| 6 | RCC slab | Reinforced concrete | Carries storage load | 1 |
| 7 | Finish | Epoxy / PU screed | Hygiene & durability | 1 |

*(Sketch: vertical section showing the 7 layers — refer to SECTION 4.1 for the Mermaid diagram.)*

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Total R-value** (2 marks)

$$R_{screed} = \frac{0.100}{1.4} = 0.0714 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{EPS} = \frac{0.150}{0.038} = 3.9474 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{RCC} = \frac{0.150}{1.4} = 0.1071 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.0714 + 3.9474 + 0.1071 = 4.1259 \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2: U-value** (2 marks)

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{4.1259} = 0.2424 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3: Total heat gain** (3 marks)

$$\Delta T = 20 - (-25) = 45\;\text{K}$$

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 0.2424 \times 45 = 10.91 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

$$Q = q \cdot A = 10.91 \times 250 = 2727 \;\text{W} \approx 2.73 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using 273 instead of 20 in temperature difference (1 mark lost). Always subtract in °C or K — both give the same ΔT.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert **mm to m** in thickness. (1 mark lost).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing R values without units — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating layer functions in (a) — list clearly or lose 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **phenomenon of frost heaving** in cold storage floors. How is it prevented? Describe the working of a **floor heating system** with a diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A freezer floor of 80 m² has total R-value = 3.5 m²K/W. Ground temperature = 18 °C, room temperature = −20 °C. The floor heater is designed to keep soil interface at +2 °C. Calculate: **(i)** heat loss through floor, **(ii)** required heater capacity per m² and total heater power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Frost Heaving (3 marks):**
- (1) When a freezer operates continuously, cold penetrates downward into the soil.
- (1) If soil temperature drops below 0 °C, the moisture in soil freezes and **expands by ~9%**.
- (1) This expansion exerts an **uplift force of up to 100–200 kN/m²** that lifts and cracks the floor and foundation — a phenomenon called **frost heave**.

**Prevention (2 marks):**
- (1) Provide **adequate insulation** between the cold room and the soil.
- (1) Install a **floor heating system** (electric cables or warm-water pipes) to maintain soil temperature at +1 °C to +5 °C.

**Working of Floor Heating (2 marks):**
- (1) Heating cables are embedded in the concrete screed above the vapor barrier.
- (1) A **thermostat** senses soil temperature and switches the heater ON when soil drops below the set point (~+2 °C) and OFF when above it, ensuring energy-efficient operation.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Heat loss through floor** (3 marks)

$$\Delta T = T_g - T_r = 18 - (-20) = 38\;\text{K}$$

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{3.5} = 0.2857 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$Q = \frac{A \cdot \Delta T}{R_{total}} = \frac{80 \times 38}{3.5} = 868.6 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 2: Heater capacity per m²** (2 marks)

To keep soil at +2 °C, the heater must compensate heat flow as if room were at +2 °C:

$$q_h = \frac{T_g - T_{soil,target}}{R_{total}} = \frac{18 - 2}{3.5} = \frac{16}{3.5} = 4.57 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Total heater power** (2 marks)

$$P_{h,total} = q_h \times A = 4.57 \times 80 = 365.7 \;\text{W} \approx 0.37 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **heat loss through floor (Q)** with **heater power (P_h)** — they are different. Q goes upward (to room); P_h compensates downward flow.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $T_r = -20\,°C$ instead of $T_{soil} = +2\,°C$ in the heater calculation — the heater's job is soil protection, NOT room heating.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Drawing the floor heating diagram without the thermostat — KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Points for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ A cold storage **floor is a multi-layer sandwich**: subgrade → waterproofing → heater → vapor barrier → insulation → RCC → finish.
- ☐ The **vapor barrier is ALWAYS on the warm side** of insulation — never on the cold side.
- ☐ Floor **insulation dominates** total R-value (contributes 90–95% of thermal resistance).
- ☐ **Frost heave** occurs when soil beneath a freezer freezes; prevented by **floor heating system**.
- ☐ Typical **floor heat flux**: 8–15 W/m² for freezers (well-insulated); can exceed 30 W/m² in poorly insulated old cold stores.
- ☐ Floor heating capacity: $P_h = U \cdot (T_g - T_{soil,target})$ — **not** $(T_g - T_r)$.
- ☐ The earth is a **continuous, infinite heat source** — unlike air, which cools down.
- ☐ **Hygiene finish** (epoxy/PU) is mandatory for food-grade cold stores.
- ☐ Always convert **mm → m** in thickness when using the formula $R = x/k$.
- ☐ The **driving temperature difference** for floors is **larger** than for walls (earth is warmer than outdoor air in summer).
- ☐ **Floor heater thermostat set-point**: typically **+2 °C to +5 °C** at soil interface.
- ☐ Insulation materials for floors: **EPS, XPS, Foamglass, PIR** — Foamglass is preferred under heavy loads (zero water absorption, high compressive strength).
- ☐ In KTU numericals, remember to write **units** with every numerical value (e.g., W/m²K, m²K/W) — missing units = mark deductions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frost Heave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frost Heave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Frost Heave** is defined as the upward (or outward) displacement of the ground surface and embedded structures caused by the formation, growth, and segregation of discrete **ice lenses** within fine-grained, frost-susceptible soils when subjected to sub-freezing temperatures from refrigeration systems, cold storage foundations, or cryogenic installations.

In the context of **OEFTT612 – Refrigeration & Air Conditioning in Food Industry**, frost heave is the geotechnical phenomenon that occurs beneath:
- Cold storage warehouses (especially sub-zero frozen food storage)
- Floor slabs of chilled and frozen food processing plants
- Foundations of refrigeration compressors and condenser pads
- Underground chilled water / brine pipelines
- Frozen food floor heating systems

The governing framework is the **thermodynamic-mechanical coupling** between heat transfer, moisture migration, and ice crystallization within the soil pore structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> "Frost heave is the process by which freezing of moisture in soil causes deformation and heaving of the ground due to the formation of segregated ice lenses, resulting in differential settlement, structural cracking, and operational failure of cold-storage infrastructure."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a sponge soaked in water placed inside a freezer. As the cold penetrates the sponge, pure water in the larger pores freezes first. But because the freezing point of water held inside the tiny capillaries is *lower* than 0 °C (a phenomenon called **freezing-point depression**), the sponge keeps drawing liquid water from a reservoir below toward the freezing front. This water freezes in thin, horizontal sheets called **ice lenses**.

Each new ice lens pushes everything above it **upward**, just like a slowly inflating balloon lifting a book off a table. The book does not freeze, but it still moves up because the ground beneath it is growing taller.

**Real-world equivalence for food cold storage:**
- The "sponge" = frost-susceptible soil (silty clay)
- The "freezer" = the refrigerated floor slab (often at −18 °C to −40 °C)
- The "reservoir" = groundwater table below
- The "ice lens" = horizontal ice crystals that heave the floor upward
- The "book" = the concrete floor, racks, conveyors, and rack-loads of frozen food

If the heave exceeds **25 mm to 50 mm** (industry threshold), the floor cracks, rack feet bend, conveyor rails misalign, and doors jam — all of which are catastrophic in a 24/7 food supply chain.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Board-Relevant)

The following constants are essential for all frost-heave derivations and must be memorized:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of fusion of water | $L_f$ | **333.55 kJ/kg** (≈ 334 kJ/kg) | kJ/kg |
| Density of water at 4 °C | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Density of ice at 0 °C | $\rho_{ice}$ | **917 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Specific volume change (water→ice) | $\Delta v$ | **9.05 × 10⁻⁵ m³/kg** | m³/kg |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | J/(mol·K) |
| Molar mass of water | $M$ | **0.018 kg/mol** | kg/mol |
| Thermal conductivity of frozen soil | $k_f$ | **1.5 – 2.5 W/(m·K)** | W/(m·K) |
| Thermal conductivity of unfrozen soil | $k_u$ | **1.0 – 1.8 W/(m·K)** | W/(m·K) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these constants matter:** The freezing-point depression (Clapeyron effect) that drives ice-lens growth is directly proportional to $L_f$, inversely proportional to $T$ (in Kelvin), and inversely proportional to $\Delta v$. These constants are non-negotiable in KTU derivations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The frost heave phenomenon is governed by **four coupled physical processes** that operate simultaneously:

#### Step 1 — Heat Transfer Initiation
- Cold from the refrigeration floor (or refrigerated space floor slab at $T_s < 0 °C$) propagates downward into the soil.
- A **freezing front** (0 °C isotherm) advances at a rate governed by the Stefan transient heat conduction equation.
- Above the front: soil is **frozen**; below the front: soil is **unfrozen** but supercooled near the interface.

#### Step 2 — Capillary Suction & Cryogenic Suction
- Freezing-point depression makes pore water in capillaries remain liquid at temperatures slightly below 0 °C.
- A **pressure gradient** develops between the warm groundwater table (high chemical/pressure potential) and the cold freezing front (low potential).
- This gradient drives **upward migration of liquid water** through the unfrozen film on soil grains.

#### Step 3 — Ice Lens Nucleation & Segregation
- Migrated water accumulates at the cold side of the freezing front.
- It freezes in **discrete, horizontal lenses** perpendicular to the heat-flow direction.
- The crystal structure is **ice Ih** (hexagonal), the same form as normal ice.
- Each new lens adds a layer of volume expansion (water expands by **~9%** upon freezing).

#### Step 4 — Mechanical Heave & Structural Uplift
- Cumulative growth of ice lenses exerts **displacement pressure** on overlying soil and structures.
- Differential heave occurs due to varying soil composition, water table depth, and surface temperature.
- Repeated freeze-thaw cycles degrade soil structure (loss of bearing capacity, increased compressibility).

### 2.2 The Three Classic Stages of Frost Heave

| Stage | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | In-situ / Pore-freezing stage | Freezing of pore water *in place*; minimal heave (only 9% expansion in confined pores) |
| **II** | Segregated heave / Stable lens stage | Steady migration of water to growing ice lenses; **maximum heave rate**; longest duration |
| **III** | Diminishing / Hard-frozen stage | Water supply exhausted; lens growth slows; frost penetrates but heave rate approaches zero |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Stage II is the **engineering design stage**. Foundations and insulation must arrest heave *during Stage II*, not Stage I (because pore freezing alone rarely causes failure).

### 2.3 Frost Susceptibility Classification (Beskow's Criterion)

Soils are classified by their **frost susceptibility** based on grain size:

| Soil Type | Grain Size | Frost Susceptibility | Heave Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clean gravel, coarse sand | > 0.2 mm | **Non-susceptible** | Negligible |
| Medium sand | 0.1 – 0.2 mm | Low | Marginal |
| Fine sand | 0.05 – 0.1 mm | Moderate | Moderate |
| Silt | 0.002 – 0.05 mm | **High** | **Severe** |
| Clay | < 0.002 mm | Variable (depends on plasticity) | Low to moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why silt is the most dangerous:** Silt grains are fine enough to develop high capillary suction but coarse enough to permit rapid water migration — a "Goldilocks zone" for ice lens growth.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value expressions use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clapeyron (ice-lens pressure) | $P_{lens} = \dfrac{L_f \cdot \Delta T}{T_{abs} \cdot \Delta v}$ | $P$ in Pa, $L_f$ in J/kg, $\Delta T$ in K, $T_{abs}$ in K, $\Delta v$ in m³/kg |
| 2 | Simplified ice-lens pressure | $P_{lens} \approx 12{,}200 \cdot \Delta T$ | $P$ in kPa, $\Delta T$ in °C |
| 3 | Freezing-point depression | $\Delta T_f = -\dfrac{R \cdot T_f^2}{L_f \cdot M} \cdot \ln(X_w)$ | $X_w$ = mole fraction of water |
| 4 | Clausius–Clapeyron (general) | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{L}{T \cdot \Delta v}$ | Pressure–temperature slope at phase change |
| 5 | Stefan frost-depth equation | $X = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot k_f \cdot \Delta T_s \cdot t}{L_f \cdot \rho_w}}$ | $X$ = frost depth (m), $t$ = time (s) |
| 6 | Modified Stefan (with $k_u$) | $X = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot k_f \cdot \Delta T_s \cdot t}{L_f \cdot \rho_w + c_v \cdot \Delta T_s \cdot \dfrac{k_f}{k_u}}}$ | Accounts for unfrozen-soil sensible heat |
| 7 | Heave rate (Penner model) | $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = \dfrac{k_f}{L_f \cdot \rho_w} \cdot \nabla T \cdot SP$ | $SP$ = segregation potential (mm²/°C·day) |
| 8 | Beskow frost-susceptibility index | $F = \dfrac{d_{max}}{H_w + 1.09 \cdot d_{max}}$ | $d_{max}$ = max grain size, $H_w$ = capillary rise |
| 9 | Konovalov's heave rate | $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = k_h \cdot \nabla T$ | $k_h$ = heave coefficient |
| 10 | Taber rigidity factor | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{ice} - V_{water}}{V_{water}}$ | ≈ 0.0909 (9.09%) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **food industry**, frost heave calculations are critical for:

1. **Cold Storage Foundation Design** — Determining required insulation thickness under floor slabs to keep frost depth *below* the foundation level.
2. **Frozen Food Floor Heating Systems** — Sizing electric or glycol heating cables to prevent ice lens formation.
3. **Sub-zero Warehouse Construction** — Designing gravel/foam insulation pads that maintain the freezing front *above* the structural footing.
4. **Buried Refrigerant Line Design** — Preventing frost bulbs around ammonia/brine pipelines that could heave adjacent foundations.
5. **Cryogenic Food Processing Plants** — Managing LNG/CO₂ pipeline frost heave in food freezing tunnels.
6. **Permafrost-Affected Cold Chains** — Northern India and Himalayan food distribution hubs with seasonal freeze.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Clapeyron Equation for Ice-Lens Pressure (Full Derivation)

**Statement:** The pressure exerted by a growing ice lens on the surrounding soil matrix can be derived from the Clausius–Clapeyron equation applied to the liquid–solid phase equilibrium of pore water.

**Starting Premise:** At the freezing front, ice and liquid water coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. The specific Gibbs free energies are equal:

$$
g_{ice}(P, T) = g_{water}(P, T)
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to temperature along the equilibrium curve:**

$$
\left(\dfrac{\partial g_{ice}}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial g_{ice}}{\partial P}\right)_T dP = \left(\dfrac{\partial g_{water}}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\dfrac{\partial g_{water}}{\partial P}\right)_T dP
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Maxwell relations from thermodynamics:**

Recall that for any pure substance:
$$
\left(\dfrac{\partial g}{\partial T}\right)_P = -s \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\dfrac{\partial g}{\partial P}\right)_T = v
$$

where $s$ = specific entropy, $v$ = specific volume.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the differentiated equation:**

$$
-s_{ice} \, dT + v_{ice} \, dP = -s_{water} \, dT + v_{water} \, dP
$$

**Step 4 — Group terms (collect $dT$ and $dP$):**

$$
(s_{water} - s_{ice}) \, dT = (v_{water} - v_{ice}) \, dP
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the latent-heat relation:**

The latent heat of fusion $L_f$ is released when water freezes:
$$
L_f = T \cdot (s_{water} - s_{ice})
$$

Substituting:

$$
L_f \cdot \dfrac{dT}{T} = (v_{water} - v_{ice}) \, dP
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into the canonical Clausius–Clapeyron form:**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{L_f}{T \cdot (v_{water} - v_{ice})}
$$

**Step 7 — Note the sign convention:**

Since $v_{ice} > v_{water}$ (ice is less dense), the denominator $(v_{water} - v_{ice})$ is **negative**. Define $\Delta v = v_{ice} - v_{water} > 0$ for clarity. Then:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dT} = -\dfrac{L_f}{T \cdot \Delta v}
$$

**Step 8 — Integrate from reference state $(P_0, T_0 = 273.15 \text{ K})$ to current lens state $(P, T)$:**

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\dfrac{L_f}{\Delta v} \int_{T_0}^{T} \dfrac{dT}{T}
$$

$$
P - P_0 = -\dfrac{L_f}{\Delta v} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)
$$

**Step 9 — Use the small-temperature-difference approximation:**

Since $\vert T - T_0 \vert \ll T_0$, expand $\ln(T/T_0) \approx (T - T_0)/T_0 = -\Delta T / T_0$ where $\Delta T = T_0 - T$ (the freezing-point depression).

$$
P - P_0 \approx \dfrac{L_f \cdot \Delta T}{\Delta v \cdot T_0}
$$

**Step 10 — Substitute numerical values to obtain the engineering shortcut:**

$$
P_{lens} \approx \dfrac{(333{,}550 \text{ J/kg}) \cdot \Delta T}{(9.05 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}) \cdot (273.15 \text{ K})}
$$

**Step 11 — Compute the coefficient:**

$$
\text{Coefficient} = \dfrac{333{,}550}{9.05 \times 10^{-5} \times 273.15} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa/K}
$$

Converting to kPa and noting $\Delta T$ in °C ≈ K:

$$
\boxed{P_{lens} \approx 13{,}500 \cdot \Delta T \text{ kPa/°C}}
$$

A commonly cited Board-simplified value is **12,200 kPa/°C** (using rounded $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stefan Frost-Depth Equation

**Starting Premise:** Steady-state one-dimensional heat conduction in a semi-infinite soil mass, with phase-change occurring at a sharp moving front at depth $X(t)$.

**Step 1 — Heat balance at the moving front:**

The latent heat released by freezing water at depth $X$ must equal the heat conducted through the frozen layer:

$$
k_f \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_f}{X} = L_f \cdot \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply and separate variables:**

$$
X \cdot dX = \dfrac{k_f \cdot \Delta T_s}{L_f \cdot \rho_w} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $t = 0, X = 0$ to $t = t, X = X$:**

$$
\int_0^{X} X' \, dX' = \dfrac{k_f \cdot \Delta T_s}{L_f \cdot \rho_w} \int_0^{t} dt'
$$

$$
\dfrac{X^2}{2} = \dfrac{k_f \cdot \Delta T_s}{L_f \cdot \rho_w} \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X$:**

$$
\boxed{X = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot k_f \cdot \Delta T_s \cdot t}{L_f \cdot \rho_w}}}
$$

This is the classic **Stefan equation**, governing how deep the freezing front penetrates under a cold floor over time $t$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solving Typical KTU Frost Heave Problems

**Worked Problem:**

> A frozen-food cold storage floor is maintained at $T_s = -20 °C$. The soil below has $k_f = 2.0 \text{ W/(m·K)}$, $L_f = 334{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$, and $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The initial soil temperature is $T_0 = +10 °C$. Determine the frost depth after **180 days** of continuous operation.

**Step A — Compute surface temperature differential:**

$$
\Delta T_s = T_0 - T_s = 10 - (-20) = 30 °C = 30 \text{ K}
$$

**Step B — Convert time:**

$$
t = 180 \text{ days} \times 86400 \text{ s/day} = 1.5552 \times 10^7 \text{ s}
$$

**Step C — Substitute into Stefan equation:**

$$
X = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 30 \cdot 1.5552 \times 10^7}{334{,}000 \cdot 1000}}
$$

**Step D — Compute numerator and denominator separately:**

Numerator:
$$
2 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 30 \cdot 1.5552 \times 10^7 = 1.86624 \times 10^{9}
$$

Denominator:
$$
334{,}000 \cdot 1000 = 3.34 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step E — Divide and take square root:**

$$
X = \sqrt{5.5875} \approx 2.364 \text{ m}
$$

**Step F — Engineering interpretation:**

The frost front reaches **~2.36 m below the floor** after 6 months. Any footing or refrigerant pipe within this depth is at risk of ice-lens encapsulation and heave damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Action:** Either place the foundation below 2.36 m, or install floor insulation thick enough to reduce $X$ below the slab/foundation interface.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Heave-Pressure Calculation

> Soil at the freezing front is depressed by $\Delta T = -1.5 °C$ relative to 0 °C. Compute the ice-lens pressure on overlying soil.

**Using the simplified Clapeyron form:**

$$
P_{lens} = 12{,}200 \cdot \Delta T = 12{,}200 \cdot 1.5 = 18{,}300 \text{ kPa}
$$

Converting to MPa:

$$
P_{lens} = 18.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Trap:** Students often forget that $\Delta T$ in the Clapeyron equation is **the depression below the bulk freezing point**, not the full surface-to-soil temperature difference. $\Delta T$ for lens pressure is typically **0.1 to 2.0 °C**, not tens of degrees.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define frost heave. List the three primary factors controlling frost heave in cold storage foundations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Frost heave is the upward displacement of ground and overlying structures caused by the formation and growth of segregated ice lenses within frost-susceptible soils when subjected to sub-freezing temperatures.

**Three primary controlling factors (1 mark each):**
1. **Soil frost susceptibility** — governed by grain size distribution; silt-sized soils are most susceptible.
2. **Water supply availability** — depth to groundwater table; closer water table increases migration and heave.
3. **Temperature regime** — magnitude and duration of sub-zero surface temperature; colder and longer produces deeper frost and more heave.

A fourth acceptable factor is **overburden pressure** (concrete floor + racked goods), which suppresses heave.

---

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**State the Clausius–Clapeyron equation as applied to ice-lens pressure and explain the physical meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dT} = -\dfrac{L_f}{T \cdot \Delta v}
$$

**Term-by-term meaning (1 mark each, three selected):**

- $L_f$ — Latent heat of fusion of water (**334 kJ/kg**); the energy released per kilogram when water crystallizes to ice.
- $T$ — Absolute temperature at the freezing front (in Kelvin).
- $\Delta v$ — Difference in specific volume between ice and water (**9.05 × 10⁻⁵ m³/kg**); represents the expansion upon freezing.
- $dP/dT$ — Rate of change of pressure with temperature; gives the ice-lens pressure for a given freezing-point depression.

**Physical meaning:** The equation quantifies how much over-pressure a growing ice lens can exert on surrounding soil for every 1 °C of freezing-point depression.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A: Full 14-Mark Question**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of ice lens formation in frost-susceptible soil beneath a cold storage floor. Discuss the role of capillary suction, freezing-point depression, and cryogenic suction.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A frozen-food warehouse floor is maintained at −25 °C. The soil has thermal conductivity $k_f = 2.2 \text{ W/(m·K)}$, latent heat $L_f = 334{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$, and water density $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The initial soil temperature is +8 °C. Using the Stefan equation, calculate the frost depth after 90 days of continuous operation. If a foundation footing is placed at 1.8 m depth, will it be safe? Recommend a mitigation strategy.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Sketch (2 marks):** The student should draw a vertical cross-section showing:
- Refrigerated floor slab at top
- Insulation layer
- Freezing front (0 °C isotherm)
- Series of horizontal ice lenses
- Capillary fringe above water table
- Groundwater reservoir at bottom

**Mechanism explanation (5 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Heat transfer:** Cold propagates downward; freezing front advances into soil (Stefan equation applies).
2. **Freezing-point depression:** Pore water in narrow capillaries has depressed freezing point due to curved ice–water interfaces (Gibbs–Thomson effect), so it stays liquid below 0 °C.
3. **Capillary suction:** Small pores generate high suction (Young–Laplace), pulling water from below toward the cold front through the unfrozen film coating soil grains.
4. **Cryogenic suction:** The colder ice-lens surface creates a temperature-driven potential gradient that supplements capillary suction (Miller's secondary heave theory).
5. **Ice lens growth:** Migrated water freezes in horizontal sheets perpendicular to heat flow; each new lens exerts upward pressure, displacing the overlying soil and floor.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**

$$
T_s = -25 °C, \quad T_0 = +8 °C, \quad k_f = 2.2 \text{ W/(m·K)}, \quad L_f = 334{,}000 \text{ J/kg}, \quad \rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute temperature differential (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta T_s = T_0 - T_s = 8 - (-25) = 33 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert time to seconds (1 mark):**

$$
t = 90 \times 86400 = 7.776 \times 10^6 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Stefan equation (2 marks):**

$$
X = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot k_f \cdot \Delta T_s \cdot t}{L_f \cdot \rho_w}}
$$

$$
X = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 33 \cdot 7.776 \times 10^{6}}{334{,}000 \cdot 1000}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator (1 mark):**

$$
2 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 33 \cdot 7.776 \times 10^{6} = 1.1293 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute denominator (0.5 marks):**

$$
334{,}000 \cdot 1000 = 3.34 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation (0.5 marks):**

$$
X = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.1293 \times 10^{9}}{3.34 \times 10^{8}}} = \sqrt{3.381} \approx 1.839 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion and recommendation (1 mark):**

The frost depth after 90 days is approximately **1.84 m**, which is **just below the 1.8 m footing**. The footing is at the edge of frost penetration and is therefore **NOT safe** for long-term operation (after ~90 days the heave zone encroaches on the footing). 

**Recommended mitigation:** Install a 100–150 mm thick XPS insulation board under the floor slab, or place the footing at a minimum depth of 2.5 m, or install electric heat-trace cables along the foundation perimeter to keep the freezing front above the footing.

---

#### **Question B-B: Alternative Full 14-Mark Question**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Classify soils according to their frost susceptibility as per Beskow's criterion. Why is silty soil considered the most frost-susceptible? What is the role of the segregation potential (SP) in heave-rate prediction?

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the Clausius–Clapeyron equation for ice-lens pressure starting from the equality of Gibbs free energies. A frost front shows a freezing-point depression of 1.8 °C. Compute the maximum heave pressure using $L_f = 334 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and $T_0 = 273.15 \text{ K}$. State the engineering implication for floor slab design.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Beskow's classification table (3 marks):** Draw or describe the table showing non-susceptible gravel, low-susceptibility medium sand, moderate fine sand, high-susceptibility silt, and variable clay.

**Why silt is most susceptible (2 marks):**

Silt grains (0.002–0.05 mm) sit in a "Goldilocks zone":
- **Fine enough** to develop high capillary suction (suction rises with decreasing pore size).
- **Coarse enough** to permit rapid hydraulic conductivity (permeability falls with decreasing pore size).
- The combination maximizes the **product** of suction × permeability, which is the governing term in the Darcy-like flow of pore water to the freezing front.
- Pure clay has higher suction but nearly zero permeability; pure sand has high permeability but negligible suction. Silt balances both.

**Segregation Potential (SP) (2 marks):**
- Defined by **Konrad & Morgenstern (1980)** as the ratio of heave rate to thermal gradient in the frozen fringe.
- Units: **mm²/(°C·day)**.
- Empirical constant measured in lab; allows direct computation of heave rate as:

$$
\dfrac{dh}{dt} = SP \cdot \nabla T
$$

- SP enables engineers to predict total heave over a season without detailed multiphysics simulation.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (5 marks):** Follow the 10-step derivation given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student should arrive at:

$$
P_{lens} = \dfrac{L_f \cdot \Delta T}{\Delta v \cdot T_0}
$$

Mark distribution:
- Setting up $g_{ice} = g_{water}$ (1 mark)
- Differentiating and applying Maxwell relations (1 mark)
- Introducing $L_f = T \cdot \Delta s$ (1 mark)
- Rearranging to canonical form (1 mark)
- Integrating and simplifying to linear form (1 mark)

**Numerical computation (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{lens} = \dfrac{334{,}000 \cdot 1.8}{9.05 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 273.15}
$$

$$
P_{lens} = \dfrac{601{,}200}{0.02472} \approx 24{,}320{,}000 \text{ Pa} \approx 24.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

Or using the shortcut formula:

$$
P_{lens} \approx 12{,}200 \cdot 1.8 = 21{,}960 \text{ kPa} \approx 22.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Engineering implication (0.5 marks):**

Such pressures exceed the compressive strength of typical floor concrete (~25–40 MPa) and far exceed soil bearing capacity. Therefore, the **floor slab and insulation design must prevent ice-lens formation entirely** — not resist the heave pressure. This is achieved by keeping the freezing front above the foundation level using insulation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing frost depth with frost heave amount.** The Stefan equation gives *frost penetration depth* ($X$ in metres), **NOT** vertical heave in millimetres. Heave is typically 5–15% of frost depth; do not report $X$ as heave.
>
> 2. **Wrong sign in Clapeyron equation.** $dP/dT$ is **negative** because $v_{ice} > v_{water}$. The pressure increases as temperature drops — your sign convention must reflect this.
>
> 3. **Using $\Delta T$ incorrectly.** For Stefan equation, $\Delta T$ is the *surface* temperature differential. For Clapeyron ice-lens pressure, $\Delta T$ is the *freezing-point depression at the lens interface* (typically 0.1 to 2.0 °C, **not 20–30 °C**).
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert days to seconds** in the Stefan equation — the most common numerical error in KTU papers.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Modified Stefan correction.** If the unfrozen soil sensible heat is significant (high initial $T_0$), use the modified form with $k_f / k_u$ ratio; otherwise answer is undervalued.
>
> 6. **Confusing ice-lens pressure with frost heave displacement.** Pressure is a *force per unit area*; displacement is *length*. The two are related but distinct.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

**Core Definitions:**
- Frost heave = upward soil/structure displacement from ice-lens growth
- Ice lens = discrete horizontal ice crystal layer in soil
- Frost susceptibility = soil tendency to form ice lenses
- Freezing-point depression = depression of 0 °C due to pore curvature and solutes
- Segregation potential = lab-measured heave-rate parameter

**Mandatory Equations to Memorize:**
- Clapeyron: $P_{lens} = L_f \cdot \Delta T / (T_0 \cdot \Delta v)$ and the shortcut $P_{lens} \approx 12{,}200 \cdot \Delta T$ (kPa, °C)
- Stefan: $X = \sqrt{2 k_f \Delta T_s t / (L_f \rho_w)}$
- Heave rate: $dh/dt = SP \cdot \nabla T$
- Freezing-point depression: $\Delta T_f = -R T_f^2 \ln(X_w) / (L_f M)$

**Critical Numerical Values:**
- $L_f = 334 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{ice} = 917 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\Delta v = 9.05 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- Volume expansion on freezing = **9%**

**Frost-Susceptibility Hierarchy (Beskow):**
- **Most susceptible:** Silt (0.002–0.05 mm)
- **Moderately susceptible:** Fine sand
- **Least susceptible:** Gravel, coarse sand, stiff clay

**Three Stages of Heave:**
- Stage I — Pore freezing in place
- Stage II — Segregated ice-lens growth (design-critical)
- Stage III — Hard-frozen steady state

**Engineering Mitigation Methods:**
1. Floor insulation (XPS / PIR / PUR)
2. Electric heat-trace cables
3. Gravel coarse drainage pad
4. Thermosyphon coolers
5. Vapour barriers
6. Deep foundations below frost line
7. Soil replacement with non-frost-susceptible material

**Real-World Cold-Storage Design Rule of Thumb:**
> The frost front must remain **above** the foundation footing for the entire design life. Typical XPS floor insulation thickness for a −25 °C cold store on silty soil is **150–200 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychrometry Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Psychrometry Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Psychrometry** (from Greek *psychros* = cold, *metron* = measure) is the branch of engineering thermodynamics that deals with the study of **thermodynamic properties of moist air** (a binary mixture of **dry air** and **water vapour**) treated as a single homogeneous gas. It establishes the quantitative relationship between the **dry-bulb temperature**, **moisture content**, **relative humidity**, **enthalpy**, and **specific volume** of air–vapour mixtures, especially in the temperature range relevant to food processing (**0 °C to 60 °C**).

In the context of food refrigeration and air-conditioning engineering, psychrometry provides the analytical framework for designing **controlled atmosphere storage, food dehydration systems, cold storage humidification, ripening chambers, and clean-room processing environments** where precise moisture control is mandatory to inhibit microbial growth, enzymatic browning, and lipid oxidation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim-aligned):**
> "Psychrometry deals with the measurement and behaviour of air–water vapour mixtures. It is the foundation for evaluating heating, cooling, humidification, dehumidification, and mixing processes encountered in food industry HVAC and cold chain engineering."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a sponge: a *dry* sponge can soak up a lot of water before it is "full." The **atmosphere is like a giant invisible sponge for water vapour** — it has a *holding capacity* that depends on its temperature. Hotter air is a *bigger* sponge (it can hold more water), and cooler air is a *smaller* sponge.

- The **water it is already holding** = humidity ratio, *W* (kg water / kg dry air).
- The **total water it can hold** at that temperature = *Ws* (saturation humidity ratio).
- The ratio *W/Ws* = **relative humidity** (how "full" the air is).
- If we keep cooling the sponge-air, eventually it overflows (condensation) — that temperature is the **dew point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in food industry:** Microbial growth on bakery products, dried fruits, and meat is governed by *water activity (aw)*, which is directly related to the **equilibrium relative humidity (ERH)** of the surrounding air. Controlling RH via psychrometric processes is therefore a *food safety imperative*, not just a comfort parameter.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Memorability)

- **Specific gas constant for dry air**: *Ra* = **0.287 kJ/kg·K (287 J/kg·K)**
- **Specific gas constant for water vapour**: *Rv* = **0.4615 kJ/kg·K**
- **Molecular weight of dry air**: *Ma* = **28.97 kg/kg-mol**
- **Molecular weight of water vapour**: *Mv* = **18.02 kg/kg-mol**
- **Ratio of molecular weights** (Mv/Ma): **0.622** ← *(the most recurring constant in psychrometry)*
- **Specific heat of dry air (cp,a)**: **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific heat of water vapour (cp,v)**: **1.88 kJ/kg·K**
- **Latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C**: **2500 kJ/kg**
- **Universal gas constant**: *Ru* = **8.314 kJ/kg-mol·K**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure**: *P* = **101.325 kPa** (≈ 1 atm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Industry Insight — Water Activity (aw):**
> Water activity *aw* ≈ ERH/100. Most spoilage bacteria require *aw* > 0.91, yeasts > 0.88, and moulds > 0.70. A psychrometric calculation of storage-room RH is the *first step* in HACCP-compliant storage design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Psychrometric Properties (The "Big Six")

Every air–vapour mixture in food industry HVAC can be fully characterised by **six interdependent properties**, conventionally read on the standard **psychrometric (Carrier) chart**:

1. **Dry-Bulb Temperature (DBT or *t*)** — The temperature measured by an ordinary mercury/bimetallic thermometer whose bulb is exposed to a stream of air not exposed to radiation. It is the *true* air temperature. **Units: °C or K.**
