2. **Wet-Bulb Temperature (WBT or *tw*)** — The steady-state temperature reached when air flows over a water-wetted thermometer bulb. Heat lost by evaporation equals the sensible heat gained by the air. **WBT < DBT** unless air is saturated (100% RH), in which case WBT = DBT = DPT. **Units: °C.**

3. **Dew Point Temperature (DPT or *tdp*)** — The temperature at which the water vapour in the air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) and begins to condense, upon cooling at constant pressure. Beyond DPT, water would deposit on cold surfaces (frost on evaporator coils, condensate on chilled food surfaces). **Units: °C.**

4. **Humidity Ratio / Specific Humidity (*W*)** — The mass of water vapour associated with *unit mass of dry air*. **Units: kg water vapour / kg dry air.** This is the **most useful mass-basis property** for mass-balance psychrometric calculations.

5. **Relative Humidity (RH or φ)** — The ratio of the *actual* partial pressure of water vapour (*Pv*) to the *saturation* pressure of water vapour (*Pg*) at the same DBT. **Dimensionless (0 to 1, or 0% to 100%).**

6. **Specific Enthalpy (*h*)** — The total energy content of the moist air per kg of dry air, referenced to **0 °C for both dry air and water vapour**. **Units: kJ/kg dry air.**

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation of the Core Relations

#### Step 1: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures
For the air–vapour mixture behaving as an ideal gas at total pressure *P*:

$$P = P_a + P_v$$

where *Pa* = partial pressure of dry air, *Pv* = partial pressure of water vapour.

#### Step 2: Humidity Ratio (W) Derivation
Applying the ideal gas equation to each component on a molar basis, the mole fraction of vapour = *Pv/P*. Converting to mass ratio using molecular weights:

$$W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$

> The factor **0.622** arises directly from *Mv/Ma = 18.02/28.97 ≈ 0.622* — this is the single most important dimensionless constant in psychrometry.

#### Step 3: Degree of Saturation (*μ*)
Defined as the ratio of the actual humidity ratio to the humidity ratio at saturation at the *same DBT*:

$$\mu = \frac{W}{W_s}$$

where *Ws* = 0.622 × *Pg* / (*P* − *Pg*). This is NOT the same as RH; they differ because *Pv* is not exactly proportional to *W* unless *P* >> *Pv*.

#### Step 4: Enthalpy of Moist Air
Taking reference at 0 °C, with *h* expressed per kg **dry air**:

$$h = h_a + W \cdot h_v = c_{p,a} \cdot t + W \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot t)$$

$$h = 1.005\,t + W\,(2500 + 1.88\,t) \quad \text{[kJ/kg dry air]}$$

> The first term (**1.005 t**) is the *sensible* heat of dry air. The second (**W × (2500 + 1.88 t)**) is the *latent* heat of vapour plus its sensible heat.

#### Step 5: Specific Volume (*v*)
The volume per kg of *dry* air of the moist air mixture:

$$v = \frac{R_a \, T}{P_a} = \frac{R_a \, T}{P - P_v} = \frac{R_a \, T}{P}\,(1 + 1.6078\,W) \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/kg dry air]}$$

> The factor **1.6078** comes from (1/0.622) × (*Ra/Rv*) correction for humid volume expansion.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | SI Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Humidity Ratio | $W = 0.622 \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg/kg da | Valid for *P* = 101.325 kPa |
| 2 | Saturated Humidity Ratio | $W_s = 0.622 \dfrac{P_g}{P - P_g}$ | kg/kg da | At saturation, *Pv* = *Pg* |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_g} = \dfrac{W}{W_s} \cdot \dfrac{(1+W_s)}{(1+W)}$ | % or – | At DBT |
| 4 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s}$ | – | Always $\mu < \phi$ for *W* < *Ws* |
| 5 | Specific Enthalpy | $h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ | kJ/kg da | Reference: 0 °C |
| 6 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P}(1 + 1.6078\,W)$ | m³/kg da | $R_a$ = 0.287 kJ/kg·K |
| 7 | Dew Point | *tdp* = saturation T at *Pv* | °C | Read from steam tables |
| 8 | Wet-Bulb | Empirical, $t_w$ from chart | °C | Found by energy balance |
| 9 | Humid Heat | $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88\,W$ | kJ/kg da·K | Used in WBT derivation |
| 10 | Humid Volume | $v_H = v$ as above | m³/kg da | Density of moist air inverse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for KTU Problems:**
> If RH is given, compute *Pv* = *φ · Pg* (with *Pg* read at **DBT**, not WBT), then plug into equation (1) to get *W*. This *W* is the **actual** humidity ratio — used everywhere downstream (enthalpy, density, mass balance).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Industry

| Application | Psychrometric Parameter Controlled | Food Quality Effect |
|-------------|------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Cold Storage (0–4 °C)** | RH = 85–95% | Prevents weight loss (shrinkage) in apples, leafy vegetables |
| **Frozen Storage (–18 °C)** | RH = 90–95% | Prevents freezer burn (sublimation) in meat, fish |
| **Spray Drying of Milk** | Inlet air DBT 180–200 °C, RH < 5% | Drives evaporation; outlet RH = 10–25% |
| **Banana Ripening** | DBT 15–20 °C, RH 90–95%, C₂H₄ 100–150 ppm | Ethylene-humidity synergy for uniform ripening |
| **Bakery Proofing** | DBT 35–40 °C, RH 75–85% | Yeast activity and crust development |
| **Dry Sausage Curing** | DBT 12–15 °C, RH 75–80% | Controlled *aw* drop to inhibit pathogens |
| **Maple Syrup Concentration** | Outlet RH < 20% at 80 °C | Achieves 67° Brix safely |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** Modern food plants use **on-line psychrometers** (capacitance RH sensors + Pt100 RTD) integrated with PLC/DCS for real-time *aw* monitoring — a direct application of the equations above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Psychrometric (Wet-Bulb) Equation

The Wet-Bulb Temperature (*tw*) is defined thermodynamically by the *steady-state* energy balance on a small water wick fed by an infinite air stream. Let us derive the relationship that allows *W* to be computed from (*t*, *tw*, *P*).

**Setup (Figure of merit):** Consider 1 kg of dry air in contact with water at temperature *tw*. In the steady state, the latent heat released by evaporating water is exactly balanced by the sensible heat lost by the (cooler-dry-bulb) air.

**Step (a) — Mass of water evaporated per kg dry air:**
If the humidity ratio changes from *W₁* (at the upstream state) to *W₂* (at the saturated state *tw* = *tw*), the water picked up is:

$$dW = W_s(t_w) - W_1$$

**Step (b) — Latent heat absorbed for evaporation:**

$$Q_{latent} = (W_s - W) \cdot h_{fg}(t_w)$$

**Step (c) — Sensible heat lost by air (from DBT *t* to WBT *tw*):**

$$Q_{sensible} = c_{p,a} \cdot (t - t_w) + W \cdot c_{p,v} \cdot (t - t_w) = (1.005 + 1.88\,W)(t - t_w)$$

> The factor (1.005 + 1.88 W) is the **humid heat** *cs*.

**Step (d) — Equating the two in steady state:**

$$(1.005 + 1.88\,W)(t - t_w) = (W_s - W)\,h_{fg}(t_w)$$

**Step (e) — Solving for the humidity ratio W:**

Rearranging and isolating *W* on the left:

$$(1.005 + 1.88\,W)(t - t_w) = W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w) - W \cdot h_{fg}(t_w)$$

$$W\left[1.88(t - t_w) + h_{fg}(t_w)\right] = W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w) - 1.005(t - t_w)$$

$$W = \dfrac{1.005(t - t_w) - W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w)}{h_{fg}(t_w) - 1.88(t - t_w)} \cdot (-1)$$

A more conventionally written form (rearranged to put *W* on top, numerator only positive when *t* > *tw*):

$$\boxed{\,W = \dfrac{(1.005)(t - t_w) + W_s\,h_{fg}(t_w)}{h_{fg}(t_w) - 1.88(t - t_w)}\,}$$

**Validation of signs:** Since WBT < DBT, (*t* − *tw*) > 0; the denominator *hfg*(*tw*) is large (~2400–2500 kJ/kg), much bigger than 1.88 (*t* − *tw*) < 100, so denominator is always positive. Numerator is also positive → *W* > 0. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Food-Industry Significance:** This equation is the *theoretical backbone* of sling-psychrometer readings. Quality-assurance technicians in food plants use a calibrated sling-psychrometer daily to verify HVAC sensor calibration in cold rooms.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Problem)

**Problem [Adapted from KTU 2019 Scheme — Module 3]:**
In a cold storage chamber, air is at DBT **30 °C** and WBT **20 °C**. Atmospheric pressure = **101.325 kPa**. Determine: (a) Humidity Ratio, (b) Relative Humidity, (c) Specific Enthalpy, (d) Specific Volume, (e) Dew Point Temperature.

**Given Data (from steam tables):**
- *Pg*(30 °C) = 4.246 kPa
- *Pg*(20 °C) = 2.339 kPa
- *hfg*(20 °C) = 2458.0 kJ/kg
- *W*s at 20 °C = 0.622 × 2.339 / (101.325 − 2.339) = 0.01468 kg/kg da

**(a) Humidity Ratio W:**

$$W = \dfrac{(1.005)(30 - 20) + (0.01468)(2458.0)}{2458.0 - (1.88)(30 - 20)}$$

$$W = \dfrac{10.05 + 36.08}{2458.0 - 18.8} = \dfrac{46.13}{2439.2}$$

$$W = 0.01891 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}$$

**(b) Partial pressure of vapour:**

$$P_v = \dfrac{W \cdot P}{0.622 + W} = \dfrac{0.01891 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.01891} = \dfrac{1.916}{0.6409}$$

$$P_v = 2.990 \text{ kPa}$$

**Relative Humidity:**

$$\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_g(30\,°C)} = \dfrac{2.990}{4.246} = 0.7042 = 70.42\%$$

**(c) Specific Enthalpy:**

$$h = 1.005(30) + (0.01891)\big(2500 + 1.88(30)\big)$$

$$h = 30.15 + 0.01891 \times 2556.4 = 30.15 + 48.34$$

$$h = 78.49 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**(d) Specific Volume (using *T* = 303 K):**

$$v = \dfrac{0.287 \times 303}{101.325 - 2.990} = \dfrac{86.96}{98.335}$$

$$v = 0.8843 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg dry air}$$

**(e) Dew Point Temperature:** The temperature at which *Pg* = *Pv* = **2.990 kPa**.

From steam tables: at 24 °C, *Pg* = 2.985 kPa; at 24.1 °C, *Pg* ≈ 3.00 kPa. By linear interpolation:

$$t_{dp} \approx 24.0\,°C + \dfrac{(2.990 - 2.985)}{(3.000 - 2.985)} \times 0.1 = 24.03\,°C$$

$$\boxed{t_{dp} \approx 24.0\,°C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Dew Point Quick Method:** Without tables, use Antoine equation $\log_{10} P_g = A - \dfrac{B}{C + t}$ with constants A = 8.07131, B = 1730.63, C = 233.426 (valid 1–100 °C, *Pg* in mmHg). Convert to kPa via × 0.13332.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reading the Psychrometric Chart

| If you know … | Then you can find … | Method |
|---------------|---------------------|--------|
| DBT + WBT | All properties | WBT line is the *adiabatic saturation* line; trace to saturation curve to read *Ws* |
| DBT + RH | W, h, v, tdp | From DBT go horizontally to φ curve, then drop vertically to read *W* and *h* |
| DBT + DPT | W, RH | From DBT go vertically down (constant *W* line) to intersect DPT isotherm; read *W* and φ |
| DBT + W | All properties | Constant *W* vertical line from DBT to saturation curve |
| DBT + h | W, RH | Slope = −1/*cs*; tilt along constant-h line to saturation curve |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Psychrometric Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measured Inputs: DBT and WBT] --> B[Steam Table Lookup]
    A --> C[Barometric Pressure Sensor]
    B --> D[Compute Saturation Ws at WBT]
    D --> E[Apply Psychrometric Equation]
    A --> E
    C --> E
    E --> F[Humidity Ratio W]
    F --> G[Partial Pressure Pv]
    F --> H[Specific Enthalpy h]
    F --> I[Specific Volume v]
    G --> J[Relative Humidity phi]
    G --> K[Dew Point tdp Lookup]
    K --> L[Final Psychrometric State Vector]
    J --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    L --> M[Food Industry Decision]
    M --> M1[Cold Storage RH Check]
    M --> M2[Drying Process Design]
    M --> M3[AC Load Calculation]
    M --> M4[Water Activity Correlation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The "state vector" *L* = {W, h, v, φ, tdp, t} is the *complete* psychrometric signature of the air sample. In food industry digital twins, this vector is updated every 10–60 seconds and fed to the building-management system (BMS).

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Psychrometric Processes in Food-Industry HVAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[State 1: Outdoor Air DBT 35 RH 60 percent] --> P1[Sensible Heating via Coil]
    P1 --> P2[State 2: Heated Air DBT 45 RH 18 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Adiabatic Saturation via Water Spray]
    P3 --> P4[State 3: Cooled and Humidified DBT 28 WBT 26]
    P4 --> P5[Cooling and Dehumidification via Chilled Coil]
    P5 --> P6[State 4: Supply Air DBT 12 RH 95 percent]
    P6 --> P7[Space Load Pickup in Cold Room]
    P7 --> P8[State 5: Return Air DBT 18 RH 70 percent]
    P8 --> P9[Mixing with Outdoor Fresh Air 20 percent]
    P9 --> P0
```

**Process Identification Table (KTU Board-Favourite Question Type):**

| Process | Lines on Chart | What Changes | What Stays Constant |
|---------|----------------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sensible Heating / Cooling | Horizontal | DBT, h | W, tdp, v (approx.) |
| Humidification (water spray) | Along constant *h* (WBT line) | W ↑, t ↓ | h, tw (approx.) |
| Dehumidification | Across saturation curve | W ↓, h ↓ | Sub-cooling |
| Cooling & Dehumidification | Down-left, crossing φ = 100% | W ↓, t ↓, h ↓ | DPT may change |
| Heating & Humidification | Up-right | W ↑, t ↑, h ↑ | — |
| Mixing of two air streams | Straight line on W vs. h chart | Mass-weighted avg | W is linear average |

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Cross-Linking Psychrometry to Food Science

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Condition DBT, RH] --> B[Equilibrium Relative Humidity ERH]
    B --> C[Water Activity of Food aw]
    C --> D1[Microbial Growth Boundary]
    C --> D2[Enzymatic Browning Rate]
    C --> D3[Lipid Oxidation Kinetics]
    C --> D4[Texture Moisture Migration]
    D1 --> E[Shelf Life Prediction]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[HACCP Compliant Storage Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Food-Industry Insight:** The classical **Labuza sorption isotherm** *aw* vs. moisture content, when combined with psychrometric RH, gives the *critical equilibrium moisture content* for crisp biscuits, dried milk, or freeze-dried coffee — this dictates the **storage RH setpoint** in food warehouses.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define the following terms with one example each from the food industry: (a) Dry Bulb Temperature, (b) Dew Point Temperature, (c) Humidity Ratio.

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

- **(a) DBT (1 mark):** The temperature of air measured by a standard thermometer shielded from radiation and moisture. *Example:* A cold room at 4 °C DBT stores apples.
- **(b) DPT (1 mark):** The temperature at which water vapour in the air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) and begins to condense on a cooling surface. *Example:* When warm humid air (DBT 30 °C, RH 70%) enters a cold room at 4 °C, moisture condenses on the evaporator coil and chilled food surfaces — the coil surface temperature is below the DPT (~24 °C).
- **(c) Humidity Ratio *W* (1 mark):** Mass of water vapour (in kg) per kg of dry air. *Example:* *W* = 0.018 kg/kg da in the cold storage air of the earlier numerical example.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Differentiate between *Relative Humidity* and *Degree of Saturation*. Why are they unequal at sub-saturated conditions?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Relative Humidity φ (1 mark):** Ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapour to saturation pressure at the **DBT**.
$$\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_g(t)}$$

- **Degree of Saturation μ (1 mark):** Ratio of actual humidity ratio to saturated humidity ratio at the same DBT.
$$\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s}$$

- **Why unequal (1 mark):** The relationship between *W* and *Pv* is non-linear (*W* = 0.622 *Pv* / (*P* − *Pv*)). Hence the ratio of *W*-values is not the same as the ratio of *Pv*-values. Only at *P* >> *Pv* (i.e., *W* ≪ 0.622) do φ ≈ μ. Numerically, φ > μ for any *W* < *Ws* at standard atmospheric pressure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 3, 14 marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the working principle of a **sling psychrometer** and explain how it is used to determine the relative humidity of air in a food cold storage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Air at DBT 35 °C and RH 50% enters an air washer in a fruit ripening chamber. The air leaves the washer saturated at 25 °C. Some of this air is then reheated to 32 °C before entering the ripening chamber. Calculate: (i) The humidity ratio of the inlet air, (ii) The rate of water added per kg of dry air in the washer, (iii) The RH of the reheated air at 32 °C, (iv) The enthalpy change of the air across the washer. Take *P* = 101.325 kPa. (Use: *Pg*(35 °C) = 5.628 kPa, *Pg*(25 °C) = 3.169 kPa, *hfg*(25 °C) = 2442 kJ/kg.)

---

**Model Solution for Q3A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A sling psychrometer consists of **two identical mercury/bimetallic thermometers** mounted on a common frame (1 mark). The bulb of one thermometer (the *wet bulb*) is covered with a muslin wick soaked in distilled water; the other remains dry (the *dry bulb*) (1 mark). The frame is whirled manually or by a motor at ~5 m/s relative air velocity for 60–90 seconds (1 mark).

Working principle (2 marks): As air passes over the wet bulb, water evaporates by absorbing the latent heat of vaporisation from the bulb itself, cooling the wet-bulb thermometer. In steady state, the rate of heat loss by evaporation equals the rate of sensible heat transfer from the air, fixing the **wet-bulb temperature**. The *dry-bulb* thermometer records the true air temperature since it is dry. The *wet-bulb depression* (DBT − WBT) is then used to look up a psychrometric table or chart to read **RH** (1 mark). In a food cold storage, RH must be 85–95% for fresh produce; a sling psychrometer is the cheapest, most reliable field instrument for HACCP verification (1 mark).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Humidity ratio of inlet air (State 1):** (2 marks)

$$P_{v1} = 0.50 \times 5.628 = 2.814 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.814}{101.325 - 2.814} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{2.814}{98.511} = 0.01777 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 2: Humidity ratio of air leaving washer (State 2 — saturated at 25 °C):** (1 mark)

$$P_{v2} = P_g(25\,°C) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$$

$$W_2 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.169}{101.325 - 3.169} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.169}{98.156} = 0.02008 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3: Water added per kg dry air in the washer:** (1 mark)

$$\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 = 0.02008 - 0.01777 = 0.00231 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}$$

**Step 4: RH of reheated air (State 3 — 32 °C, same *W*):** (1 mark)

$$P_g(32\,°C) \approx 4.760 \text{ kPa (interpolated)}$$

$$P_{v3} = \dfrac{W_2 \cdot P}{0.622 + W_2} = \dfrac{0.02008 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.02008} = 3.165 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\phi_3 = \dfrac{3.165}{4.760} = 66.5\%$$

**Step 5: Enthalpy change across washer:** (2 marks)

$$h_1 = 1.005(35) + 0.01777(2500 + 1.88 \times 35) = 35.175 + 0.01777 \times 2565.8 = 35.175 + 45.59 = 80.77 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005(25) + 0.02008(2500 + 1.88 \times 25) = 25.125 + 0.02008 \times 2547.0 = 25.125 + 51.14 = 76.27 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 76.27 - 80.77 = -4.50 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

> (The negative sign indicates the air loses enthalpy in the washer — primarily due to evaporation of recirculated water, idealised as an *adiabatic saturation* process; the small energy balance residual is the makeup-water energy.)

**Final Answer Box:**
- (i) *W*₁ = **0.01777 kg/kg da**
- (ii) Δ*W* = **0.00231 kg water/kg da**
- (iii) φ₃ = **66.5%**
- (iv) Δ*h* = **−4.50 kJ/kg da** (cooling)

---

**Q3B. [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the term **"specific enthalpy of moist air"** and derive the expression $h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ kJ/kg dry air. State clearly the reference state used.

**(b) [7 marks]** The air in a food dehydration tunnel has DBT 80 °C and RH 8%. Calculate: (i) Humidity ratio, (ii) Dew point temperature, (iii) Specific enthalpy, (iv) Specific volume of the moist air. Take *P* = 101.325 kPa. Use steam-table data: *Pg*(80 °C) = 47.39 kPa.

---

**Model Solution for Q3B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Specific enthalpy of moist air is the total energy content (sensible + latent) per unit mass of *dry air*, expressed in kJ/kg of dry air, with the reference state chosen as **0 °C for both dry air and water vapour (in saturated liquid state)**.

**Derivation (5 marks):** The moist air mixture contains 1 kg of dry air + *W* kg of water vapour. The enthalpy of this mixture (relative to 0 °C reference) is:

$$h = h_{\text{dry air}} + W \cdot h_{\text{water vapour}}$$

For dry air heated from 0 °C to *t* °C:

$$h_a = c_{p,a} \cdot t = 1.005\,t \quad \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

For water vapour at temperature *t* (with reference as saturated liquid at 0 °C), the enthalpy includes both latent heat at 0 °C (*hfg,0* = 2500 kJ/kg) and sensible heat from 0 °C to *t*:

$$h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot t = 2500 + 1.88\,t \quad \text{kJ/kg vapour}$$

Multiplying by *W* (kg vapour per kg dry air) and adding:

$$h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t) \quad \text{kJ/kg dry air} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Partial pressure of vapour (1 mark):**

$$P_v = 0.08 \times 47.39 = 3.791 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Humidity ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$W = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.791}{101.325 - 3.791} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.791}{97.534} = 0.02417 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3: Dew point temperature (1.5 marks):** Find saturation T at *Pg* = 3.791 kPa. From steam tables: at 28 °C, *Pg* = 3.778 kPa; at 29 °C, *Pg* = 4.045 kPa.

$$t_{dp} = 28 + \dfrac{(3.791 - 3.778)}{(4.045 - 3.778)} \times 1 = 28.05\,°C \approx 28.0\,°C$$

**Step 4: Specific enthalpy (1.5 marks):**

$$h = 1.005(80) + 0.02417(2500 + 1.88 \times 80)$$

$$h = 80.40 + 0.02417 \times 2650.4 = 80.40 + 64.06 = 144.46 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Step 5: Specific volume (1.5 marks):** With *T* = 353 K:

$$v = \dfrac{0.287 \times 353}{101.325 - 3.791} = \dfrac{101.31}{97.534} = 1.0387 \text{ m}^3\text{/kg dry air}$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- (i) *W* = **0.02417 kg/kg da**
- (ii) *t*dp = **28.0 °C**
- (iii) *h* = **144.46 kJ/kg da**
- (iv) *v* = **1.039 m³/kg da**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Steam-table interpolation is mandatory** (1 mark deduction if skipped). Always show 2–3 line interpolations.
> 2. **Use DBT (not WBT) for *Pg* in RH formula.** Many students mistakenly use *Pg* at WBT — full 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Partial pressure via W formula requires inversion** for many numerical answers. Use the *two* equivalent forms:
> $$P_v = \dfrac{W \cdot P}{0.622 + W} \quad \text{AND} \quad W = 0.622 \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$
> Mark allocation usually includes 1 mark for correct formula selection.
> 4. **Reference state confusion:** Enthalpy reference is 0 °C; do NOT use 0 K (gas constant *RaT* uses 0 K in gas tables, but psychrometric *h* uses 0 °C).
> 5. **Mixing of two streams:** Always plot or write the mass-weighted average for *W* and *h* on a psychrometric chart — the mixed state lies on the straight line joining the two stream states, *not* on the WBT line.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Psychrometry** = study of air + water vapour as a single homogeneous gas.
- **Six key properties** = DBT, WBT, DPT, W, RH, h (plus v and μ).
- **Magic number 0.622** appears in *every* W-formula — memorise the origin (*Mv/Ma*).
- **Humidity ratio *W*** is the master mass-basis property; all other quantities can be derived from (*t*, *W*, *P*).
- **Sling psychrometer** measures DBT + WBT → gives all properties via chart/table.
- **DPT** is read from steam tables at *Pv*; **WBT** is found from adiabatic-saturation energy balance.
- **Specific enthalpy formula** $h = 1.005\,t + W(2500 + 1.88\,t)$ — reference 0 °C.
- **Specific volume** $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P}(1 + 1.6078\,W)$ — the **1.6078** factor is the humid-volume correction.
- **Relative humidity** φ = *Pv* / *Pg(t)* — the *Pg* is evaluated at **DBT**.
- **Degree of saturation** μ = *W* / *Ws*; always μ < φ when *W* < *Ws* at standard *P*.
- **Food-industry process mapping:** Cold storage (high RH, low DBT); drying (low RH, high DBT); ripening (moderate RH, ethylene); spray drying (very low RH, very high DBT).
- **Chart processes:** horizontal = sensible; vertical = latent; tilted along WBT line ≈ adiabatic saturation; cross of saturation curve = dehumidification.
- **Top KTU question patterns:** (1) Two-property → all other properties, (2) Process identification on chart, (3) Mixing of two streams, (4) Cooling coil Bypass Factor, (5) Air washer / evaporative cooling analysis.
- **Conversion shortcuts:** 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg; 1 kJ/kg·K = 0.2388 Btu/lb·°F.
- **Always show all 4–5 significant figures** in KTU 14-mark numericals; partial-step marks depend on intermediate clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Comfort Air-Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Comfort Air-Conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comfort Air-Conditioning (CAC)** is defined as the simultaneous control of **temperature, humidity, air movement, air purity (ventilation), and radiant heat** within an enclosed space such that at least **80 % of the occupants** experience a state of *thermal neutrality* — neither feeling too warm nor too cool — while performing light, primarily sedentary activity.

The condition is technically evaluated against the **ASHRAE Standard 55 – Thermal Environmental Conditions for Human Occupancy**, which prescribes the **operative temperature**, permissible **relative humidity band**, and **mean radiant temperature limits** that define the human comfort zone.

### The Six Cardinal Environmental Variables

| # | Factor | Primary Controlling Parameter | Unit (SI) |
|---|--------|------------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Air Temperature | Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) | °C or K |
| 2 | Humidity | Relative Humidity (RH) / WBT | % / °C |
| 3 | Air Movement | Air velocity (V) | m/s |
| 4 | Air Purity | CO₂ concentration, dust (PM) | ppm, µg/m³ |
| 5 | Radiant Heat | Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT) | °C |
| 6 | Occupant Activity | Metabolic Rate (MR) | met or W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Out of the six variables above, only the **first three (DBT, RH, air velocity)** are *controllable* by an HVAC system at the supply-air side. The remaining three (MRT, metabolic rate, clothing) are **occupant/architecture-driven** but the system must *compensate* for them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath-Tub of Body Heat"

Imagine your body as a **100-W glowing electric bulb** continually radiating heat into the surrounding air. Now, comfort is achieved when the heat this bulb *gives out* is **exactly equal** to the heat it *loses to the surroundings* — neither more, nor less.

- If the surrounding air is **hot & humid** → bulb cannot lose heat → body feels **stuffy/sweaty** (think of a poorly ventilated kitchen in Kerala monsoon).
- If the surrounding air is **cold & drafty** → bulb loses heat too fast → body feels **chilled** (think of an over-cooled multiplex).
- The **temperature, humidity and air movement** of the room act as the three "valves" the air-conditioner must tune to keep this heat balance perfect.

> [!TIP]
> A useful one-liner for viva: *"Comfort = Heat produced by body = Heat lost to surroundings."* This is the foundation of the **Heat Balance Equation** derived in Section 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factor 1 — Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)

The **DBT** is the temperature of air measured by a standard thermometer shielded from radiation and moisture. It governs the **sensible heat transfer** between the body and the environment.

- **Sensible heat loss** from the body ∝ (Tₛₖᵢₙ − Tₐᵢᵣ) — the temperature gradient between skin (~33 °C) and air.
- For Indian/Kerala tropical climate, KTU-typical comfort DBT lies in the range **24 – 27 °C** (Kerala being hot-humid, slightly higher DBT with lower RH is preferred).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Condition (KCPL/IS 1391):** **DBT = 26.7 °C (80 °F)**, **RH = 50 %**, **Air Velocity = 0.15 m/s** — used in the Indian Standard for summer comfort in offices.

## 2.2 Factor 2 — Relative Humidity (RH) & Dew Point

Humidity quantifies the moisture content of air. **Relative Humidity (φ)** is the ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapour (Pᵥ) to the saturation pressure (Pₛ) at the same DBT.

$$\phi \;=\; \frac{P_{v}}{P_{s}(T_{db})}\;\times\;100\;\text{(\%)}$$

| RH Band | Effect on Occupant |
|---------|-------------------|
| < 30 % | Dry skin, static shocks, respiratory irritation |
| 40 – 55 % | **Comfort zone** (ASHRAE) |
| 60 – 70 % | Sticky, muggy feel, microbial growth in food areas |
| > 75 % | Condensation on surfaces, sweat does not evaporate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** A common exam error is conflating *Relative Humidity* with *Specific Humidity (ω)*. Specific humidity (kg water / kg dry air) is what **changes** when air is cooled below dew point and moisture condenses. RH is only a ratio — it can *rise* even when absolute moisture drops, simply because cool air holds less vapour.

## 2.3 Factor 3 — Air Movement (Velocity)

Air movement aids **convective and evaporative** heat loss from the skin. But there is a **non-linear trade-off**:

- **Low velocity (< 0.05 m/s):** stagnant, body heat builds up locally — "stuffy"
- **Optimum (0.15 – 0.25 m/s):** perceptible but not drafty — ideal for comfort
- **Excessive (> 0.5 m/s):** causes *local discomfort*, the **draft sensation** (especially at neck/ankle)

The convective heat-transfer coefficient is empirically related to air velocity:

$$h_{c} \;\approx\; 10.45\;-\;v\;+\;10\;\sqrt{v} \quad\;[\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}]$$

where *v* is in m/s. This is the **Mitchell equation**, valid for 0 < v < 2.5 m/s.

## 2.4 Factor 4 — Air Purity & Ventilation

The human body exhales **CO₂** (≈ 0.04 L/min at rest, 0.25 L/min during moderate work). A build-up of CO₂ beyond **1000 ppm** produces drowsiness, headaches, and reduced productivity.

**ASHRAE 62.1 – Ventilation Standard** mandates:
- Offices: **8.5 L/s per person** + **0.6 L/s per m²** of floor area
- Conference rooms: **8.5 L/s per person** + **0.6 L/s per m²**
- Food-service kitchens: **3.8 L/s per m²** (grease/dust loaded)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Food-Industry Context (OEFTT612):** In food processing halls, contaminants are *not just* CO₂ — they include **spores, aerosols, product dust (e.g., flour), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs)**. Hence **HEPA filtration** + **higher fresh-air ratio (15–20 %)** is mandated, unlike offices (~10 %).

## 2.5 Factor 5 — Radiant Heat (Mean Radiant Temperature, MRT)

Heat gained or lost by the body via *radiation* depends on the **Mean Radiant Temperature** of the surrounding surfaces, not the air temperature. Two surfaces can have identical DBT but vastly different MRT (e.g., a sun-lit glass wall vs. an insulated one).

$$\text{MRT} \;=\; \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{i}\,T_{i}^{\,4}\right]^{1/4} \quad\;[\text{K}]$$

where Fᵢ is the **angle factor (view factor)** from the occupant toward surface *i*, and Tᵢ is the absolute temperature of that surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operative Temperature (Tₒₚ)** is the *weighted* mean of air temperature and MRT used in comfort standards:
> $$T_{op} \;=\; \frac{h_{c}\,T_{a} \;+\; h_{r}\,\overline{T_{r}}}{h_{c}+h_{r}}$$
> For air velocities **v ≤ 0.2 m/s**, hᵣ ≃ 4.7 W/m²K, and Tₒₚ ≈ (Tₐ + MRT)/2.

## 2.6 Factor 6 — Clothing Insulation (CLO)

Clothing traps a layer of still air next to the skin, reducing heat loss. The unit is **clo** (1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W).

| Garment | clo |
|---------|-----|
| Naked skin | 0.0 |
| Light tropical (shirt + trousers) | 0.5 – 0.6 |
| Standard business suit | 1.0 |
| Heavy winter overcoat | 3.0 – 4.0 |

For **Kerala tropical offices**, a typical clo ≈ **0.5** is assumed in comfort calculations.

## 2.7 Factor 7 — Metabolic Rate (MET)

1 met = **58.2 W/m²** = the metabolic rate of a **seated, resting person**.

| Activity | met |
|----------|-----|
| Sleeping | 0.7 |
| Seated, typing | 1.0 – 1.2 |
| Standing, light work | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Walking (3 km/h) | 2.0 |
| Heavy labour | 3.0 – 5.0 |

## 2.8 📋 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = P_{v}/P_{s}$ | % | ψ = partial / saturation pressure |
| 2 | Specific Humidity | $\omega = 0.622\;P_{v}/(P_{t}-P_{v})$ | kg/kg dry air | $P_{t}$ = total pressure |
| 3 | Sensible Heat Load | $Q_{s} = m\,c_{p}\,(T_{i}-T_{f})$ | kW | $c_{p}$ ≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K |
| 4 | Latent Heat Load | $Q_{L} = m\,h_{fg}\,(\omega_{i}-\omega_{f})$ | kW | $h_{fg}$ ≈ 2501 kJ/kg |
| 5 | Convective Coefficient | $h_{c} = 10.45 - v + 10\sqrt{v}$ | W/m²·K | Mitchell Eqn. |
| 6 | MRT | $MRT = \bigl(\sum F_{i}T_{i}^{4}\bigr)^{1/4}$ | K | View factor weighted |
| 7 | Operative Temperature | $T_{op} = (h_{c}T_{a}+h_{r}MRT)/(h_{c}+h_{r})$ | °C | For low v, hᵣ ≈ 4.7 |
| 8 | Metabolic Rate | 1 met = 58.2 W/m² | W/m² | Resting seated person |
| 9 | Clothing Resistance | 1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W | m²·K/W | Trapped air layer |
| 10 | Heat-Balance on Body | $M - W = (C+R+E_{sk})$ | W/m² | First-law on skin |
| 11 | Effective Temp. Index | $ET \approx T_{wbt}$ for 50 % RH | °C | Houghten-Yaglou |
| 12 | Heat Stress Index | $HSI = (E_{req}/E_{max})\times 100$ | % | Belding Hatch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorise equations (5), (7), and (10) above. These three alone are the basis of 80 % of the 14-mark comfort-AC problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat-Balance Equation on Human Body

We apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the human body taken as a control volume. The body is at thermal steady state, so net energy storage = 0.

### Step 1 — Define Energy Flows

- **M** = Total metabolic heat produced (W/m²)
- **W** = External mechanical work done (W/m²) ≈ 0 for most sedentary cases
- **C** = Heat loss by **convection** to the surrounding air
- **R** = Heat loss by **radiation** to surrounding surfaces
- **Eₛₖ** = Heat loss by **evaporation** of sweat from the skin
- **Cᵣₑₛ** = Heat loss by **respiration** (convective + latent from lungs)
- **Eᵣₑₛ** = Latent heat lost with exhaled moisture

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Energy Balance

Rate of energy in = Rate of energy out

$$
M \;-\; W \;=\; (C + R + E_{sk}) \;+\; (C_{res}+E_{res})
$$

### Step 3 — Express Each Term

**Convection:**
$$
C \;=\; h_{c}\,(T_{skin}-T_{air}) \quad\;[\text{W/m}^{2}]
$$

**Radiation:**
$$
R \;=\; h_{r}\,(\overline{T_{skin}}-\overline{T_{r}})
$$
where $h_{r}=4\varepsilon\sigma\overline{T}^{3} \approx 4.7$ W/m²·K at 30 °C.

**Evaporative Skin Loss** (driven by vapour-pressure difference):
$$
E_{sk} \;=\; h_{e}\,w\,(P_{sk}-P_{a})
$$
where $h_{e}$ = evaporative coefficient (Lewis relation: $h_{e}=16.5\,h_{c}$), $w$ = skin wettedness (0 to 1), $P_{sk}, P_{a}$ = saturated vapour pressure at skin & partial pressure in air.

### Step 4 — Define Operative & Climatic Variables

Using $T_{op}$ and assuming $T_{skin}\approx 33$ °C, the *required* evaporative loss for comfort becomes:
$$
E_{req} \;=\; M-W \;-\; (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(T_{skin}-T_{op})
$$

### Step 5 — Skin-Wettedness Limitation

For a person at rest, $w$ is bounded to **0.06 (no sweating) to 1.0 (fully drenched)**. The minimum required $w$ for thermal neutrality is:
$$
w_{req} \;=\; \frac{E_{req}}{h_{e}\,(P_{sk,sat}-P_{a})}
$$

> If $w_{req} > 1$ → environment **too hot/humid** for comfort, body cannot sweat enough.
> If $w_{req} < 0.06$ → environment **too cold**, body must shiver to maintain heat.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Temperature (ET)

**ET** combines the effects of DBT, humidity, and air velocity into a single *temperature-equivalent* index. Originally developed by **Houghten & Yaglou (1923)**.

### Step 1 — Reference Air Condition

Define **standard air** at: DBT = 17.8 °C, RH = 100 %, v = still (≃ 0.05 m/s). The wet-bulb temperature of this air = DBT = 17.8 °C.

### Step 2 — Procedure (Psycho-Physiological Test)

A large group of subjects is moved between two chambers:
- Chamber A: standard (saturated) air at temperature $T_s$
- Chamber B: test air at DBT $T_{db}$, WBT $T_{wb}$, velocity $v$

They switch chambers until the *sensations of warmth are equal*.

### Step 3 — Resultant Index

The temperature $T_s$ of the standard air at which subjects feel equal warmth is the **Effective Temperature**:

$$
\boxed{\;ET \;=\; T_{s}\big|_{sensations\;equal}\;}
$$

### Step 4 — Practical Approximation

For *moderate conditions*, ET approximates the **Wet-Bulb Temperature** at 50 % RH:

$$
ET \;\approx\; T_{wb}\;(50\%\,RH)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** ET over-emphasises humidity at low temperatures and under-emphasises it at high temperatures. Hence ASHRAE replaced it with the **Standard Effective Temperature (SET)**, which uses a more rigorous two-node human thermoregulation model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Stress Index (HSI) — Belding & Hatch

The HSI quantifies the *physiological strain* of an environment.

### Step 1 — Define Maximum Evaporative Capacity

Given a saturated skin (w = 1), the maximum possible sweat evaporation rate is:

$$
E_{max} \;=\; h_{e}\,(P_{sk,sat}-P_{a}) \quad\;[\text{W/m}^{2}]
$$

### Step 2 — Define Required Evaporation

From Section 3.1, $E_{req}$ is the sweat rate needed to maintain thermal equilibrium.

### Step 3 — Form the Index

$$
\boxed{\;HSI \;=\; \frac{E_{req}}{E_{max}}\;\times\;100\;\%}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation Bands

| HSI Range | Condition |
|-----------|-----------|
| 0 | No heat stress (no sweat needed) |
| 0 – 10 | Mild, comfortable |
| 10 – 30 | Mild to moderate |
| 30 – 60 | Severe (industrial caution) |
| 60 – 100 | Very severe |
| > 100 | Heat-stroke threshold (impossible equilibrium) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comfort at a Glance

| Variable | Increases (↑) → Body feels | Decreases (↓) → Body feels | Control Lever (HVAC) |
|----------|----------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| DBT (Air Temp.) | Warmer | Cooler | Chiller / Heater coil |
| RH (Humidity) | Sticky / muggy | Dry, irritated | Cooling coil dehumidification |
| Air Velocity | Cooler (drafts) | Stagnant | Fan / duct sizing |
| MRT (Radiation) | Warmer (esp. hot wall/glass) | Cooler (cold glass) | Insulation, blinds |
| Clo (Clothing) | Warmer | Cooler | Occupant control |
| Met (Activity) | Warmer | Cooler | Occupant control |
| CO₂ / Dust | Drowsy, unhealthy | Fresh, alert | Filter + fresh-air damper |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Six-Pillar Comfort Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Human Body in CAC Space] --> B[Comfort Loop]
    B --> C1[Factor 1: DBT - 24 to 27 C]
    B --> C2[Factor 2: RH - 40 to 55 percent]
    B --> C3[Factor 3: Air Velocity 0.15 to 0.25 m per s]
    B --> C4[Factor 4: Air Purity CO2 less than 1000 ppm]
    B --> C5[Factor 5: Mean Radiant Temperature]
    B --> C6[Factor 6: Clothing - clo value]
    B --> C7[Factor 7: Metabolic Rate - met value]
    C1 --> D[Heat Balance M minus W equals C plus R plus Esk]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    C7 --> D
    D --> E{Comfort Achieved}
    E -->|Yes| F[State of Thermal Neutrality]
    E -->|No| G[Discomfort - Stuffy or Chilled]
    G --> H[AHU Recalibration]
    H --> B
    C4 --> I[Ventilation Loop]
    I --> J[Fresh Air Damper]
    I --> K[Filter Bank]
    J --> L[Indoor Air Quality OK]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Psychrometric Comfort Zone Overlay

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[ASHRAE Comfort Zone] --> B[Summer: 23 to 26 C DBT]
    A --> C[Winter: 20 to 23.5 C DBT]
    A --> D[Common RH band: 30 to 60 percent]
    B --> E[Optimum Point: 25 C and 50 percent RH]
    C --> F[Optimum Point: 22 C and 35 percent RH]
    G[Kerala Tropical CAC] --> H[26.7 C and 50 percent RH]
    H --> I[IS 1391 Indian Standard]
    A --> J[Saturated Line Phi equals 100]
    A --> K[Constant WBT Lines sloping upward right]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Heat Loss Pathways from Body

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Metabolic Heat M] --> W[External Work W]
    M --> Q[Net Heat to Lose M minus W]
    Q --> P1[Convective Loss C - to Air]
    Q --> P2[Radiative Loss R - to Surfaces]
    Q --> P3[Evaporative Loss Esk - Sweat]
    Q --> P4[Respiratory Loss Cres and Eres]
    P1 --> OUT[Comfort Reached]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P1 -.Increased by.-> V[Raise Air Velocity]
    P2 -.Increased by.-> MRT[Lower Mean Radiant Temp]
    P3 -.Increased by.-> RH[Lower Humidity]
    P4 -.Constant.-> RES[Function of Activity]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Role of the AHU in Comfort Delivery

```mermaid
graph LR
    RA[Return Air from Room] --> MIX[Mixing Box]
    OA[Outside Fresh Air] --> MIX
    MIX --> F[Filter Section - Purity]
    F --> CL[Cooling or Heating Coil - DBT]
    CL --> HMD[Humidifier or Dehumidifier - RH]
    HMD --> FN[Fan Section - Air Velocity]
    FN --> SA[Supply Air to Room]
    SA --> ROOM[Conditioned Space - Comfort Achieved]
    ROOM --> RA
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
> *List any six factors that affect human comfort in an air-conditioned space and indicate which are controllable by the air-conditioning system.*

**Model Answer:**

The six primary factors affecting comfort AC are:
1. **Dry-bulb temperature (DBT)** of air – *controllable* by cooling/heating coil.
2. **Relative Humidity (RH)** – *controllable* via dehumidification or humidification at the cooling coil.
3. **Air velocity** – *controllable* by fan speed and duct design.
4. **Air purity (CO₂, dust, microbes)** – *controllable* via filtration and ventilation.
5. **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT)** of surrounding surfaces – *partially controllable* by insulation and shading.
6. **Clothing insulation (clo) and Metabolic rate (met)** – *occupant-driven, not directly controllable*; the AC must compensate for them.

(Any six with correct classification = 3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
> *Define Effective Temperature (ET) and write its limitations.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Effective Temperature is an empirically derived single index that combines the effects of DBT, humidity, and air velocity into a temperature-equivalent value. It is defined as the temperature of a *standard* saturated environment (RH = 100 %, v ≃ 0.05 m/s) at which an occupant experiences the *same sensation of warmth* as in the actual environment.

**Limitations:**
1. Over-emphasises humidity at low temperatures and under-emphasises it at high temperatures.
2. Derived for sedentary, lightly-clothed subjects — not valid for industrial metabolic rates.
3. Considers only two-node physiology; does not account for MRT or radiant asymmetry.
4. Replaced in modern practice by **Standard Effective Temperature (SET)** and **PMV (Predicted Mean Vote)** models.

(Definition 1.5 marks + any 3 limitations 1.5 marks = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### 🔵 QUESTION A — 14 MARKS
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **psychrometric comfort zone** as defined by **ASHRAE Standard 55** for summer and winter conditions. Highlight the Indian Standard conditions for tropical comfort. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A small office in Kochi is maintained at DBT = **27 °C** and RH = **55 %**. The office has 12 occupants doing light office work (met = 1.2) wearing summer clothing (clo = 0.5). The MRT is approximately equal to the DBT. Estimate (i) the operative temperature, and (ii) comment qualitatively on whether the conditions are within the comfort band. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(a) — 7 Marks

The **ASHRAE Comfort Zone** is plotted on the psychrometric chart with DBT (°C) on the x-axis and RH (%) on the y-axis. Key features:

**Summer Zone:**
- DBT: **23 °C to 26 °C**
- Operative temperature: 23 – 26 °C
- RH band: **30 – 60 %**
- Air velocity ≤ 0.25 m/s

**Winter Zone:**
- DBT: **20 °C to 23.5 °C**
- Operative temperature: 20 – 23.5 °C
- RH band: 30 – 60 %

**Indian Standard (IS 1391 / KCPL):**
- Summer (tropical): **DBT = 26.7 °C, RH = 50 %, v = 0.15 m/s**
- Winter: DBT ≈ 22 °C, RH ≈ 35 %

**Schematic description (replaces sketch):**

```
   RH (%)
   100 |*
       |  *
       |    *  (Saturation line φ=1)
   60  |--------*---------
       |  SUMMER   WINTER   |
       |  ZONE      ZONE    |
   30  |--------*---------
       |  *      *
       |*     *
     0 +----------------------
       17   20  23  26  29  DBT (°C)
```

The comfort region lies well below saturation to allow sweat evaporation, and within the **30–60 % RH** envelope to prevent dryness or microbial growth.

(Marks: Summer zone 2, Winter zone 2, Indian standard 2, Sketch 1 = 7)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** DBT = 27 °C, RH = 55 %, MRT ≈ 27 °C, met = 1.2, clo = 0.5, v ≃ 0.15 m/s (assumed for low-velocity indoor office).

**Step 1 — Compute convective coefficient (Mitchell):**

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.15 \;+\; 10\,\sqrt{0.15}
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.15 \;+\; 10\,(0.3873)
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.15 \;+\; 3.873
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{c} \;\approx\; 14.17\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}}
$$

**Step 2 — Use radiative coefficient:** At indoor conditions, $h_r \approx 4.7$ W/m²K.

**Step 3 — Compute Operative Temperature:**

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{h_{c}\,T_{a} \;+\; h_{r}\,MRT}{h_{c}+h_{r}}
$$

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{(14.17)(27) \;+\; (4.7)(27)}{14.17+4.7}
$$

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{382.59 + 126.9}{18.87}
$$

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{509.49}{18.87}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{op} \;\approx\; 27.0\;^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ASHRAE Summer Comfort Band:**

| Parameter | Condition | ASHRAE Limit | Verdict |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|---------|
| Tₒₚ | 27.0 °C | 23 – 26 °C | **Slightly above** |
| RH | 55 % | 30 – 60 % | ✓ Within |
| Velocity | 0.15 m/s | ≤ 0.25 m/s | ✓ Within |

**Comment (qualitative):** The **operative temperature of 27 °C is marginally higher (by ~1 °C)** than the upper limit of the ASHRAE summer comfort zone. The occupants may experience a *slight sensation of warmth* but it remains within the Indian Standard tropical limit of 26.7 °C (within 0.3 °C of standard).

**Recommendation:** Lowering the DBT by **1 °C** to 26 °C, or raising the air velocity to 0.25 m/s, would push the operative temperature comfortably into the ASHRAE band. For tropical Kerala climate, the 27 °C is *acceptable*, though *not optimum* by international benchmark.

(Marks: Step 1: 2, Step 2: 1, Step 3: 2, Step 4: 1, Recommendation: 1 = 7)

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Derive the **steady-state human heat-balance equation** and explain each term. Show how the **operative temperature** is used to simplify the analysis. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A food-processing hall in Thrissur is to be maintained at DBT = **24 °C**, RH = **50 %**, and air velocity = **0.20 m/s**. Workers are performing light standing work (met = 1.5) wearing light overalls (clo = 0.7). Determine (i) the convective heat-transfer coefficient using the Mitchell equation, and (ii) the **Heat Stress Index (HSI)** using the Belding-Hatch method, assuming $P_{sk,sat}=5.628$ kPa, $P_a=1.475$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law to the human body (steady state):**

$$
M - W \;=\; (C + R + E_{sk}) + (C_{res} + E_{res})
$$

**Step 2 — Identify each term:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| M | Metabolic heat rate | 58.2 W/m² (1 met) |
| W | External mechanical work | 0 (sitting) |
| C | Convective loss | $h_c(T_{sk}-T_a)$ |
| R | Radiative loss | $h_r(T_{sk}-MRT)$ |
| Eₛₖ | Evaporative loss from skin | sweat-driven |
| Cᵣₑₛ + Eᵣₑₛ | Respiratory loss | ~10 % of M |

**Step 3 — Define operative temperature:**

$$
T_{op} \;=\; \frac{h_{c}\,T_{a} + h_{r}\,MRT}{h_{c}+h_{r}}
$$

This allows us to *lump* C and R together:

$$
C+R \;=\; (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(T_{sk}-T_{op})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified heat balance:**

$$
\boxed{M - W \;=\; (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(T_{sk}-T_{op}) + E_{sk} + (C_{res}+E_{res})}
$$

**Step 5 — For a seated person, $T_{sk}\approx 33\;^{\circ}$C** (skin regulatory set-point).

**Step 6 — Required evaporative loss for neutrality:**

$$
E_{req} \;=\; M - W - (h_{c}+h_{r})\,(33-T_{op}) - (C_{res}+E_{res})
$$

> This single equation encapsulates all six comfort factors and is the *operational* formula used in Fanger's PMV model.

(Marks: Eqn 2, Term identification 2, Tₒₚ definition 1, Final balance 2 = 7)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** v = 0.20 m/s, met = 1.5, clo = 0.7, Tₒₚ ≈ Tₐ = 24 °C, $P_{sk,sat}=5.628$ kPa, $P_a=1.475$ kPa.

**Part (i) — Mitchell Convective Coefficient:**

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.20 \;+\; 10\,\sqrt{0.20}
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.20} \;=\; 0.4472
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.20 \;+\; 10(0.4472)
$$

$$
h_{c} \;=\; 10.45 \;-\; 0.20 \;+\; 4.472
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{c} \;\approx\; 14.72\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}}
$$

**Part (ii) — HSI Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Metabolic rate:**
$$
M \;=\; 1.5 \times 58.2 \;=\; 87.3\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Required evaporative loss** (assuming W ≈ 0, $C_{res}+E_{res} \approx 0.1M$):

Using $T_{sk}=33\;^{\circ}$C, $T_{op}=24\;^{\circ}$C, and $h_r=4.7$:

$$
C+R \;=\; (14.72 + 4.7)\,(33 - 24) \;=\; 19.42 \times 9 \;=\; 174.78\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

Wait — that is **larger** than M. This means at these conditions the body would *gain* heat. Let's correct: we should use a more appropriate skin temperature for a working person, or note that the body invokes vasodilation to lower $T_{sk}$. Assume $T_{sk}=34\;^{\circ}$C (slightly warmer due to work):

$$
C+R \;=\; 19.42 \times 10 \;=\; 194.2\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

This is still problematic; it means the operative temperature 24 °C is **too cool** for the activity. We instead compute $E_{req}$ as the *excess metabolic* load:

$$
E_{req} \;=\; M - W - (h_{c}+h_{r})(T_{sk}-T_{op}) - 0.1M
$$

$$
E_{req} \;=\; 87.3 - 19.42(33-24) - 8.73
$$

$$
E_{req} \;=\; 87.3 - 174.78 - 8.73
$$

$$
E_{req} \;=\; -96.2\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Negative required evaporation** means the body is *losing* more heat than it produces → mild **cold stress**, not heat stress. Hence HSI is effectively **0** (no sweating required).

**Step 3 — Compute maximum evaporative capacity (for completeness):**

$$
h_{e} \;=\; 16.5 \times h_{c} \;=\; 16.5 \times 14.72 \;=\; 242.9\;\text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}
$$

$$
E_{max} \;=\; h_{e}\,(P_{sk,sat}-P_{a}) \;=\; 242.9 \times (5.628 - 1.475)
$$

$$
E_{max} \;=\; 242.9 \times 4.153 \;\approx\; 1008.7\;\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat Stress Index:**

Since $E_{req}<0$ (no sweat needed):
$$
\boxed{HSI \;\approx\; 0\;\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** The conditions are thermally *neutral to slightly cool*; HSI of 0 % indicates **no heat stress**. For a food-processing hall this is ideal for product stability, though workers may want to add one light garment layer.

(Marks: Mitchell calc 2, M 1, $E_{req}$ 2, $E_{max}$ 1, HSI 1 = 7)

> [!WARNING]
> **🚩 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.1·M respiratory loss term** in the heat balance. This costs ~1 mark.
> 2. **Conflating air temperature with operative temperature.** When MRT ≈ Tₐ, Tₒₚ ≈ Tₐ — but students often write Tₒₚ = Tₐ without justification.
> 3. **Incorrect unit of $h_e$:** It is **W/m²·kPa**, not W/m²·K. The Lewis relation introduces kPa; many students drop the unit and lose a mark.
> 4. **Sign of $E_{req}$:** A *negative* $E_{req}$ is physically meaningful — it means the body needs *no* evaporation. Students often write a fake positive value.
> 5. **Mitchell equation validity range** (0 ≤ v ≤ 2.5 m/s). If a problem states v = 5 m/s, you must use a different correlation or note the limitation.
> 6. **Indian Standard values:** Forgetting to convert the IS 1391 conditions (26.7 °C, 50 %, 0.15 m/s) into SI units with proper units shown loses a mark.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Six + one comfort factors**: DBT, RH, air velocity, air purity, MRT, clothing (clo), metabolic rate (met).
- [x] **First three (DBT, RH, velocity) are HVAC-controllable**; the rest are occupant/architecture-driven.
- [x] **Comfort heat-balance equation**: $M - W = C + R + E_{sk} + C_{res} + E_{res}$.
- [x] **Mitchell equation** for $h_c$: $h_c = 10.45 - v + 10\sqrt{v}$ — *memorise it*.
- [x] **Operative temperature** $T_{op} = (h_c T_a + h_r MRT)/(h_c + h_r)$.
- [x] **ASHRAE 55 summer zone**: 23 – 26 °C, RH 30 – 60 %, v ≤ 0.25 m/s.
- [x] **Indian Standard (IS 1391, tropical)**: 26.7 °C DBT, 50 % RH, 0.15 m/s.
- [x] **1 met = 58.2 W/m²**; **1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W**.
- [x] **Effective Temperature (ET)** ≈ WBT at 50 % RH (approximation).
- [x] **HSI = (E_req / E_max) × 100 %** — Belding-Hatch method.
- [x] **Ventilation (ASHRAE 62.1)**: ~8.5 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s per m².
- [x] **Kerala context**: Hot-humid → prefer slightly higher DBT (27 °C) with stronger dehumidification and ≥ 0.2 m/s air movement.
- [x] **Food-industry twist (OEFTT612)**: HEPA filtration, 15–20 % fresh air, strict control of microbial-load humidity (RH ≤ 55 %).
- [x] **Negative $E_{req}$** ⇒ cold stress, not heat stress. Always comment on the *sign*.
- [x] **Always** state units of $h_c, h_r, h_e$ explicitly — examiners reward this.
- [x] **Always** mention the **standard reference atmosphere** (1 atm = 101.325 kPa) when working with vapour pressures.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Six Factors:** **"CARE – MVP"**
> **C**lothing (clo) • **A**ir purity • **R**adiant heat (MRT) • **E**vaporative (humidity) • **M**etabolic (met) • **V**elocity • **P**ressure (atmospheric, for high-altitude corrections)
> *(Reorder to DBT, RH, V, Purity, MRT, Clo, Met as per the standard presentation.)*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Air-Conditioning: Winter/Summer/Year-Round Air-Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Industrial Air-Conditioning: Winter/Summer/Year-Round Air-Conditioning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Industrial Air-Conditioning — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Air-Conditioning (IAC)** is the systematic control of **temperature, humidity, air purity (dust/odor), and air motion (circulation)** within an industrial or food-processing enclosure to maintain prescribed environmental conditions that ensure product quality, worker comfort, and process stability, regardless of the external ambient climatic conditions. In food industry applications, IAC is mission-critical because most biochemical, microbiological, and enzymatic reactions in food products are highly sensitive to even small deviations in temperature and humidity. --- ## 1.2 Sub-Classification of Industrial Air-Conditioning Industrial air-conditioning is broadly classified into three operational categories, distinguished by the climatic season they are designed to combat: ### (a) Winter Air-Conditioning (WAC) > [!NOTE] > **Winter Air-Conditioning (WAC)** is the conditioning of air during the **cold and dry** outdoor season. The system must **heat** the air and **add moisture (humidify)** to maintain the required indoor comfort/process conditions. ### (b) Summer Air-Conditioning (SAC) > [!NOTE] > **Summer Air-Conditioning (SAC)** is the conditioning of air during the **hot and humid** outdoor season. The system must **cool** the air and **remove moisture (dehumidify)** to maintain the required indoor conditions. ### (c) Year-Round Air-Conditioning (YAC) > [!NOTE] > **Year-Round Air-Conditioning (YAC)** is a unified, automated HVAC system capable of providing either heating + humidification **OR** cooling + dehumidification (or any combination) **throughout the entire calendar year**, switching automatically based on outdoor weather and indoor load demands. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Cabin" Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of your Car's Climate Control System on a cold winter morning in Kerala versus a hot April afternoon in Palakkad.** > - On a **winter morning** (15 °C, foggy): the AC *heats* the cabin air and the defogger *adds moisture* to the windshield — this is **Winter AC**. > - On a **summer afternoon** (38 °C, 90 % RH): the AC *cools* the cabin and *dries* the air (water drips from the condenser drain) — this is **Summer AC**. > - A **modern climate-controlled car** automatically switches between heating, cooling, humidifying, and dehumidifying depending on what is needed — this is **Year-Round AC**. > > Now simply **scale this from a 4-seater car to a 100-tonne cold storage or a banana-ripening chamber** — and you have Industrial Air-Conditioning! --- ## 1.4 Standard Reference Design Conditions (KTU Convention) For calculation consistency, the KTU/ISHRAE-recommended **indoor design conditions** for food industry applications are: | Parameter | Standard Indoor Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Dry Bulb Temperature ($DBT_i$) | **25 °C ± 1 °C** | °C | | Relative Humidity ($RH_i$) | **50 % ± 5 %** | % | | Air Velocity | < **0.25** | m/s | | Air Cleanliness | < **10** μg/m³ dust | μg/m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > These indoor values are the **target state** — the supply air must be *processed* from any outdoor state (OAC) to reach these indoor values (IAC) via the psychrometric processes of heating/cooling/humidification/dehumidification. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Winter Air-Conditioning (WAC) — Detailed Working ### 2.1.1 Operating Conditions - Outdoor: Cold + Dry → $DBT_o \approx 5–15 °C$, $RH_o \approx 30–60\%$ - Indoor: Warm + Moderately Humid → $DBT_i \approx 22–25 °C$, $RH_i \approx 40–50\%$ ### 2.1.2 Required Air-Processing Operations The air entering from outdoors must undergo **two sequential processes**: 1. **Sensible Heating**: Raise the temperature of the air without changing its moisture content (the DBT rises at constant specific humidity $W$). 2. **Humidification**: Add water vapor to the air (the $W$ rises, and DB temperature falls slightly due to adiabatic evaporation, approaching the wet-bulb line). ### 2.1.3 Psychrometric Path — Winter AC The classical two-process winter cycle on the psychrometric chart is: $$O \xrightarrow{\text{Heating Coil}} H \xrightarrow{\text{Spray/Air-Washer}} R$$ Where: - **O** = Outdoor Air Condition - **H** = Heated Air Condition (after the heating coil) - **R** = Room Condition (the desired indoor state) ### 2.1.4 Components of a Winter AC Plant | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Air Filter** | Removes dust/particulates from intake air | | **Pre-Heater** | Initial heating to prevent freezing in humidifier | | **Heating Coil (Finned)** | Sensible heating using hot water/steam/electricity | | **Spray-Type Humidifier / Air Washer** | Adiabatic humidification using water sprays | | **Re-Heater (Optional)** | Final temperature trim to match desired supply temperature | | **Blower / Fan** | Circulates air through ducts | | **Automatic Controls** | Modulate heating/humidification based on sensor feedback | ### 2.1.5 Engineering Use Case in Food Industry - Bakery proofing rooms (dough fermentation at ~30 °C, 75 % RH) - Cheese ripening chambers (12–15 °C, 85–90 % RH) - Confectionery enrobing halls - Citrus storage --- ## 2.2 Summer Air-Conditioning (SAC) — Detailed Working ### 2.2.1 Operating Conditions - Outdoor: Hot + Humid → $DBT_o \approx 35–45 °C$, $RH_o \approx 60–90\%$ (e.g., Kerala coast) - Indoor: Cool + Moderately Dry → $DBT_i \approx 22–25 °C$, $RH_i \approx 50–55\%$ ### 2.2.2 Required Air-Processing Operations The air entering from outdoors must undergo **two sequential processes**: 1. **Cooling & Dehumidification**: Pass the air over a chilled cooling coil — the air is cooled below its dew point, causing condensation. Both DBT and $W$ fall. The exit condition lies on a line connecting the **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** to the entering air. 2. **Reheating (often required)**: Because overcooling occurs to dehumidify effectively, the air may need to be reheated to the desired supply temperature without adding moisture (sensible heating). ### 2.2.3 Psychrometric Path — Summer AC $$O \xrightarrow{\text{Cooling Coil}} C \xrightarrow{\text{Reheater}} S \rightarrow R$$ Where: - **O** = Outdoor Air Condition - **C** = Condition after cooling/dehumidification coil - **S** = Supply Air Condition (after reheat) - **R** = Room Condition ### 2.2.4 Components of a Summer AC Plant | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Air Filter** | Removes particulates | | **Cooling Coil (Chilled Water/DX)** | Cools and dehumidifies the air via refrigeration | | **Condensate Drain Pan** | Removes condensed moisture | | **Re-Heater (optional)** | Sensible heating after overcooling | | **Blower / Fan** | Air circulation | | **Compressor-Condenser-Expansion Valve Train** | The vapor-compression refrigeration cycle supplying chilled fluid | | **Controls** | Thermostat, humidistat | ### 2.2.5 Engineering Use Case in Food Industry - Meat/fish processing halls - Dairy chilling rooms - Beverage carbonation/bottling rooms - Pharmaceutical-cum-food ingredient storage --- ## 2.3 Year-Round Air-Conditioning (YAC) — Detailed Working ### 2.3.1 Operating Philosophy YAC is a **dual-mode** system capable of switching between WAC and SAC modes automatically. The mode selection is determined by a year-round **enthalpy-based outdoor air sensor** or a temperature/humidity comparator. ### 2.3.2 Modes of Operation in a YAC Plant | Mode | Outdoor Condition | Required Action | |---|---|---| | **Winter Mode** | Cold + Dry | Heating + Humidification | | **Spring/Autumn Mode** | Mild | Possibly just sensible heating/cooling | | **Summer Mode** | Hot + Humid | Cooling + Dehumidification | | **Monsoon Mode** | Warm + Very Humid | Heavy Dehumidification + Sensible Cooling | | **Dry-Summer Mode (e.g., Delhi May)** | Hot + Dry | Evaporative Cooling + Humidification | ### 2.3.3 Components of a Year-Round AC Plant The YAC system integrates **all** components of WAC and SAC into a unified air-handling unit (AHU): 1. **Pre-filter + Secondary filter** 2. **Pre-heater** (steam/hot-water coil) 3. **Cooling coil** (chilled water from chiller or direct expansion) 4. **Re-heater** (steam/hot-water coil, downstream of cooling coil) 5. **Spray-type humidifier / Air washer** 6. **Face & bypass dampers** around the cooling coil (for modulation) 7. **Mixed-air dampers** (return air + fresh air) 8. **Supply + Return fan with VFD** 9. **Microprocessor-based controller** managing all dampers, valves, humidifier ### 2.3.4 The "Bypass Factor" Modulation Strategy > [!IMPORTANT] > A YAC system often uses **face-and-bypass dampers** around the cooling coil. A fraction of warm return air **bypasses** the coil and mixes with the cooled air, achieving precise temperature control without continuous compressor cycling. The fraction that passes through the coil undergoes full cooling, while the bypassed fraction remains at the pre-coil state. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All quantities below are per unit mass of dry air (basis: $m_a$ in kg of dry air/s). | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sensible Heat (Heating or Cooling) | $Q_s = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·°C | | 2 | Latent Heat (Humidification/Dehumid.) | $Q_L = m_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | kW | $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg | | 3 | Total Heat | $Q_t = Q_s + Q_L = m_a \cdot \Delta h$ | kW | $\Delta h$ = enthalpy change | | 4 | Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) | $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}$ | — | Dimensionless; lies on chart | | 5 | Bypass Factor (BPF) of Coil | $BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ | — | $T_1$ = entering, $T_2$ = leaving, $T_{ADP}$ = coil surface temp | | 6 | Contact Factor (CF) of Spray | $CF = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{WB1}}$ | — | For adiabatic saturator | | 7 | Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) | $T_{ADP} \approx T_{WB}$ of entering air | °C | For dehumidifying coil | | 8 | Humidification Effectiveness | $\eta_{hum} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_s - W_1}$ | — | $W_s$ = saturation at $T_1$ | | 9 | By-Passed Air Fraction (modulating damper) | $x_{bp} = \dfrac{T_{supply} - T_{cooled}}{T_{precoil} - T_{cooled}}$ | — | Determines damper position | | 10 | Mixed Air Temperature | $T_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{OA} T_{OA} + \dot{m}_{RA} T_{RA}}{\dot{m}_{OA} + \dot{m}_{RA}}$ | °C | Mass-weighted mixing | | 11 | Mass of dry air from RSHF | $m_a = \dfrac{RSH}{1.005(T_i - T_s)}$ | kg/s | RSH = Room Sensible Heat | | 12 | Apparatus Dew Point for SHF | $ADP = T_i - \dfrac{RSH}{m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (1-BPF)}$ | °C | Used in coil selection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tip for KTU Exams:** Always carry out the **psychrometric analysis on the chart FIRST** to extract $\Delta T$, $\Delta W$, $\Delta h$, and SHF, and *then* plug into the formula. Examiners award **2 marks** for an accurate chart sketch. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Industry | Food Industry Application | Required $DBT_i$ | Required $RH_i$ | AC Type | |---|---|---|---| | Banana Ripening | 15–20 °C | 90–95 % | WAC / YAC (winter heavy humid) | | Apple Cold Storage | 0–4 °C | 90–95 % | YAC (winter mode) | | Bakery Proofing | 30–35 °C | 75–80 % | WAC | | Ice Cream Hardening | -25 to -40 °C | — | SAC (extreme) | | Mushroom Growing | 12–16 °C | 80–90 % | YAC (winter) | | Meat Processing | 7–10 °C | 80–85 % | YAC | | Chocolate Tempering | 18–22 °C | 50–55 % | YAC (strict, narrow band) | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Bypass Factor of a Cooling/Heating Coil ### Physical Setup Consider air entering a finned cooling coil at temperature $T_1$ (with humidity ratio $W_1$). The coil surface is maintained at $T_{ADP}$. A fraction of the air contacts the cold surface fully, while the rest bypasses it. ### Step 1 — Define the Bypass Factor By definition: $$BPF = \frac{\text{Amount bypassed}}{\text{Total amount}} = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$ where: - $T_1$ = dry-bulb temperature entering the coil (°C) - $T_2$ = dry-bulb temperature leaving the coil (°C) - $T_{ADP}$ = apparatus dew point (°C) — the surface temperature of the coil ### Step 2 — Verify Limiting Cases - **Case A — Perfect Coil ($BPF = 0$)**: Air leaves at coil surface temperature → $T_2 = T_{ADP}$. The numerator $T_2 - T_{ADP} = 0$, so $BPF = 0$. ✓ - **Case B — No Coil Effect ($BPF = 1$)**: Air passes through without contacting surface → $T_2 = T_1$. Numerator equals denominator → $BPF = 1$. ✓ ### Step 3 — Derive from Energy Balance Assume adiabatic coil. The mixed leaving air is a mass-weighted average: $$\dot{m}_{total} T_2 = \dot{m}_{bypass} T_1 + \dot{m}_{contact} T_{ADP}$$ Dividing by $\dot{m}_{total}$ and letting $x = \dot{m}_{bypass}/\dot{m}_{total}$: $$T_2 = x T_1 + (1-x) T_{ADP}$$ Rearrange to isolate $x$: $$T_2 - T_{ADP} = x(T_1 - T_{ADP}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$ Since by definition $x = BPF$, the derivation is complete. ∎ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A larger $BPF$ means a less effective coil (smaller contact area, lower surface temperature drop, higher air velocity). For typical industrial chilled water coils, $BPF$ ranges from **0.05 to 0.30**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Apparatus Dew Point from Room Sensible Heat (RSH) and Bypass Factor ### Given Data - Room Sensible Heat: $RSH$ (kW) - Room temperature: $T_i$ (°C) - Mass flow rate of dry air: $m_a$ (kg/s) - Coil Bypass Factor: $BPF$ (known from manufacturer) ### Step 1 — Express the Leaving Coil Temperature The temperature of air leaving the coil in terms of ADP: $$T_2 = T_{ADP} + BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})$$ ### Step 2 — Relate RSH to Air Temperature Drop Assuming the room sensible heat is absorbed by the supply air (entering at $T_s$, leaving at $T_i$): $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_s)$$ For the room, the supply air enters at $T_2$ (after coil), so $T_s = T_2$: $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_2)$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute $T_2$ $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot \big[T_i - \big\{T_{ADP} + BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})\big\}\big]$$ $$RSH = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot \big[(T_i - T_{ADP}) - BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})\big]$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for ADP $$\boxed{T_{ADP} = T_i - \frac{RSH}{m_a \cdot c_p} - BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP} - ??? )}$$ Re-derive cleanly by isolating $T_{ADP}$ from: $$T_2 = T_{ADP}(1 - BPF) + BPF \cdot T_1$$ $$T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1 = T_{ADP}(1 - BPF)$$ $$T_{ADP} = \frac{T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1}{1 - BPF}$$ But $T_2 = T_i - \dfrac{RSH}{m_a c_p}$, therefore: $$\boxed{T_{ADP} = \frac{\left(T_i - \dfrac{RSH}{m_a c_p}\right) - BPF \cdot T_1}{1 - BPF}}$$ This is the standard coil-selection equation used by KTU exam problems. ∎ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Summer AC (KTU Style, 14-Mark Format) ### Problem Statement A food processing hall has the following data: - Outdoor air: $DBT_o = 40 °C$, $W_o = 0.024$ kg/kg d.a. (hot & humid Kerala coast) - Desired indoor: $DBT_i = 25 °C$, $RH_i = 50\%$ - Room sensible heat: $RSH = 12$ kW - Room latent heat: $RLH = 4$ kW - Coil bypass factor: $BPF = 0.20$ - Air supplied at room temp ($T_s = T_i$) **Required:** (a) Apparatus Dew Point and supply mass flow rate. (b) Total refrigeration load in TR. ### Step 1 — Total Heat from RSH and RLH $$Q_t = RSH + RLH = 12 + 4 = 16 \text{ kW}$$ ### Step 2 — Sensible Heat Factor $$SHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75$$ ### Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air Using the sensible heat equation (air supplied at room temp, so $\Delta T$ between supply and room is zero in this scenario — we instead use the total enthalpy difference): The outside air enthalpy at $40 °C$, $W = 0.024$: $$h_o = 1.005 \cdot 40 + 0.024 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 40)$$ $$h_o = 40.20 + 0.024 \cdot (2501 + 75.2) = 40.20 + 0.024 \cdot 2576.2$$ $$h_o = 40.20 + 61.83 = 102.03 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$ The desired indoor air enthalpy at $25 °C$, $50\% RH$: $$W_i = 0.0098 \text{ kg/kg d.a. (from psychrometric tables)}$$ $$h_i = 1.005 \cdot 25 + 0.0098 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 25) = 25.125 + 0.0098 \cdot 2548 = 25.125 + 24.97$$ $$h_i = 50.10 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$ ### Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air $$m_a = \frac{Q_t}{h_o - h_i} = \frac{16}{102.03 - 50.10} = \frac{16}{51.93}$$ $$\boxed{m_a = 0.308 \text{ kg/s of dry air}}$$ ### Step 5 — Apparatus Dew Point For the leaving coil state at $T_2$, the room-load method gives: $$T_2 = T_i - \frac{RSH}{m_a c_p} = 25 - \frac{12}{0.308 \times 1.005} = 25 - \frac{12}{0.3095} = 25 - 38.77$$ $$T_2 = -13.77 °C$$ This is below the coil ADP — therefore the coil must operate **below** this, requiring sub-zero chilled water. Recalculating with proper assumption (room supplies air at $T_s = 14 °C$, the cooling coil provides 11 °C of temperature drop): $$T_2 = 14 °C, \quad T_1 = 40 °C$$ $$T_{ADP} = \frac{T_2 - BPF \cdot T_1}{1 - BPF} = \frac{14 - 0.20 \cdot 40}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{14 - 8}{0.80} = \frac{6}{0.80}$$ $$\boxed{T_{ADP} = 7.5 °C}$$ ### Step 6 — Total Refrigeration Load in TR $$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW (heat extraction rate)}$$ $$\text{Load in TR} = \frac{Q_t}{3.517} = \frac{16}{3.517}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Load} = 4.55 \text{ TR}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry 4 significant figures in intermediate steps and round only at the final answer. Marks are awarded for **method clarity** — write every equation you use. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — WAC vs SAC vs YAC | Parameter | Winter AC (WAC) | Summer AC (SAC) | Year-Round AC (YAC) | |---|---|---|---| | Outdoor state | Cold, Dry | Hot, Humid | Variable | | Primary process | Heating | Cooling | Both, as needed | | Secondary process | Humidification | Dehumidification | Both, as needed | | Main equipment | Heating coil | Cooling coil | Heating + Cooling coils | | Humidity device | Air washer / Steam injector | Cooling coil (condensation) | Both | | Re-heater needed? | Usually NO (rare) | **YES** (commonly) | **YES** (in summer mode) | | Energy source | Boiler / Heat pump / Electric heater | Vapor compression refrigeration | Both | | COP concern | High COP (heat pumps > 4) | COP ~ 2.5–3.5 | Mode-dependent | | Typical capacity | Heating coil: 30–100 kW | Cooling coil: 50–500 kW | Combined | | Common applications | Proofing, ripening | Meat/dairy halls | Cold storage, ripening, processing | | Seasonal usage | 3–4 months/year | 4–5 months/year | 12 months/year | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Summer Air-Conditioning Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeOA[OUTDOOR AIR
Hot Humid 40C 90%RH] --> nodeF1[FILTER
Dust Removal] nodeF1 --> nodeCC[COOLING COIL
Chilled Water DX] nodeCC --> nodeCD[CONDENSATE
Drain Pan] nodeCC --> nodeRH[REHEATER
Trim Temperature] nodeRH --> nodeBF[BLOWER
Supply Fan] nodeBF --> nodeDUCT[DUCTS] nodeDUCT --> nodeROOM[FOOD PROCESSING
HALL 25C 50%RH] nodeROOM --> nodeRA[RETURN AIR
Plenum] nodeRA --> nodeMIX[MIXED AIR
DAMPER] nodeMIX --> nodeF1 nodeCC -.Chilled Water.-> nodeCHILL[CHILLER UNIT
Vapor Compression] nodeCHILL -.Hot Gas Reject.-> nodeCOND[CONDENSER] nodeCTRL[CONTROLLER
T sensor and W sensor] -.Modulate.-> nodeCC nodeCTRL -.Modulate.-> nodeRH nodeCTRL -.Modulate.-> nodeMIX ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Outdoor air is filtered → cooled/dehumidified → reheated for temperature precision → blown to the room. Return air mixes with fresh air at the mixing damper. The controller continuously modulates the cooling coil valve and reheater to maintain set-point. --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Winter Air-Conditioning Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeOAC[OUTDOOR AIR
Cold Dry 10C 40%RH] --> nodeF2[FILTER] nodeF2 --> nodePH[PRE HEATER
Anti Freeze] nodePH --> nodeHC[HEATING COIL
Steam or Hot Water] nodeHC --> nodeSP[SPRAY HUMIDIFIER
Adiabatic Water Spray] nodeSP --> nodeBL[BLOWER] nodeBL --> nodeDK[DUCTS] nodeDK --> nodeRM[INDOOR ROOM
22C 45%RH Bakery Proofing] nodeRM --> nodeRET[RETURN AIR] nodeRET --> nodeMD[MIXING DAMPER] nodeMD --> nodeF2 nodeBOIL[BOILER] -.Hot Water Steam.-> nodeHC nodeBOIL -.Hot Water.-> nodePH nodeCT2[CONTROLLER
Thermostat and Humidistat] -.Modulate.-> nodeHC nodeCT2 -.Modulate.-> nodeSP nodeCT2 -.Modulate.-> nodeMD ``` --- ## 4.3 Year-Round AC — Modular Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INTAKE["INTAKE SECTION"] nodeOA2[OUTDOOR AIR] nodeMD2[RETURN AIR] nodeMIX2[MIXING DAMPER] end subgraph FILTER_BANK["FILTER SECTION"] nodePF[PRE FILTER] nodeSF[SECONDARY FILTER] end subgraph PROCESSING["AIR PROCESSING SECTION"] nodePH2[PRE HEATER] nodeCC2[COOLING COIL] nodeRH2[REHEATER] nodeFBP[FACE BYPASS DAMPERS] nodeHUM2[SPRAY HUMIDIFIER] end subgraph SUPPLY["SUPPLY SECTION"] nodeBL2[SUPPLY FAN] nodeDK2[SUPPLY DUCT] nodeRM2[CONDITIONED ROOM] end nodeOA2 --> nodeMIX2 nodeMD2 --> nodeMIX2 nodeMIX2 --> nodePF --> nodeSF nodeSF --> nodeFBP nodeFBP --> nodeCC2 nodeCC2 --> nodeRH2 nodeRH2 --> nodeHUM2 nodeHUM2 --> nodeBL2 nodeBL2 --> nodeDK2 nodeDK2 --> nodeRM2 nodeRM2 --> nodeMD2 nodePH2 -.Aux.-> nodeFBP nodeCTRL2[CENTRAL CONTROLLER
Enthalpy Based] -.Modulates.-> nodeMIX2 nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeFBP nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeCC2 nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeRH2 nodeCTRL2 -.Modulates.-> nodeHUM2 ``` --- ## 4.4 Psychrometric Process Flow — Winter & Summer Cycles Combined ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph WINTER["WINTER AC PATH"] O1[O Outdoor Cold Dry] -->|Sensible Heating| H1[H Heated] H1 -->|Adiabatic Humidification| R1[R Room 22C 45%RH] end subgraph SUMMER["SUMMER AC PATH"] O2[O Outdoor Hot Humid] -->|Cooling Dehumid| C2[C Cooled Coil] C2 -->|Sensible Reheat| S2[S Supply 14C] S2 -->|Room Load Pickup| R2[R Room 25C 50%RH] end subgraph YEAR["YEAR ROUND AC"] WINTER SUMMER end ``` --- ## 4.5 Schematic — Face-and-Bypass Damper Assembly (Critical for YAC) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIN[RETURN AIR
25C] --> nodeFBD[FACE AND BYPASS DAMPER ASSEMBLY] nodeFBD --> nodeCOIL[COOLING COIL
Chilled Water 7C] nodeCOIL --> nodeOUTMIX[MIXING CHAMBER
Post Coil] nodeFBD -.Bypass Path.-> nodeOUTMIX nodeOUTMIX --> nodeSPLY[SUPPLY FAN] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **How it works:** When the cooling demand is *high*, the face damper opens (forcing air through the coil) and bypass closes. When demand is *low*, the face damper closes and bypass opens (mixing cooled and warm air for precise modulation). This eliminates compressor short-cycling and is the heart of YAC energy efficiency. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Winter Air Conditioning. List any four essential components of a winter air-conditioning system with their functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Winter Air Conditioning (WAC) is the process of conditioning outdoor air during the cold and dry season to maintain prescribed indoor conditions of temperature and relative humidity by **heating and humidification** of the supply air. **Components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Filter** — Removes dust and contaminants from intake air. 2. **Pre-Heater** — Initial heating to prevent freezing in downstream humidifier. 3. **Heating Coil** — Sensible heating of air to raise DBT. 4. **Spray Humidifier / Air Washer** — Adiabatic humidification to raise moisture content. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is meant by Year-Round Air Conditioning? Why is it preferred over separate WAC and SAC plants?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Year-Round Air Conditioning (YAC) is a single integrated HVAC plant capable of automatically switching between heating + humidification (winter mode) and cooling + dehumidification (summer mode) depending on the outdoor ambient conditions, throughout all 12 months of the year. **Why Preferred (1.5 marks):** 1. **Space saving** — single AHU replaces two plants. 2. **Lower capital cost** than installing two separate systems. 3. **Automated control** — eliminates manual seasonal changeover. 4. **Energy-efficient** via face-bypass dampers and enthalpy-based modulation. 5. **Consistent indoor conditions** — no transitional season disruption. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **a) Explain with the help of a psychrometric chart the various air-conditioning processes involved in a Winter Air-Conditioning system. (7 marks)** **b) A bakery proofing room requires 1500 m³/h of conditioned air at 30 °C DBT and 75 % RH. Outdoor air is at 15 °C DBT and 40 % RH. Assuming adiabatic humidification, determine: (i) the heat required in the heating coil, (ii) the mass of water added in the humidifier, and (iii) the sensible heat supplied by the re-heater (if any).
