# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
> *"List any three chilling-sensitive vegetables and their safe minimum storage temperatures."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Tomato (ripe)** — Safe minimum storage temperature: **7 °C** (below this, chilling injury occurs).
> 2. **Cucumber** — Safe minimum storage temperature: **10 °C** (below this, surface pitting and water-soaked lesions develop).
> 3. **Brinjal (Eggplant)** — Safe minimum storage temperature: **10 °C** (below this, internal browning occurs).
>
> All three are tropical vegetables susceptible to **chilling injury** when stored at temperatures above their freezing point but below their critical threshold (typically 7–13 °C).

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> *"What is the Q₁₀ coefficient in vegetable respiration? State its typical range."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Q₁₀ coefficient** quantifies the **temperature sensitivity of respiration rate** in vegetables. It is defined as the **factor by which the respiration rate increases for every 10 °C rise** in temperature.
>
> **Mathematical form:** $R_2 = R_1 \cdot (Q_{10})^{\Delta T / 10}$
>
> **Typical range for vegetables:** $Q_{10} = 2.0$ to $3.0$.
>
> **Engineering significance:** A higher Q₁₀ means the vegetable is more sensitive to temperature abuse — emphasizing the importance of **rapid pre-cooling** and **strict cold-chain maintenance**.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024, Module 4]**
> *(a)* Discuss the **post-harvest physiology of vegetables** with reference to **respiration, transpiration, and ethylene effects**. Explain how each factor influences refrigeration design. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A cold room stores **10,000 kg of cabbage** at **0 °C**. The cabbage enters at **22 °C** and must be cooled in **6 hours**.
> Given:
> - Specific heat $C_p = 3.90\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Respiration rate at 0 °C = $5\,\text{mL CO}_2/(\text{kg·h)}$
> - $Q_{10} = 2.5$
>
> Calculate:
> - (i) Sensible heat load
> - (ii) Total respiration heat over 6 hours
> - (iii) Total refrigeration load in **kW** and **TR** **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Post-Harvest Physiology (7 marks)

**1. Respiration (2 marks)**

Vegetables are **living tissues** that continue aerobic metabolism post-harvest:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \to 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Heat}
$$

- Generates **metabolic heat** that adds to refrigeration load.
- Respiration depletes sugars/starches → loss of nutritional quality.
- Higher temperature → higher respiration → shorter shelf life.
- **Refrigeration design implication:** Must account for **respiratory heat ($Q_r$)** in total load calculation. Pre-cooling should be **fast** to drop $R$ to minimum.

**2. Transpiration (2 marks)**

- Vegetables lose water through surface evaporation.
- Even **5 %** weight loss makes them **unmarketable** (wilting, shrivelling).
- Depends on **vapour pressure deficit (VPD)** = $(P_{sat,\,prod} - P_{air})$.
- **Refrigeration design implication:** Maintain **high RH (95–98 %)** using humidifiers or by **minimizing air velocity** over produce.

**3. Ethylene Effects (2 marks)**

- Many vegetables (tomato, capsicum) produce ethylene; others (cucumber, brinjal) are **ethylene-sensitive**.
- Trace ethylene (≥ 0.1 ppm) triggers **senescence and yellowing** in leafy vegetables.
- **Refrigeration design implication:** Include **ethylene scrubbers** (KMnO₄ filters) and **separate storage rooms** for ethylene-producing and ethylene-sensitive produce.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** An integrated refrigeration design must simultaneously control **T, RH, ethylene, and atmosphere** to maximize vegetable shelf life.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $m = 10{,}000\,\text{kg}$
- $T_i = 22\,°C$
- $T_f = 0\,°C$
- $C_p = 3.90\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $R = 5\,\text{mL CO}_2/(\text{kg·h)}$
- Cooling time = 6 h

**(i) Sensible Heat Load $Q_s$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \\[6pt]
&= 10{,}000 \times 3.90 \times (22 - 0) \\[6pt]
&= 10{,}000 \times 3.90 \times 22 \\[6pt]
&= 858{,}000\,\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Respiration Heat over 6 hours (2 marks):**

Total CO₂ produced:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CO_2} &= m \cdot R \cdot t \\[6pt]
&= 10{,}000 \times 5 \times 6 \\[6pt]
&= 300{,}000\,\text{mL} = 300\,\text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming RQ = 1.0, equivalent O₂ consumed = 300 L:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_r &= 10.5 \times 300 \\[6pt]
&= 3{,}150\,\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Load in kW and TR (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_s + Q_r = 858{,}000 + 3{,}150 = 861{,}150\,\text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time = 6 h = 21,600 s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{861{,}150}{21{,}600} \approx 39.87\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

In tonnes of refrigeration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{TR} &= \dfrac{39.87}{3.517} \approx 11.34\,\text{TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Total refrigeration load $\approx$ **39.87 kW** or **11.34 TR**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Dec 2024, Module 4]**
> *(a)* Explain the concept of **Controlled Atmosphere (CA) and Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** in vegetable storage. Discuss their **principle, gas composition, and benefits** with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Compare the **storage requirements of leafy vegetables, root vegetables, and tropical fruit vegetables (solanaceous and cucurbits)** in tabular form. Highlight the **chilling injury risk** for each category. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): CA and MAP in Vegetable Storage (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**

Both CA and MAP work by **altering the storage atmosphere** to suppress respiration and senescence:

- **Reduce O₂** → slows aerobic respiration and ethylene action.
- **Elevate CO₂** → competitive inhibition of ethylene receptors; suppresses microbial growth.
- **Balance N₂** → inert filler gas.

**2. Difference between CA and MAP (2 marks):**

| Feature | CA Storage | MAP |
|---------|------------|-----|
| Scale | Large cold rooms | Individual retail packs |
| Gas control | Continuous dynamic regulation | Initial gas flush, passive modification |
| Equipment | N₂ generators, CO₂ scrubbers, O₂ analyzers | Sealed polymer films |
| Application | Bulk storage of apples, cabbage, carrots | Retail tomatoes, lettuce, broccoli |

**3. Typical Gas Composition (2 marks):**

| Gas | Normal Air | CA (Cabbage) | CA (Tomato) | MAP (Lettuce) |
|-----|-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| O₂ | 21 % | 2–3 % | 3–5 % | 1–3 % |
| CO₂ | 0.03 % | 3–5 % | 3 % | 5–10 % |
| N₂ | 78 % | Balance | Balance | Balance |

**4. Benefits (1 mark):**

- Shelf life **extended 2–4×** vs normal refrigeration.
- Reduced respiration rate by **40–60 %**.
- Delayed softening, yellowing, and microbial decay.
- Retention of **vitamins (Vitamin C), colour, and texture**.

**Examples:** Apples (CA at 0 °C, 1 % O₂, 1 % CO₂ — stored 9–12 months); Lettuce (MAP, shelf life 14 → 21 days).

---

#### Part (b): Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Leafy Vegetables | Root & Tuber Vegetables | Tropical Fruit Vegetables (Solanaceous/Cucurbits) |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| Examples | Lettuce, Spinach, Cabbage | Carrot, Beet, Potato (cured) | Tomato, Brinjal, Cucumber, Capsicum, Pumpkin |
| Optimum T (°C) | 0 | 0–4 | 7–13 |
| RH (%) | 95–100 | 95–98 | 85–95 |
| Freezing Point (°C) | -0.3 to -0.9 | -0.6 to -1.4 | -0.5 to -0.7 |
| Shelf Life | 1–3 weeks | 2–9 months | 1–4 weeks |
| Chilling Injury Risk | **Low** (non-sensitive) | **Low** (non-sensitive) | **HIGH** — sensitive below 10–13 °C |
| Respiration Rate | **High** (10–25 mL/kg·h) | **Low–Medium** (3–6 mL/kg·h) | **Medium** (8–15 mL/kg·h) |
| Recommended Storage | Hydro-cooled + 0 °C, high RH | Cured, dry-stored or cold-stored | Just-above-CI-temperature with CA |
| Ethylene Behaviour | Highly **sensitive** | **Low** sensitivity | **Produce** ethylene; mixed tolerance |

**Key Engineering Inference:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leafy and root vegetables** can be safely stored at **0 °C** with high RH, achieving shelf lives of weeks to months. **Tropical fruit vegetables** must be stored at **7–13 °C** to avoid chilling injury, sacrificing some shelf-life extension but preventing irreversible tissue damage. Refrigeration design must therefore incorporate **dual-temperature zones** or **product-specific cold rooms** in commercial vegetable storage facilities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in vegetable refrigeration problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the respiration heat load** — Many students compute only sensible heat, missing the metabolic contribution. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Unit conversion error** — Confusing mL and L when computing respiration heat. Always convert mL → L **before** multiplying by 10.5 kJ/L.
> 3. **Confusing freezing point depression with chilled storage** — Vegetables stored at 0 °C are **NOT** frozen. Latent heat $L_f$ applies only when crossing the freezing point.
> 4. **Ignoring chilling injury** for tropical vegetables — Storing tomato or brinjal at 0 °C is **incorrect design**, even if refrigeration capacity exists.
> 5. **Not using RQ = 1.0** approximation when only CO₂ data is given — RQ = CO₂ produced / O₂ consumed ≈ 1.0 for aerobic respiration of carbohydrates.
> 6. **Time conversion errors** — 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,660 kJ/h. Verify units before final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — Vegetables (Module 4):**
>
> - Vegetables are **living, respiring** commodities post-harvest — refrigeration **slows but does not stop** metabolism.
> - **Respiration equation:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \to 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Heat}$.
> - **Respiration heat formula:** $Q_r = 10.5 \times V_{O_2}$ (in L, energy in kJ).
> - **Q₁₀ rule:** Every 10 °C rise **doubles or triples** respiration rate ($Q_{10} = 2$ to $3$).
> - **Transpiration** causes wilting; control via **RH 95–98 %** in cold rooms.
> - **Chilling injury** affects tropical vegetables stored at **7–13 °C threshold** — symptoms: pitting, browning, off-flavour.
> - **Freezing injury** occurs **below** the initial freezing point ($-0.5$ to $-2.5\,°C$); intracellular ice ruptures cells.
> - **Optimum storage conditions** depend on vegetable type (see classification table).
> - **CA storage** uses $O_2 = 2$–$5\%$, $CO_2 = 3$–$10\%$; extends shelf life by 2–4×.
> - **MAP** is the retail-scale version of CA using polymeric films.
> - **Ethylene** is a senescence hormone — sensitive vegetables (cucumber, brinjal) must be kept away from producers (tomato, banana).
> - **Specific heat** of fresh vegetables $\approx 3.5$–$4.0\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.
> - **Latent heat of freezing** $\approx 250$–$290\,\text{kJ/kg}$.
> - **Total refrigeration load** = $Q_s + Q_r + Q_{trans} + Q_{equip} + Q_{infil}$.
> - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,660 kJ/h** — critical unit conversion for KTU exams.
> - **Pre-cooling methods:** Vacuum cooling (leafy), Hydro cooling (roots), Forced air (dense), Room cooling (general).
> - Always identify **chilling sensitivity** *before* selecting storage temperature in design problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fish and Poultry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fish and Poultry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fish as a Refrigeration Commodity

**Fish** is a highly perishable biological commodity with a water content ranging from **65 % to 80 %** and a significant fraction of **unsaturated lipids**. The intrinsic enzymatic activity (autolysis), microbial load (psychrotrophs), and oxidative degradation of unsaturated fatty acids make fish one of the most demanding materials in the cold chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Fish* is a cold-blooded aquatic protein commodity whose shelf life is governed by the rate of three concurrent deteriorative reactions: (1) enzymatic autolysis, (2) bacterial spoilage, and (3) oxidative rancidity. Refrigeration is employed to suppress all three by reducing the temperature to as close to its **freezing point (-0.6 °C to -2.2 °C)** as possible.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge on Ice":**
Imagine a kitchen sponge soaked in salt water (fish muscle). At room temperature, bacteria multiply on its surface in minutes, the trapped water begins to evaporate and oxidize, and the sponge "rots." Now place that exact same sponge inside a freezer. The water becomes immobilized as ice crystals, bacteria fall into a dormant state, and chemical reactions slow to a crawl. **This is precisely what refrigeration does to fish — it does not kill spoilage, it merely "presses the pause button" on it.**

## 1.2 Poultry as a Refrigeration Commodity

**Poultry** refers to domesticated birds (chicken, turkey, duck, quail) raised for meat. Its muscle composition is approximately **70–75 % water**, **20–22 % protein**, and **3–5 % lipid**, with a freezing point near **-2.7 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Poultry* is a warm-blooded protein commodity with a higher initial microbial load (skin and gut flora) and a longer post-mortem pH decline (rigor mortis). Pre-chilling immediately after slaughter is a non-negotiable step to retard *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter*, and *Pseudomonas* growth.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Body Without a Coat":**
After slaughter, a chicken's body loses its natural defense (circulation and immune system) but still carries its own "internal furnace" — residual body heat. Without external cooling, this heat accelerates spoilage dramatically. Chilling the carcass is like immediately putting a feverish patient under a cold compress; the goal is to drop the deep-muscle temperature from ~**40 °C** to **≤ 4 °C** within hours.

## 1.3 The Cold-Chain Philosophy for Fish & Poultry

| Parameter | Fish | Poultry |
|---|---|---|
| Initial microbial load | High (skin, gills, gut) | High (skin, gut) |
| Freezing point | **-0.6 °C to -2.2 °C** | **-2.5 °C to -2.8 °C** |
| Recommended chilled storage | **0 °C ± 1 °C** | **0 °C to 4 °C** |
| Recommended frozen storage | **-18 °C to -25 °C** | **-18 °C** |
| Maximum chilled shelf life | 7–14 days (iced) | 5–7 days |
| Maximum frozen shelf life | 6–12 months | 9–12 months |
| Dominant spoilage | Autolysis + oxidation | Bacterial (psychrotrophs) |

> [!NOTE]
> Both commodities require the **"3-T Rule"** of the cold chain: **T**emperature, **T**ime, and **T**olerance. A single break in any T is equivalent to breaking the entire chain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition and Spoilage Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Composition Breakdown

**Fish muscle composition (typical):**
- **Water:** 65–80 %
- **Protein:** 16–22 % (myofibrillar — actin & myosin; sarcoplasmic)
- **Lipid:** 0.5–25 % (highly variable: cod ~0.5 %, mackerel ~16 %)
- **Ash/Minerals:** 1–2 %
- **Carbohydrate:** < 0.5 % (mostly glycogen, depleted at death)

**Poultry muscle composition (chicken breast, typical):**
- **Water:** ~74 %
- **Protein:** ~23 %
- **Lipid:** ~1.5 % (skin-on up to 15 %)
- **Ash:** ~1 %

### 2.1.2 Spoilage Cascade in Fish (Three Concurrent Phases)

1. **Rigor Mortis Phase (0–24 h post-mortem):** ATP depletion causes muscle stiffening. pH drops from ~7.0 to ~6.2–6.5.
2. **Autolytic Phase:** Endogenous enzymes (cathepsins, calpains) break down proteins → softening, off-odor precursors.
3. **Bacterial Phase (dominant in spoilage):** *Pseudomonas*, *Shewanella putrefaciens*, *Photobacterium* convert TMA → TMAO, producing H₂S, putrescine, cadaverine.
4. **Lipid Oxidation:** Especially critical in fatty fish — peroxides → aldehydes → rancidity.

### 2.1.3 Spoilage in Poultry

- **Skin contamination** at slaughter is the primary inoculum.
- Rigor mortis is faster in poultry than red meat.
- Psychrotrophic *Pseudomonas* and *Acinetobacter* dominate chilled storage.
- Lipid oxidation in dark meat (leg) is more rapid than in white meat (breast).

## 2.2 Pre-Cooling and Chilling Technologies

### 2.2.1 For Fish
- **Icing (wet icing):** Crushed ice (0 °C) in ratio 1:1 fish to ice; most common traditional method.
- **Refrigerated sea water (RSW):** Fish immersed in chilled brine (-1 °C); faster cooling.
- **Chilled sea water (CSW):** Pre-chilled seawater at ~0 °C; large-scale pelagic fisheries.
- **Slurry ice (ice slurry):** Pumpable ice-water mixture; rapid, uniform cooling.

### 2.2.2 For Poultry
- **Immersion chilling (spin chiller):** Carcasses immersed in chilled water (0–4 °C) with counter-flow design; industry standard.
- **Air chilling:** Cold air blast in a tunnel; slower but reduces water uptake and cross-contamination.
- **Cryogenic chilling:** Liquid N₂ or CO₂ spray; rapid surface chilling for premium cuts.

## 2.3 Freezing Methods for Fish and Poultry

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Air blast freezing** | Forced convection cold air (-30 °C to -40 °C) | Most common industrial method |
| **Plate freezing (contact)** | Fish/poultry pressed between refrigerated plates | Blocks of fish, packaged fillets |
| **Immersion freezing** | Direct immersion in cold brine or glycol (-18 °C) | Whole tuna, sardines |
| **Spray freezing** | Brine spray on product | Crustacean, shrimp |
| **Cryogenic freezing** | Liquid N₂ (-196 °C) or CO₂ (-78 °C) | High-value IQF items (shrimp, fillet portions) |
| **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing)** | Fluidized bed of cold air; prevents clumping | Berries analogy: each piece frozen individually |

## 2.4 Cold Storage Design Parameters

**Refrigeration Load (Q_total):**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{product} + Q_{package} + Q_{walls} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{equipment} + Q_{respiration}$$

Where:
- **Q_product** = Sensible + Latent heat removed = $m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f$
- **Q_package** = $m_{pack} \cdot c_{p,pack} \cdot \Delta T$
- **Q_walls** = $U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{wall}$ (conduction through insulated envelope)
- **Q_infiltration** = Heat from door openings & air leakage
- **Q_equipment** = Lights, fans, defrost heaters
- **Q_respiration** = ≈ 0 for fish/poultry (negligible compared to fresh produce)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat removal (above freezing) |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat of freezing |
| 3 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{\theta}\left[\dfrac{P_a' \cdot a}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot b}{h}\right]$ | **Plank's equation** for freezing time |
| 4 | $\theta = T_f - T_c$ | Mean temperature difference (freezing point − coolant) |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{T_f - T_c}$ | Plank's R (surface parameter) |
| 6 | $P_a' = 1/2$ (slab), $1/4$ (cyl), $1/6$ (sphere) | Plank's geometry constant |
| 7 | $a, b$ | Shape factors for Plank's equation |
| 8 | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Sensible Heat Ratio (psychrometric) |
| 9 | $q_m = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{h_{fg,ref} \cdot \eta}$ | Mass flow rate of refrigerant |
| 10 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (system efficiency) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plank's Equation Geometry Constants (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):**
> - **Infinite slab:** $a = 1/8, \; b = 1/8, \; P_a' = 1/2, \; R = 1/4$
> - **Infinite cylinder:** $a = 1/8, \; b = 1/4, \; P_a' = 1/4, \; R = 1/4$
> - **Sphere:** $a = 1/6, \; b = 1/6, \; P_a' = 1/6, \; R = 1/6$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refrigerated trucks and reefer ships** transporting tuna at **-25 °C** rely on Plank's equation to pre-size the freezing tunnels.
- **Spin chillers** in poultry plants are sized by the sensible heat load equation over a target **90-minute dwell time**.
- **IQF fluidized bed freezers** for shrimp use psychrometric SHR analysis to size the air-side heat exchanger.
- **Cold storage RH** must be **85–95 %** for fish (to prevent dehydration/freezer burn) and **85–90 %** for poultry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Freezer Burn** in fish is essentially sublimation of surface ice due to low ambient RH. Mitigated by **glaze coating** (water film) or **vacuum packaging**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Plank's Equation for Freezing Time

Plank's equation is a semi-empirical model derived from **Neumann's solution of the Stefan problem** (moving boundary heat conduction). Plank made the following assumptions:

1. The frozen layer is at uniform temperature equal to the coolant-side surface temperature.
2. Initial product temperature is uniform and above the freezing point.
3. The thermal conductivity, density, and latent heat of the frozen layer are constant.
4. Sensible heat above and below the freezing point is neglected (lumped to a single effective latent heat).

### 3.1.1 Plank's Equation (Final Form)

For a body of characteristic dimension $a$ (half-thickness for slab, radius for cylinder/sphere), the freezing time $t_f$ is given by:

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{(T_f - T_c)}\left[\frac{P_a' \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k}\right]$$

Where:
- $t_f$ = freezing time (s)
- $\rho$ = density of the frozen product (kg/m³)
- $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $T_f$ = initial freezing temperature of the product (°C)
- $T_c$ = temperature of the cooling medium (°C)
- $h$ = surface heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of frozen layer (W/m·K)
- $a$ = characteristic dimension (m)
- $P_a' , R$ = Plank's dimensionless constants (geometry-dependent)

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example — Freezing a Fish Slab

**Given:**
- Fish slab thickness = $2a = 4$ cm → $a = 0.02$ m
- Density $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- Latent heat $L_f = 2.35 \times 10^5$ J/kg
- Initial freezing point $T_f = -1.5$ °C
- Coolant (air) temperature $T_c = -30$ °C
- Surface heat transfer coefficient $h = 25$ W/m²·K
- Thermal conductivity of frozen fish $k = 1.4$ W/m·K
- Geometry: Infinite slab → $P_a' = 1/2, R = 1/4$

**Step 1: Compute the mean temperature driving force.**

$$\theta = T_f - T_c = (-1.5) - (-30) = 28.5 \; °C$$

**Step 2: Substitute Plank's constants for infinite slab.**

$$P_a' = \frac{1}{2} = 0.500, \quad R = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250$$

**Step 3: Compute the surface-resistance term.**

$$\text{Term}_1 = \frac{P_a' \cdot a}{h} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.02}{25} = \frac{0.010}{25} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2 \cdot K / W$$

**Step 4: Compute the internal-conduction term.**

$$\text{Term}_2 = \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k} = \frac{0.25 \times (0.02)^2}{1.4} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.0004}{1.4} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.4} = 7.14 \times 10^{-5} \; m^2 \cdot K / W$$

**Step 5: Sum the resistance terms.**

$$\text{Term}_{total} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} + 7.14 \times 10^{-5} = 4.714 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2 \cdot K / W$$

**Step 6: Compute the latent heat term.**

$$\frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} = \frac{1050 \times 2.35 \times 10^5}{28.5} = \frac{2.4675 \times 10^8}{28.5} = 8.658 \times 10^6 \; J/(m^3 \cdot K)$$

**Step 7: Multiply to obtain freezing time.**

$$t_f = 8.658 \times 10^6 \times 4.714 \times 10^{-4} = 4080 \; s$$

**Step 8: Convert to hours.**

$$t_f = \frac{4080}{3600} = 1.13 \; h \approx 68 \; \text{minutes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of physical sense:** The freezing time scales with the **square of thickness** (conduction-dominated) and inversely with $h$ and $\Delta T$. This is why doubling slab thickness quadruples freezing time — a key KTU exam insight.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Refrigeration Load for a Fish Cold Storage

**Given:**
- Storage capacity: 10,000 kg of fish
- Inlet temperature: 5 °C; storage temperature: -18 °C
- Specific heat above freezing: $c_p = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Latent heat: $L_f = 235$ kJ/kg
- Specific heat below freezing: $c_{pf} = 1.8$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Freezing point: -1.5 °C
- Packaging: 200 kg cardboard, $c_{p,pack} = 1.7$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Walls: $U = 0.25$ W/m²·K, $A = 400$ m², $T_{amb} = 30$ °C
- Equipment heat: 2 kW (fans + lights)
- Operating time: 24 h
- Plant operation: 18 h/day

**Step 1: Sensible heat — cooling from 5 °C to freezing point -1.5 °C.**

$$Q_{s1} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 10000 \times 3.6 \times (5 - (-1.5)) = 10000 \times 3.6 \times 6.5 = 2.34 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 2: Latent heat of freezing.**

$$Q_l = m \cdot L_f = 10000 \times 235 = 2.35 \times 10^6 \; kJ$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat — cooling frozen fish from -1.5 °C to -18 °C.**

$$Q_{s2} = m \cdot c_{pf} \cdot \Delta T = 10000 \times 1.8 \times (-1.5 - (-18)) = 10000 \times 1.8 \times 16.5 = 2.97 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 4: Packaging sensible heat.**

$$Q_{pack} = m_{pack} \cdot c_{p,pack} \cdot \Delta T = 200 \times 1.7 \times 23.5 = 7990 \; kJ$$

**Step 5: Wall (transmission) heat gain.**

$$Q_{walls} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{wall} = 0.25 \times 400 \times (30 - (-18)) = 0.25 \times 400 \times 48 = 4800 \; W = 4.8 \; kW$$

For 18 h operation:

$$Q_{walls,total} = 4.8 \times 18 \times 3600 / 1000 = 311.04 \; MJ = 3.11 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 6: Equipment heat.**

$$Q_{eq} = 2 \times 18 \times 3600 / 1000 = 1.296 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 7: Total refrigeration load (for one cool-down cycle).**

$$Q_{total} = (2.34 + 23.5 + 2.97) \times 10^5 + 7.99 \times 10^3 + 3.11 \times 10^5 + 1.296 \times 10^5$$

$$Q_{total} = 28.81 \times 10^5 + 0.08 \times 10^5 + 3.11 \times 10^5 + 1.296 \times 10^5 = 3.33 \times 10^6 \; kJ$$

**Step 8: Convert to average refrigeration capacity (kW).**

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{Q_{total}}{t} = \frac{3.33 \times 10^6}{18 \times 3600} = \frac{3.33 \times 10^6}{64800} \approx 51.4 \; kW$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Freezing Geometry Constants

| Geometry | $P_a'$ | $R$ | $a$ | $b$ | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Infinite slab | 1/2 | 1/4 | 1/8 | 1/8 | Fish fillet, packaged meat block |
| Infinite cylinder | 1/4 | 1/4 | 1/8 | 1/4 | Sausage, cylindrical meat |
| Sphere | 1/6 | 1/6 | 1/6 | 1/6 | Meatballs, peas (analogy) |
| Short cylinder (length ≈ 3·d) | 1/4 | 1/4 | 1/8 | 1/4 | Whole small fish, drumstick |

> [!NOTE]
> For a **whole chicken** (short, squat cylinder), use the short cylinder constants. For a **fish fillet** (flat, wide), use the infinite slab assumption when length & width >> thickness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cold-Chain Flow for Fish — Catch to Consumer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvest at Sea] --> B[Onboard Pre-Cooling RSW or Ice Slurry]
    B --> C[Sorting and Grading]
    C --> D{Cold Chain Mode}
    D -->|Fresh| E[Chilled Storage 0 deg C]
    D -->|Frozen| F[Plate or Air Blast Freezer -30 deg C]
    F --> G[Glazing and Packaging]
    G --> H[Frozen Storage -18 to -25 deg C]
    E --> I[Reefer Truck or Air Freight]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Wholesale Cold Store]
    J --> K[Retail Display Chiller or Freezer]
    K --> L[Consumer Refrigerator]
    H2[Humidity Control 85-95 percent RH] -.-> E
    H2 -.-> H
```

## 4.2 Poultry Processing & Refrigeration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Live Bird Reception] --> B[Stunning and Slaughter]
    B --> C[Bleeding 2 to 3 min]
    C --> D[Scalding 50 to 55 deg C]
    D --> E[Defeathering]
    E --> F[Evisceration]
    F --> G[Washing and Chill Rinse]
    G --> H{Chilling Method}
    H -->|Spin Chiller| I[Immersion Chill 0 to 4 deg C 90 min]
    H -->|Air Chiller| J[Air Blast Tunnel 0 to 2 deg C 2 to 4 h]
    I --> K[Drainage and Grading]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Packaging Type}
    L -->|Fresh| M[Chilled Storage 0 to 4 deg C]
    L -->|Frozen| N[Air Blast or Cryogenic Freezer -35 deg C]
    N --> O[Frozen Storage -18 deg C]
    M --> P[Distribution Reefer 0 to 4 deg C]
    O --> Q[Distribution Reefer -18 deg C]
    P --> R[Retail Display]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.3 Freezing Process — Heat Transfer & Phase Change Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Frozen_Zone
        F1[Surface Temp Tc] --> F2[Temperature Gradient across Frozen Layer]
        F2 --> F3[Moving Freezing Front at Tf]
    end
    subgraph Unfrozen_Zone
        U1[Initial Temp Ti] --> U2[Gradual Cooling above Freezing]
        U2 --> F3
    end
    F1 -. Convective Resistance 1/h .-> AMB[Coolant at Tamb]
    AMB --> COMP[Refrigeration System with COP]
    COMP -. Energy W .-> AMB
    F3 -->|Latent Heat Lf released| F1
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Cold Storage for Fish

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inlet_Area
        I1[Fish Receiving Dock] --> I2[Pre-Cooling Spiral or Slurry]
    end
    I2 --> ST1[Short Term Buffer 0 deg C]

    ST2 --> FP1[Frozen Pack Line]
    FP1 --> FS1[Frozen Storage Room 1 Minus 18 deg C]
    FP1 --> FS2[Frozen Storage Room 2 Minus 25 deg C for Tuna]
    subgraph Chilled_Area
        CH1[Chilled Pack Line] --> CH2[Chilled Storage 0 deg C 90 to 95 percent RH]
    end
    ST2 --> CH1
    FS1 --> DI1[Dispatch Dock Reefer]
    CH2 --> DI1
    FS2 --> DI1
    DI1 --> OUT[Distribution]
    subgraph Utilities
        UT1[Compressor Room] --> UT2[Evaporator Coils]
        UT2 --> UT3[Defrost System]
    end
    UT2 -. Supplies Cold .-> FS1
    UT2 -. Supplies Cold .-> CH2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define the freezing point of fish and explain why it varies among species.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The freezing point of fish is the temperature at which the intracellular and extracellular water of fish muscle begins to crystallize into ice. For most marine fish, the freezing point lies in the range **-0.6 °C to -2.2 °C**, while freshwater fish have a slightly higher freezing point (~-0.5 °C to -1.0 °C). The variation among species is due to differences in **salt (ash) content** — salt depresses the freezing point colligatively. Fatty fish (e.g., mackerel) and lean fish (e.g., cod) also show slight differences due to lipid and bound-water fractions. (3 marks — split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark range, 1 mark cause of variation)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**List any three methods of pre-cooling poultry with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Immersion (spin) chilling:** Carcasses are passed counter-current through chilled water at **0–4 °C** for 60–90 min, providing rapid and uniform cooling. (1 mark)
2. **Air blast chilling:** Carcasses are exposed to high-velocity cold air at **0–2 °C** in a tunnel for 2–4 hours, avoiding cross-contamination. (1 mark)
3. **Cryogenic chilling:** Liquid nitrogen or CO₂ is sprayed, achieving rapid surface freezing for premium or processed cuts. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Plank's equation** — Students frequently mix $a$ (half-thickness) and full thickness. Always re-check: for a slab of thickness $2a$, use $a$ in the formula.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert seconds to hours** when comparing freezing times.
> 3. **Ignoring sensible heat above/below the freezing point** in load calculations — examiners specifically test the **2-stage sensible + latent** approach.
> 4. **Wrong Plank's constants** — using sphere constants for a fish fillet is an instant 3-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not stating COP or compressor work** when asked to "size the refrigeration system" — examiners mark 2 marks for the energy-side completeness.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Module 4]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **composition, spoilage mechanism, and recommended refrigeration regime** for fish. State the chemical indicators used to assess fish freshness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fish block of dimensions **30 cm × 20 cm × 6 cm** is to be frozen from an initial temperature of **5 °C** to a final centre temperature of **-18 °C** in an air blast freezer at **-35 °C**. The density of frozen fish is **1050 kg/m³**, $L_f = 2.35 \times 10^5$ J/kg, $c_p$ (above freezing) = **3.6 kJ/(kg·K)**, $c_p$ (below freezing) = **1.8 kJ/(kg·K)**, $h = 30$ W/m²·K, $k = 1.4$ W/m·K, and freezing point $T_f = -1.5$ °C. Using Plank's equation, calculate the **freezing time**, treating the block as an **infinite slab of half-thickness 0.03 m**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Composition (2 marks):**
- Water 65–80 %, Protein 16–22 %, Lipid 0.5–25 %, Ash 1–2 %
- Water content is the critical parameter governing refrigeration load and freezing point.

**Spoilage Mechanism (3 marks):**
- **Autolysis:** Endogenous cathepsins and calpains degrade myofibrillar proteins → textural softening.
- **Bacterial spoilage:** *Pseudomonas*, *Shewanella putrefaciens*, *Photobacterium* convert trimethylamine oxide (TMA-O) → trimethylamine (TMA) producing putrid odor.
- **Oxidation:** Unsaturated lipids (PUFAs) react with O₂ → peroxides → aldehydes → rancidity (dominant in fatty fish).

**Refrigeration Regime (1 mark):**
- Chilled: 0 °C ± 1 °C with 90–95 % RH, shelf life 7–14 days.
- Frozen: -18 °C to -25 °C, shelf life 6–12 months.

**Chemical Freshness Indicators (1 mark):**
- TMA-N content (limit ~10–15 mg N/100 g)
- TVB-N (Total Volatile Basic Nitrogen) limit 30–35 mg/100 g
- Peroxide value (PV) for lipid oxidation
- K-value (ratio of inosine + hypoxanthine to ATP breakdown products) — < 20 % for very fresh, > 60 % for spoiled.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Identify geometry & constants.**

Infinite slab → $P_a' = 1/2 = 0.5$, $R = 1/4 = 0.25$, half-thickness $a = 0.03$ m.

**Step 2: Temperature difference.**

$$\theta = T_f - T_c = -1.5 - (-35) = 33.5 \; °C$$

**Step 3: Surface-resistance term.**

$$\text{Term}_1 = \frac{P_a' \cdot a}{h} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.03}{30} = \frac{0.015}{30} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Internal-conduction term.**

$$\text{Term}_2 = \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k} = \frac{0.25 \times (0.03)^2}{1.4} = \frac{0.25 \times 9 \times 10^{-4}}{1.4} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-4}}{1.4} = 1.607 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5: Sum resistance terms.**

$$\text{Term}_{total} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.607 \times 10^{-4} = 6.607 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2 \cdot K / W$$

**Step 6: Latent-heat term.**

$$\frac{\rho L_f}{\theta} = \frac{1050 \times 2.35 \times 10^5}{33.5} = \frac{2.4675 \times 10^8}{33.5} = 7.366 \times 10^6 \; J/(m^3 \cdot K)$$

**Step 7: Freezing time.**

$$t_f = 7.366 \times 10^6 \times 6.607 \times 10^{-4} = 4866 \; s$$

**Step 8: Convert to hours.**

$$t_f = \frac{4866}{3600} \approx 1.35 \; h \approx 81 \; \text{minutes}$$

**Step 9 (Bonus 1 mark):** Note that Plank's equation does NOT include the sensible heat above and below the freezing point. If required, an additional **~15–20 %** should be added to $t_f$ for accurate engineering design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Module 4 — Internal Choice]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **different methods of freezing fish and poultry** with their relative advantages and limitations. Compare air blast, plate, immersion, and cryogenic freezing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A poultry cold storage of capacity **15 tonnes** receives dressed chicken at **5 °C** and stores it at **-18 °C**. The freezing point of poultry is **-2.7 °C**, $L_f = 233$ kJ/kg, $c_p$ (above) = **3.2 kJ/(kg·K)**, $c_p$ (below) = **1.6 kJ/(kg·K)**. The insulated walls lose heat at the rate of **6 kW** continuously. The equipment heat (fans + lights) is **3 kW** for 16 hours of operation. Calculate the **total refrigeration load (kJ)** for one cool-down cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Air Blast Freezing (2 marks):**
- Forced convection with cold air at -30 °C to -40 °C, velocity 3–6 m/s.
- *Advantage:* Versatile, suitable for irregular shapes.
- *Limitation:* Slow, freezer burn risk if not packaged.

**Plate (Contact) Freezing (2 marks):**
- Product pressed between hollow metal plates with circulating refrigerant.
- *Advantage:* Excellent heat transfer, low dehydration.
- *Limitation:* Restricted to flat/packaged products (fish blocks, poultry cuts).

**Immersion Freezing (1.5 marks):**
- Direct immersion in cold brine or glycol at -15 °C to -18 °C.
- *Advantage:* Very fast heat transfer; used for tuna and shrimp.
- *Limitation:* Salt uptake (osmotic effect), corrosion of equipment.

**Cryogenic Freezing (1.5 marks):**
- Liquid N₂ (-196 °C) or CO₂ (-78 °C) spray.
- *Advantage:* Extremely rapid, premium product quality.
- *Limitation:* High cost, cracking in large items, requires insulated tunnel.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Sensible heat — 5 °C to -2.7 °C (above freezing).**

$$Q_{s1} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 15000 \times 3.2 \times (5 - (-2.7)) = 15000 \times 3.2 \times 7.7 = 3.696 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 2: Latent heat of freezing.**

$$Q_l = m \cdot L_f = 15000 \times 233 = 3.495 \times 10^6 \; kJ$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat — -2.7 °C to -18 °C (below freezing).**

$$Q_{s2} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 15000 \times 1.6 \times (-2.7 - (-18)) = 15000 \times 1.6 \times 15.3 = 3.672 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 4: Transmission heat (walls) over 16 hours.**

$$Q_{walls} = 6 \; kW \times 16 \times 3600 \; s = 6 \times 57600 = 3.456 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 5: Equipment heat over 16 hours.**

$$Q_{eq} = 3 \; kW \times 16 \times 3600 = 1.728 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

**Step 6: Total refrigeration load.**

$$Q_{total} = 3.696 \times 10^5 + 34.95 \times 10^5 + 3.672 \times 10^5 + 3.456 \times 10^5 + 1.728 \times 10^5$$

$$Q_{total} = (3.696 + 34.95 + 3.672 + 3.456 + 1.728) \times 10^5 = 47.50 \times 10^5 \; kJ$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 4.75 \times 10^6 \; kJ \approx 4.75 \; GJ}$$

**Step 7: Average refrigeration capacity.**

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{4.75 \times 10^6}{16 \times 3600} = \frac{4.75 \times 10^6}{57600} \approx 82.5 \; kW \; (\approx 23.5 \; TR)$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 4: Fish & Poultry**

- **Composition memory anchors:** Fish = high water (65–80 %), high unsat. lipid; Poultry = 70–75 % water, 20 % protein.
- **Freezing point memory anchor:** Fish ≈ -1.5 °C; Poultry ≈ -2.7 °C.
- **Recommended storage temps:** Chilled 0 °C; Frozen -18 °C (general), -25 °C (tuna/sashimi-grade).
- **Plank's equation is THE high-yield formula** — practice at least 3 numericals (slab, cylinder, sphere).
- **3 spoilage mechanisms of fish:** Autolysis, Bacterial, Oxidation — must know one named organism per mechanism.
- **3 spoilage organisms of poultry:** *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter*, *Pseudomonas*.
- **Chemical freshness indices:** TMA-N, TVB-N, K-value, Peroxide value (PV).
- **Immersion chilling vs Air chilling:** Water is faster; air gives lower water uptake and microbial cross-contamination.
- **IQF advantage:** Individual pieces do not clump; enables portion control.
- **Freezer burn = surface sublimation** — prevented by glazing, vacuum packaging, high RH (90–95 %).
- **Rigor mortis duration:** Fish rigor is fast and short; poultry rigor is rapid and brief; red meat rigor is slow and long.
- **Spin chiller design criterion:** 90 min, counter-current, water 0–4 °C.
- **Cold storage load components (must enumerate all 5):** Product, package, walls, infiltration, equipment.
- **Plank's geometry constants must be memorized** — slab $P_a' = 1/2, R = 1/4$ is the most-tested combination.
- **Real-world anchor:** Sashimi-grade tuna frozen at -60 °C (ultra-low) for parasite control — the *only* exception to the -25 °C rule.
- **COP expectation:** A well-designed poultry cold storage operates at **COP = 2.5–3.5** with R-404A or R-448A refrigerant.
- **Respiration heat** is **NOT** applicable to fish or poultry (animal tissues, not fresh produce).
- **Defrost cycle** is critical in poultry blast freezers due to high humidity ingress — typically 4 cycles/day, 30 min each.
- **Final exam mantra:** *If the question says "fish" — assume slab; if it says "poultry whole" — assume short cylinder; if it says "shrimp/peeled meat" — assume sphere.*

---

> **End of Module 4 — Fish and Poultry Notes | OEFTT612 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dairy Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: DAIRY PRODUCTS — REFRIGERATION PERSPECTIVE

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dairy Products** are defined by the *Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)* and the *Codex Alimentarius* as products obtained exclusively from the milk of healthy milch animals such as cow, buffalo, sheep, goat, or camel, including cream, butter, cheese, yogurt, ice cream, condensed/dried milk, and fermented products. From a **refrigeration engineering** perspective, dairy products are highly **perishable, water-rich biological systems** with active microbial flora and enzymatic activity, requiring a tightly controlled **cold chain** from farm-to-fork.

### 1.2 Why Refrigeration is Indispensable in Dairy

Fresh milk is a **near-perfect microbial growth medium**. It contains:
- **Water content:** ~ **87.5 %**
- **Lactose (carbohydrate):** ~ **4.8 %**
- **Milk fat:** ~ **3.5 – 5.5 %**
- **Proteins (casein & whey):** ~ **3.2 – 3.5 %**
- **Minerals (ash):** ~ **0.7 %**

These constituents, combined with a near-neutral **pH of 6.6 – 6.8**, allow psychrotrophic, mesophilic, and thermophilic bacteria to multiply rapidly. The **generation (doubling) time** of common milk microflora at ambient temperature (~30 °C) is only **20 – 30 minutes**, leading to **souring and spoilage within hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "4 °C Rule"** of the dairy industry: Every **1 °C rise above 4 °C** in bulk milk storage shortens shelf life by approximately **10 %**, and bacterial count may increase by up to **10–15 % per hour** at farm-side unrefrigerated conditions.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of milk as a "biological clock"** that is permanently running. The instant it leaves the udder, the clock starts ticking. Refrigeration is the **pause button**. The colder you keep the milk, the slower the clock ticks. At **4 °C** the clock ticks roughly **10× slower** than at **20 °C**, and at **-20 °C** it nearly stops, with only biochemical aging (e.g., lipolysis) slowly progressing. The dairy cold chain is therefore essentially a **time-management system** built on thermodynamic temperature control.

### 1.4 The Three-Layer Spoilage Model

| Layer | Phenomenon | Temperature Range | Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microbial** | Bacterial proliferation (Pseudomonas, *E. coli*, *Lactobacillus*) | 5 °C – 40 °C | Doubles in 20 min at 30 °C |
| **Enzymatic** | Lipase, protease activity causing rancidity & bitterness | 0 °C – 60 °C | Arrhenius-dependent |
| **Chemical/Oxidative** | Lipid oxidation, Maillard browning | 20 °C – 150 °C | Accelerates above 4 °C |

Refrigeration addresses primarily the **microbial** and **enzymatic** layers; the **oxidative** layer is countered by vacuum/light-protective packaging (often called *aseptic cold chain*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — REFRIGERATION IN DAIRY OPERATIONS

## 2.1 Classification of Dairy Products by Refrigeration Demand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Based on **thermodynamic state** during cold-chain delivery, dairy products fall into three practical engineering groups: **chilled (above freezing)**, **frozen (below freezing)**, and **ambient-stable (after-processing)**.

### Group A — Chilled Products (Storage 0 °C to 7 °C)
- Fluid milk, cream, yogurt, fresh cheese (paneer, mozzarella), butter (chilled grade)
- Goal: **extend shelf life 2 – 4 weeks** while retaining fresh organoleptic properties.

### Group B — Frozen Products (Storage ≤ -18 °C, typically -25 °C)
- Ice cream, frozen yogurt, frozen butter blocks, frozen curd, frozen cheese (mozzarella for pizza).
- Goal: **arrest all biological activity**, preserve 6 – 24 months.

### Group C — Ambient-Stable Products (After-Processing, often pre-refrigerated)
- UHT milk, condensed milk, milk powder, ghee.
- Refrigeration required only for **intermediate in-process steps**.

## 2.2 The Farm-to-Fork Cold Chain

The cold chain for dairy is a **continuous, time-temperature logged** sequence:

1. **Pre-cooling at the farm** — bulk milk cooler (direct expansion or ice-bank type). Milk enters udder at ~37 °C, must drop to **4 °C within 2 hours of milking** as per FSSAI/EU regulation.
2. **Insulated road tanker transport** — typically **3 – 5 °C** with temperature data loggers.
3. **Reception & plate heat exchanger (PHE) cooling** — pre-pasteurization cooling from ~5 °C to 2 °C.
4. **Pasteurization** — *HTST* (72 °C / 15 s) or *LTLT* (63 °C / 30 min) or *UHT* (135 – 150 °C / 4 – 15 s).
5. **Post-pasteurization cooling** — back down to **4 °C** via chilled water/regenerative section.
6. **Cold storage at dairy plant** — insulated rooms maintained at **2 – 4 °C, 85 – 90 % RH**.
7. **Distribution** — refrigerated trucks/reefers at **4 – 6 °C**.
8. **Retail display** — open or closed chilled cabinets at **2 – 7 °C**.
9. **Domestic refrigeration** — consumer refrigerator at **0 – 4 °C**.

## 2.3 Key Refrigeration Sub-Systems in a Dairy Plant

- **Ice-Bank Tank (IBT):** Builds ice on evaporator tubes during off-peak (night) hours; melts ice during peak milking hours. The **coefficient of performance (COP)** improves by up to **2.5 – 3.0** versus direct expansion.
- **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE):** The **workhorse** of dairy refrigeration — typically stainless-steel 316L gasketed plates; overall heat transfer coefficient **U = 3000 – 5500 W/m²·K** for milk-to-water.
- **Scraped-Surface Heat Exchanger (SSHE):** Used for **ice cream freezers** and high-viscosity products; continuously scrapes frozen layer off the barrel wall.
- **Blast Freezers:** Forced-air convection at **-30 °C to -40 °C** with air velocity **3 – 5 m/s**, used for butter blocks (25 kg) and bulk ice cream hardening.

## 2.4 Thermophysical Properties of Milk (Engineering Data)

| Property | Value (approx.) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Density (ρ) at 4 °C | 1030 | kg/m³ |
| Density at 20 °C | 1028 | kg/m³ |
| Specific heat (c_p), whole milk | 3.93 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat, skim milk | 4.06 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat, cream (30 % fat) | 3.78 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat, butter | 2.30 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat, ice cream mix | 3.30 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Thermal conductivity (k) | 0.55 | W/(m·K) |
| Freezing point (T_f) | -0.55 | °C |
| Latent heat of fusion (h_sf) | 232 | kJ/kg |
| Viscosity (μ) at 4 °C | 2.0 × 10⁻³ | Pa·s |

> [!NOTE]
> The **freezing point of milk** is depressed below 0 °C because dissolved lactose and salts cause **colligative depression**: ΔT_f = i·K_f·m. For typical milk composition, this is **-0.55 °C**. The latent heat released between **-1 °C and -5 °C** is **not released at one temperature**, but progressively — about **50 %** of water freezes between **-1 °C and -2.2 °C**, the rest over a much wider band. This makes **ice cream freezing a true transient freezing problem**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Dairy Refrigeration

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible cooling load | Q_s = m · c_p · (T_i - T_f) | kJ or kW | Pre-cooling of bulk milk |
| 2 | Latent heat in freezing | Q_L = m · h_sf | kJ or kW | During initial freezing |
| 3 | Total cooling load | Q_T = m [c_p (T_i - T_f) + h_sf + c_f (T_f - T_storage)] | kW | For batch freezer |
| 4 | Heat of respiration (cream, butter ripening negligible, hence N/A) | Q_R = m · q_R | W | Negligible for milk/ice cream |
| 5 | Heat conducted through walls | Q_wall = U · A · ΔT | W | Cold room walls |
| 6 | Infiltration load | Q_inf = V · ρ · c_p · ΔT · n | W | Door opening cycles |
| 7 | Heat from equipment/lighting | Q_eq = Σ P_i | W | Pumps, fans, lights |
| 8 | Product load in time t | m = Q_total / (h_total) | kg | From capacity data |
| 9 | Compressor power | W_comp = Q_e / COP | kW | Design of refrigeration system |
| 10 | Storage capacity | V_storage = m_24h / (ρ · φ) | m³ | Cold room sizing (φ = utilization factor, 0.6–0.8) |
| 11 | Plank-Evers formula (freezing time) | t = (ρ · h_sf / (T_f - T_a)) · (P · a · D / h) + (P · D² / 4k) · (R·D/h + 1/2) | h | For block freezing |
| 12 | Heat load due to people | Q_people = n · q_p · t | W | 250–300 W per person |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard refrigeration conditions for dairy (FSSAI & IDF guidelines):**
> - **Pasteurized fluid milk storage:** 0 °C to 4 °C
> - **Ice cream hardened storage:** -25 °C to -30 °C
> - **Butter (long-term frozen):** -18 °C to -20 °C
> - **Cheese ripening:** 10 °C to 13 °C, **RH 85 – 90 %**
> - **Cheese storage:** 0 °C to 4 °C, **RH 80 – 85 %**
> - **Yogurt:** 2 °C to 6 °C

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The dairy cold chain is the **largest single industrial consumer of refrigeration** globally — accounting for roughly **25 – 30 % of all food refrigeration energy**. In India, dairy ranks **#1** in cold-chain electricity demand among all food categories. The principles learned here directly scale to:
- Pasteurized and **aseptic packaged milk** design
- **Continuous ice cream freezers** (e.g., Tetra Pak, GEA, Gram Equipment)
- **Cold-room design** for cheese ripening and storage
- **Export-grade butter and ghee** cold storage
- **Pharma-grade whey** refrigeration (an off-shoot of dairy processing)

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Sensible Heat Load for Milk Cooling (Newton's Law of Cooling Approach)

We consider a **well-agitated jacketed vat** of milk of mass *m*, initial temperature *T_i*, with a coolant at temperature *T_c* (constant). The lumped capacitance approach yields the time-temperature relation.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on milk (control volume):**

$$
\frac{dQ_{milk}}{dt} = -m \cdot c_p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat transferred to coolant (Newton's Law of Cooling across jacket):**

$$
\frac{dQ}{dt} = U \cdot A \cdot (T - T_c)
$$

**Step 3 — Equating the two expressions:**

$$
U \cdot A \cdot (T - T_c) = -m \cdot c_p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging (separation of variables):**

$$
\frac{dT}{(T - T_c)} = -\frac{U \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Integration from T_i at t = 0 to T at time t:**

$$
\int_{T_i}^{T} \frac{dT}{(T - T_c)} = -\frac{U \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluating the integral:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{T - T_c}{T_i - T_c}\right) = -\frac{U \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} \, t
$$

**Step 7 — Final form (lumped cooling curve):**

$$
\boxed{T(t) = T_c + (T_i - T_c) \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{U \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} \, t\right)}
$$

**Step 8 — Total heat extracted in time t:**

$$
Q_{extracted} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T(t)) = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_c) \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{U \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} \, t\right)\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **time constant τ = m·c_p / (U·A)** governs the cooling. For milk cooling in a plate heat exchanger, **U·A is large**, so τ is small (seconds). For a farm bulk tank, τ is on the order of **45 – 90 minutes**.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Problem — A Typical KTU Board Question

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**
> A dairy plant receives **10 000 kg of raw milk per day** at **30 °C**. It is first cooled to **4 °C** in a plate heat exchanger using chilled water at **1 °C**, then pasteurized at **72 °C for 15 s (HTST)**, and finally re-cooled to **4 °C** before storage. Calculate:
> (a) Sensible cooling load for pre-cooling section.
> (b) Heat to be removed in the final cooling section.
> (c) Total refrigeration capacity required (assume regeneration recovers 90 % of pre-cooling heat).

### Given:
- Mass m = 10 000 kg/day
- c_p (whole milk) = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K)
- T_in = 30 °C, T_cool = 4 °C, T_pasteurize = 72 °C, T_coldwater = 1 °C
- HTST hold: 15 s

### (a) Pre-cooling sensible load (30 °C → 4 °C)

$$
Q_{pre} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f) = 10000 \times 3.93 \times (30 - 4)
$$

$$
Q_{pre} = 10000 \times 3.93 \times 26 = 1{,}021{,}800 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

Convert to kW (per day operation, but assume plant runs 16 h/day for refrigeration):

$$
Q_{pre} = \frac{1{,}021{,}800}{16 \times 3600} = 17.75 \text{ kW}
$$

### (b) Final cooling after pasteurization (72 °C → 4 °C)

$$
Q_{final} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (72 - 4) = 10000 \times 3.93 \times 68
$$

$$
Q_{final} = 2{,}672{,}400 \text{ kJ/day} = \frac{2{,}672{,}400}{16 \times 3600} = 46.39 \text{ kW}
$$

### (c) Net refrigeration load (assuming 90 % regeneration)

In a regenerative PHE, the **hot incoming milk** (30 °C) pre-warms the **hot outgoing pasteurized milk** (72 °C → ~35 °C) — saving ~90 % of the final cooling load.

Heat saved by regeneration:

$$
Q_{regen} = 0.90 \times 46.39 = 41.75 \text{ kW}
$$

Net refrigeration load:

$$
Q_{net} = Q_{pre} + Q_{final} - Q_{regen} = 17.75 + 46.39 - 41.75 = 22.39 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** Regenerative heat exchange reduces the net refrigeration load by **~65 %** in HTST pasteurization. This is why every KTU board answer must include the **regeneration** step — it carries **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cooling Strategies for Major Dairy Products

| Product | Initial Temp (°C) | Final Storage Temp (°C) | c_p [kJ/(kg·K)] | Heat Removal Step | Equipment Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw Milk (Farm) | 35 | 4 | 3.93 | Sensible only | Bulk milk cooler / IBT |
| Pasteurized Milk | 72 | 4 | 3.93 | Sensible | PHE + regenerative section |
| Cream (40 % fat) | 4 | 2 | 3.78 | Sensible | Jacketed vat |
| Butter (chilled) | 12 | 4 | 2.30 | Sensible | Cold room |
| Butter (frozen) | 12 | -20 | 2.30 | Sensible + Latent | Blast freezer |
| Ice Cream Mix | 5 | -25 | 3.30 | Sensible + Latent | SSHE continuous freezer + blast hardening |
| Yogurt (post-ferm) | 42 | 4 | 4.05 | Sensible | Cold room / tunnel |
| Cheese (curd) | 35 | 4 | 3.55 | Sensible | Cold brine immersion |
| Cheese (ripening) | 4 | 12 | 3.55 | Sensible (slow) | Ripening chamber 85% RH |
| Ghee (clarified) | 60 | 30 | 2.30 | Sensible | Open air (then ambient) |

## 3.4 Derivation — Plank-Evers Freezing Time for a Slab of Butter

For a slab of thickness 2D, initially at T_i, in a medium at T_a, with **h** as the surface heat transfer coefficient:

$$
t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot h_{sf}}{T_f - T_a} \left(\frac{P \cdot a \cdot D}{h}\right) + \frac{\rho \cdot P \cdot D^{2}}{4 k} \left(\frac{R \cdot D}{h} + \frac{1}{2}\right)
$$

where
- **a = 0.426** (geometric constant for infinite slab)
- **P = D · ρ · (h_sf) / (2·(T_i - T_f))** (Plank number)
- **R = 0.125** (shape constant for slab)

> This **Plank-Evers equation** is fundamental to KTU board problems on **frozen butter**, **frozen paneer blocks**, and **ice cream hardening** in moulds.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME — EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the thermophysical properties of whole milk relevant to refrigeration design.**

**Model Answer:**
- Specific heat c_p = **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**
- Density ρ = **1030 kg/m³** at 4 °C
- Freezing point T_f = **-0.55 °C** (depressed due to dissolved salts and lactose)
- Latent heat of fusion h_sf = **232 kJ/kg**
- Thermal conductivity k = **0.55 W/(m·K)**
- Viscosity at 4 °C = **2.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- Initial pH = **6.6 – 6.8**

These properties govern the **cooling time, refrigeration capacity**, and **compressor selection** in dairy plant design. *(3 marks: properties 2 + engineering relevance 1)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "4 °C rule" in dairy cold chain management? Why is it critical?**

**Model Answer:**
The **"4 °C rule"** dictates that fluid milk must be cooled to **4 °C or below** within **2 hours of milking** and maintained at that temperature through every stage of the cold chain. It is critical because:
1. It minimizes the **microbial doubling time** (extended from 20 min to > 6 h).
2. It preserves **sensory and nutritional quality** (prevents lipolysis and proteolysis).
3. It is the **legal benchmark** under FSSAI and Codex for pasteurized milk.
4. It extends shelf life by **3 – 4 weeks** versus unrefrigerated storage.
*(3 marks: rule 1 + reasons 2)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse specific heat of whole milk (3.93) with cream (3.78) or butter (2.30).** Choosing the wrong value leads to **3-mark deduction**.
> - **Always state the assumed operation hours** when converting kJ/day to kW. Default = **16 h/day** for plant refrigeration.
> - **Regeneration credit** must be calculated explicitly in HTST problems — examiners allot **2 marks** for it.
> - **Units in final answer** (kW, TR, or kJ/h) must be consistent. **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12660 kJ/h**.

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A dairy plant processes 15 000 kg/day of whole milk, received at 32 °C. The milk is cooled to 4 °C in a plate heat exchanger using chilled water at 1 °C, then HTST pasteurized at 72 °C, and finally re-cooled to 4 °C. A regenerative section recovers 85 % of the sensible heat from the hot milk to the cold incoming milk. Compute:**
**(a) The refrigeration load for pre-cooling and the final cooling sections. (7 marks)**
**(b) The net refrigeration capacity of the plant in TR and compressor kW (assume COP = 2.8). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Given:**
- m = 15 000 kg/day
- c_p (whole milk) = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K)
- T_in = 32 °C, T_cool = 4 °C, T_pasteurize = 72 °C
- Regeneration efficiency η_r = 0.85
- COP = 2.8
- Operating time = 16 h/day

### Part (a) — Sensible Heat Calculations

**Pre-cooling load (32 °C → 4 °C):**

$$
Q_{pre} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 15000 \times 3.93 \times (32 - 4)
$$

$$
Q_{pre} = 15000 \times 3.93 \times 28 = 1{,}650{,}600 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Convert to kW:**

$$
Q_{pre,kW} = \frac{1{,}650{,}600}{16 \times 3600} = 28.65 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final cooling load (72 °C → 4 °C):**

$$
Q_{final} = 15000 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 4) = 15000 \times 3.93 \times 68
$$

$$
Q_{final} = 4{,}008{,}600 \text{ kJ/day} = \frac{4{,}008{,}600}{57{,}600} = 69.59 \text{ kW}
$$

**Heat recovered by regeneration:**

$$
Q_{regen} = 0.85 \times 69.59 = 59.15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Net final cooling load:**

$$
Q_{final,net} = 69.59 - 59.15 = 10.44 \text{ kW}
$$

### Part (b) — Total Refrigeration Capacity

**Total net load:**

$$
Q_{net} = Q_{pre} + Q_{final,net} = 28.65 + 10.44 = 39.09 \text{ kW}
$$

**Convert to TR:**

$$
Q_{TR} = \frac{39.09}{3.517} = 11.11 \text{ TR}
$$

**Compressor power:**

$$
W_{comp} = \frac{Q_{net}}{\text{COP}} = \frac{39.09}{2.8} = 13.96 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** Q_net = **39.09 kW ≈ 11.11 TR**; Compressor power = **13.96 kW**

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A continuous scraped-surface ice cream freezer processes 2 000 kg/h of mix. The mix enters at 5 °C, leaves at -5 °C (semi-frozen with 50 % water frozen). Calculate:**
**(a) The total refrigeration load including both sensible and latent heat contributions. (7 marks)**
**(b) The refrigerant mass flow rate if R-717 (NH₃) is used, with evaporator temperature -30 °C and condenser temperature 25 °C. Take h_fg(NH₃) at -30 °C = 1167 kJ/kg. Assume isentropic compression with k = 1.31 for NH₃. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Given:**
- Mass flow rate ṁ = 2 000 kg/h = 0.5556 kg/s
- c_p (ice cream mix) = 3.30 kJ/(kg·K)
- T_in = 5 °C, T_out = -5 °C
- Fraction of water frozen = 0.50
- Water content of mix ≈ 65 % → mass of water frozen per kg = 0.65 × 0.5 = 0.325 kg
- h_fg (milk-water system) ≈ 232 kJ/kg (use water's latent heat with correction)

### Part (a) — Total Heat Removal

**Sensible heat (5 °C → -5 °C):**

$$
Q_{sensible} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f) = 0.5556 \times 3.30 \times (5 - (-5))
$$

$$
Q_{sensible} = 0.5556 \times 3.30 \times 10 = 18.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Latent heat (50 % water in mix frozen):**

Mass of water frozen per second:

$$
\dot{m}_{water,frozen} = 0.5556 \times 0.65 \times 0.50 = 0.1806 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
Q_{latent} = 0.1806 \times 232 = 41.90 \text{ kW}
$$

**Total refrigeration load:**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} = 18.33 + 41.90 = 60.23 \text{ kW}
$$

### Part (b) — Refrigerant Mass Flow

**Mass flow of NH₃:**

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = \frac{Q_{total}}{h_{fg,evap}} = \frac{60.23}{1167} = 0.0516 \text{ kg/s} = 185.76 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Compressor work (theoretical):**

Pressure at -30 °C (NH₃ saturation): P₁ ≈ 1.194 MPa
Pressure at 25 °C (NH₃ saturation): P₂ ≈ 1.515 MPa
Pressure ratio: r_p = 1.515 / 1.194 = **1.269**

For isentropic compression with k = 1.31:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot (r_p)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} = 243.15 \times (1.269)^{0.2366}
$$

$$
T_2 = 243.15 \times 1.0604 = 257.8 \text{ K} = -15.35 \text{ °C}
$$

**Compressor power (theoretical):**

$$
W_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{NH_3} \cdot k \cdot R_{specific} \cdot T_1}{k-1} \left[(r_p)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]
$$

For NH₃, R_specific = 488 J/(kg·K):

$$
W_{comp} = 0.0516 \times \frac{1.31 \times 488}{0.31} \times (1.0604 - 1) = 0.0516 \times 2063 \times 0.0604
$$

$$
W_{comp} = 6.43 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** Q_total = **60.23 kW (17.13 TR)**; ṁ(NH₃) = **185.76 kg/h**; W_comp = **6.43 kW**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Re-read 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Dairy products** are water-rich, perishable biological systems requiring an unbroken cold chain from farm to fork.
- The **"4 °C rule"** is the cornerstone of fluid milk storage; **ice cream requires -25 °C** hardened storage.
- **Whole milk c_p = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**; **cream (30 % fat) c_p = 3.78 kJ/(kg·K)**; **butter c_p = 2.30 kJ/(kg·K)**; **ice cream mix c_p = 3.30 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- **Freezing point of milk = -0.55 °C** (colligative depression by lactose and salts).
- **Latent heat of milk ≈ 232 kJ/kg** (lower than pure water's 333.5 kJ/kg due to dissolved solids).
- **HTST pasteurization** = 72 °C / 15 s; **LTLT** = 63 °C / 30 min; **UHT** = 135 – 150 °C / 4 – 15 s.
- **Regenerative PHE** recovers **80 – 95 %** of final cooling sensible heat in pasteurization, drastically reducing refrigeration load.
- **Sensible cooling load formula:** Q_s = m·c_p·ΔT (kJ or kW).
- **Latent load in freezing:** Q_L = m·h_sf.
- **Lumped cooling curve:** T(t) = T_c + (T_i - T_c)·exp(-(U·A / m·c_p)·t).
- **Plank-Evers equation** governs block-freezing time for butter, paneer, and ice cream.
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12 660 kJ/h** — memorize the conversion.
- **Compressor power = Q_e / COP** — typical dairy plant COP = 2.5 – 3.5.
- **Refrigerants used:** R-134a (small chillers), R-717/NH₃ (large industrial), R-404A (ice cream), CO₂ (R-744) emerging in transcritical systems.
- **Ice cream freezer** uses a **scraped-surface heat exchanger (SSHE)** with air injection for **overrun (50 – 100 %)**.
- **Cold room RH control:** 85 – 90 % for cheese ripening; 80 – 85 % for cheese storage; 90 – 95 % for unwrapped butter.
- **Insulation standard:** **PUF (polyurethane foam)** of **80 – 100 mm** for cold rooms, **k_insul ≈ 0.022 W/(m·K)**.
- **Heat gains from people** = 250 – 300 W per person (use only if workers enter the cold room; the *ktu board* does not usually include this for milk silos).
- **Don't forget** to convert kJ/day → kW using plant operation hours (typically 16 h/day, sometimes 20 h).
- **Always state** your assumptions explicitly — **FSSAI** storage temperatures, **Codex** standards, or **NMPPB** guidelines.
- **Cheese ripening** is the *only* dairy operation where **heating** is sometimes part of cold-chain design (controlled warm-up to 10 – 13 °C for enzymatic activity).
- **Ghee** does NOT require continuous refrigeration after production; **butter** does.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-mile advice for the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam):**
> - Always write down **Given Data** and **Assumptions** in a separate box at the top of any 14-mark answer.
> - Show the **unit conversion step** explicitly (kg/day → kg/s or kJ/day → kW).
> - End every answer with a **one-line engineering interpretation** — e.g., *"This 11.11 TR plant is suitable for a mid-size dairy handling 15 000 L/day of milk."*
> - Practise at least **3 numerical problems** on milk cooling and **1 on ice cream** — these are the **most repeated** KTU question themes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling and Distribution of Food through Refrigerated and Air-Conditioned Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT612 - REFRIGERATION & AIR CONDITIONING IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Handling and Distribution of Food through Refrigerated and Air-Conditioned Transport # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Refrigerated Transport (also called "Reefer Transport")** is a specialized mode of food logistics in which perishable food products are conveyed from the point of production/processing to the point of consumption under continuously controlled low-temperature conditions, thereby preserving quality, inhibiting microbial spoilage, and extending shelf life. The system integrates three engineering subsystems: **(a) a thermally insulated enclosure, (b) a mechanical vapour-compression refrigeration plant, and (c) a controlled air-circulation network** to maintain uniform product temperature throughout the cargo space. **Air-Conditioned Transport** refers to a milder form of temperature-controlled transport used for **short-duration, short-distance movement** of foods where only ambient-temperature stabilization (typically **18–22 °C**, ~**65–72 °F**) or chilling (**4–8 °C**) is required, rather than deep-freezing. It is commonly employed for bakery, confectionery, chocolate, and fresh-cut produce. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** A student must clearly distinguish between **Refrigerated Transport** (active cooling for perishable/frozen foods) and **Air-Conditioned Transport** (passive/active thermal conditioning for non-frozen sensitive goods). Marks are awarded for explicit differentiation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View) Imagine you are carrying a scoop of vanilla ice cream home on a hot Kerala summer afternoon. What happens? - If you wrap it in a cotton cloth (insulation) and walk fast (less heat gain) → it lasts longer but still melts. - If you put it in a **thermos flask with an active cooling gel pack** (insulated container + refrigeration effect) → it stays frozen for hours. - If you put it in a **mini-fridge on wheels with a small compressor running** (full refrigerated transport) → it stays perfectly frozen even for a 12-hour highway journey. That "mini-fridge on wheels" is exactly what a **Reefer Truck** is. The cotton cloth is just **insulation**, and the gel-pack is the **air-conditioned transport** idea. > [!NOTE] > **Key Realisation:** Cold Chain = Factory Cold Store → **Reefer Truck** → Distribution Hub → Retail Display Cabinet → Consumer Refrigerator. **Break any link** and the food spoils. This is the central philosophy of Module 4. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants | Parameter | Typical Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Truck body wall U-value (PUF insulated) | **0.30 – 0.40** | W/(m²·K) | | Truck body wall U-value (EPS insulated) | **0.45 – 0.55** | W/(m²·K) | | Specific heat of fresh meat | **3.0 – 3.5** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Specific heat of fresh fish | **3.5 – 3.9** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Specific heat of fruits/vegetables | **3.5 – 4.0** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Latent heat of freezing water (ice) | **335** | kJ/kg | | Respiration heat (apples at 0 °C) | **0.010 – 0.015** | W/kg | | Respiration heat (bananas at 13 °C) | **0.10 – 0.12** | W/kg | | Recommended truck interior RH | **85 – 95** | % | | Air velocity inside cargo space | **1.0 – 2.5** | m/s | | Standard "T" floor groove depth | **3 – 5** | mm | ## 1.4 Classification of Refrigerated Transport Modes 1. **Road (Highway) – Reefer Trucks / Reefer Vans:** Most flexible; door-to-door service. 2. **Rail – Refrigerated Wagons / Containers:** Bulk long-distance haulage. 3. **Sea – Reefer Ships & Reefer Containers (ISO 40-ft/20-ft):** International intercontinental trade. 4. **Air – Air-freighter with insulated LD-3 containers:** High-value perishables (saffron, caviar, vaccines, fresh tuna). # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Refrigerated Transport System The engineering of refrigerated transport rests on **five sequential functional layers**: 1. **Pre-Cooling Layer:** The vehicle body and the food product are pre-cooled to the target carriage temperature *before* loading. **Why?** Because if you load warm product into a cold truck, the refrigeration unit has to first extract the *field heat* of the product, which dramatically **increases pull-down time** and may **overload the compressor**, causing failure mid-transit. 2. **Insulation Layer:** The walls, floor, ceiling, and doors are constructed with **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** or **Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)** of thickness **50–100 mm** to minimise conductive heat ingress. **Why?** Because the truck body is essentially a **mobile cold store**, and any conductive heat gain must be minimised to reduce compressor work. 3. **Refrigeration Unit Layer:** A compact, engine-driven or diesel-driven, or fully electric **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** is mounted on the front wall of the truck. **Why?** Because VCRS is the most thermodynamically efficient way to remove **kW-scale heat loads** in a mobile package. 4. **Air Circulation Layer:** An **evaporator blower fan** forces cold air through the top of the cargo space; the air descends through the product load and returns along the **"T-bar floor"** (grooved aluminium floor channels) to the evaporator. **Why?** Because uniform air circulation prevents **hot pockets** and ensures every product experiences the same temperature. 5. **Monitoring & Control Layer:** A **microprocessor-based digital controller** with thermistor probes continuously logs temperature, defrost cycles, and door-opening events. Modern units transmit data via **GSM/GPS telemetry** to a central cold-chain dashboard. **Why?** Because **regulatory compliance** (FSSAI, HACCP, ATP Agreement) demands continuous temperature records. ## 2.2 Modes of Heat Ingress into a Refrigerated Vehicle The total refrigeration load ($Q_{total}$) on the truck refrigeration unit is the **sum of six contributing heat streams**: $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 $$ Where: - $Q_1$ → Heat conduction through walls, floor, ceiling, doors - $Q_2$ → Sensible + latent heat of the product (pull-down + respiration) - $Q_3$ → Heat from air infiltration during door openings - $Q_4$ → Heat from fan motors, evaporator defrost, lights - $Q_5$ → Heat from solar radiation (roof, sidewalls) - $Q_6$ → Heat from crew/personnel entering the cargo space ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Load Component | Formula | Units | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wall transmission | $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | All surfaces (walls, floor, roof, doors) | | 2 | Product sensible cooling | $Q_{2s} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Pulling temperature of pre-cooled food | | 3 | Product latent (freezing) | $Q_{2L} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | W | Phase change of water in food | | 4 | Respiration heat (fresh produce) | $Q_{2R} = m \cdot q_{resp}$ | W | Fruits & vegetables during transit | | 5 | Air infiltration load | $Q_3 = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T + \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | W | Door-openings + breathing losses | | 6 | Fan & equipment heat | $Q_4 = P_{fan} + P_{light} + P_{defrost}$ | W | Internal heat sources | | 7 | Solar radiation load | $Q_5 = A_{roof} \cdot I_{solar} \cdot \alpha$ | W | Roof & sidewalls in tropical climate | | 8 | Personnel heat | $Q_6 = n \cdot q_{person}$ | W | Crew inside during loading | | 9 | Heat of respiration temperature rise | $\dot{T} = \dfrac{Q_{resp}}{m \cdot c_p}$ | K/h | Used to estimate time-temperature abuse | | 10 | Air-changes per hour (door-open) | $\text{ACH} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_d}{V \cdot t}$ | 1/h | Door-opening frequency calculation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard engineering metric for insulated truck body heat gain:** For a typical 9-tonne payload reefer truck running at $\Delta T = 30$ K, the wall transmission load $Q_1$ alone lies in the range **800–1500 W**. Always size the refrigeration compressor with a **15–20 % safety margin**. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice - **Economic Angle:** Cold-chain failure accounts for **~30–40 %** of post-harvest losses in developing economies. Properly designed refrigerated transport can cut this loss dramatically. - **Regulatory Angle:** In the European Union, the **ATP (Agreement on the International Carriage of Perishable Foodstuffs)** strictly regulates the insulation and refrigeration performance of road vehicles. - **Sustainability Angle:** A well-insulated truck with proper door-seals reduces refrigerant charge leakage and **carbon footprint**, aligning with **F-Gas Regulations** and KTU's sustainability-mandated graduate attributes. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Heat-Load Calculation for a Reefer Truck ### Given Problem Statement A refrigerated truck is to transport **5,000 kg of fresh fish** from Kochi to Delhi. The following data is supplied: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Payload mass ($m$) | 5,000 kg | | Product entering temperature ($T_1$) | +5 °C | | Product required carriage temperature ($T_2$) | 0 °C | | Outside ambient temperature ($T_{amb}$) | +35 °C | | Truck interior dimensions | 5.0 m (L) × 2.2 m (W) × 2.0 m (H) | | Wall U-value (PUF 80 mm) | 0.40 W/(m²·K) | | Specific heat of fish ($c_p$) | 3.6 kJ/(kg·K) | | Door openings per trip | 4 | | Volume of air entering per door opening | 1.5 m³ | | Total trip duration | 24 h | | Fan motor power | 250 W | | Internal lighting power | 100 W (operating 1 h) | | Roof area exposed to sun | 11.0 m² | | Solar intensity on roof (Kerala noon) | 900 W/m² | | Roof absorptivity ($\alpha$) | 0.4 | | Latent heat of freezing water ($h_{fg}$) | 335 kJ/kg | | Fraction of water frozen in fish | 0 % (already above freezing) | ### Required Compute the **total steady-state heat load** on the truck refrigeration unit. --- ### Step 1 — Wall Transmission Load ($Q_1$) **Surface area calculation:** - Floor + Ceiling = $2 \times (5.0 \times 2.2) = 22.0 \text{ m}^2$ - Two side walls = $2 \times (5.0 \times 2.0) = 20.0 \text{ m}^2$ - Front + Rear walls = $2 \times (2.2 \times 2.0) = 8.8 \text{ m}^2$ - **Total $A = 50.8$ m²** $$ \Delta T = T_{amb} - T_{interior} = 35 - 0 = 35 \text{ K} $$ $$ Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.40 \times 50.8 \times 35 $$ $$ Q_1 = 711.2 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Heat flows from hot outside air → through insulated walls → into cold cargo. Lower U-value (better insulation) and lower $\Delta T$ both reduce this load. --- ### Step 2 — Product Sensible Cooling Load ($Q_{2s}$) The fish must be cooled from +5 °C to 0 °C (sensible cooling above freezing point — no latent heat since no phase change). $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{t} $$ $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{5000 \times 3.6 \times (5 - 0)}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{2s} = \dfrac{90000}{86400} $$ $$ Q_{2s} = 1.042 \text{ kW} = 1042 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Energy required = mass × specific heat × temperature drop. Dividing by trip time gives the *average* load — but in practice, this load is highest in the **first 4–6 hours** (pull-down phase), so compressor sizing is based on **peak-hour load**, not 24-h average. --- ### Step 3 — Respiration Heat Load ($Q_{2R}$) For fresh fish, respiration is negligible (fish are not living tissues). However, if the load were **fresh fruits** (e.g., mangoes, bananas), respiration heat would apply. For the fish load: $Q_{2R} = 0$ W. > **Logic:** Only **living plant tissues** (fruits, vegetables) respire. Animal tissues and frozen foods have zero respiration heat. --- ### Step 4 — Air Infiltration Load ($Q_3$) Air enters each time the door is opened. Total air exchange = $4 \times 1.5 = 6.0$ m³ per trip. **Sensible component:** $$ Q_{3s} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_{pa} \cdot \Delta T}{t} $$ Assuming $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$: $$ Q_{3s} = \dfrac{6.0 \times 1.2 \times 1005 \times 35}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{3s} = \dfrac{253260}{86400} $$ $$ Q_{3s} = 2.93 \text{ W} \approx 3 \text{ W} $$ **Latent component (moisture carried by warm air):** Warm air at 35 °C carries moisture; cold air at 0 °C holds less, so moisture **condenses inside** the truck. Using humidity ratio difference $\Delta W \approx 0.012$ kg/kg: $$ Q_{3L} = \dfrac{6.0 \times 1.2 \times 2501000 \times 0.012}{24 \times 3600} $$ $$ Q_{3L} = \dfrac{216086.4}{86400} $$ $$ Q_{3L} = 2.50 \text{ W} $$ $$ Q_3 = Q_{3s} + Q_{3L} \approx 5.4 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** In a single 24-hour long-haul trip, air infiltration is **negligible** because door openings are few. But in **last-mile distribution** with 50+ stops, infiltration can become the **dominant load**. --- ### Step 5 — Fan + Light + Defrost Heat ($Q_4$) Assuming defrost runs for 30 minutes total in 24 hours: $$ Q_{4,fan} = 250 \text{ W (continuous)} $$ $$ Q_{4,light} = \dfrac{100 \times 1}{24} = 4.17 \text{ W} $$ $$ Q_{4,defrost} = 500 \text{ W (heater)} \times \dfrac{0.5}{24} = 10.4 \text{ W} $$ $$ Q_4 = 250 + 4.17 + 10.4 = 264.6 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Fan motor heat is the **largest internal heat source** and is often underestimated by students in KTU exams. The fan's electrical input ultimately becomes heat inside the cargo space. --- ### Step 6 — Solar Radiation Load ($Q_5$) $$ Q_5 = A_{roof} \cdot I_{solar} \cdot \alpha = 11.0 \times 900 \times 0.4 $$ $$ Q_5 = 3960 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** This is a **massive** load. A white-painted roof with $\alpha = 0.4$ is assumed; a black tarpaulin cover could push this to **8000+ W**. This is why KTU examiners often include a solar load term. --- ### Step 7 — Personnel Heat ($Q_6$) Assuming **1 worker** spends 30 minutes inside during loading/unloading: $$ Q_6 = n \cdot q_{person} \cdot \dfrac{t_{occ}}{t_{trip}} = 1 \times 275 \times \dfrac{0.5}{24} $$ $$ Q_6 = 5.7 \text{ W} $$ > **Logic:** Negligible over a 24-h period, but important in **short-trip milk or bakery distribution**. --- ### Step 8 — Total Heat Load $$ Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_{2s} + Q_{2R} + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5 + Q_6 $$ $$ Q_{total} = 711.2 + 1042 + 0 + 5.4 + 264.6 + 3960 + 5.7 $$ $$ Q_{total} = 5988.9 \text{ W} \approx 6.0 \text{ kW} $$ **Applying 20% safety margin (KTU Examiner Standard):** $$ Q_{design} = 1.20 \times 5988.9 = 7186.7 \text{ W} \approx 7.2 \text{ kW} $$ > **Result Interpretation:** A refrigeration compressor of at least **7.5 kW cooling capacity** is required.
